RQ1 Can large vision-language models (VLMs) capture personal interpretations of interlocutors toward the same reference expression

tldr: In collaborative dialogue, shared perception does not guarantee shared interpretation. Mutual understanding must be established through interaction. We investigate whether vision-language models (VLMs…
abstract: In collaborative dialogue, shared perception does not guarantee shared interpretation. Mutual understanding must be established through interaction. We investigate whether vision-language models (VLMs) can distinguish what could be shared from what has been shared between dialogue participants through grounding. We formulate this as an interpretation-matching task on 13,077 annotated reference expressions from HCRC MapTask dialogues, and evaluate VLMs under systematically controlled manipulations of dialogue context and map-information access. Our results show that providing authentic map images improves overall performance but shifts models toward over-predicting alignment. Textual descriptions of the same map content reproduce this bias, while non-informative images suppress alignment predictions entirely, indicating that the bias is driven by task-relevant map content, not the visual channel. This improvement comes at the cost of degraded accuracy on non-aligned cases. Calibration analysis and reference-chain tracking further suggest that models rely on static referential cues on the maps rather than tracking how grounding unfolds through dialogue history. We observe these patterns most clearly in Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct and, to varying degrees, in four additional models from two architecture families. In models that exhibit the bias, map content, whether presented visually or textually, is treated as evidence of mutual understanding, conflating potential with established common ground.

title: GRPO, Dr. GRPO, and DAPO Are Three Operations on One Number: The Group-Standard-Deviation Identity
tldr: Three of the most popular methods for training language models to reason look like three different tricks. They are not. All three adjust a single number: standard deviation, reflecting how much a pro…
abstract: Three of the most popular methods for training language models to reason look like three different tricks. They are not. All three adjust a single number: standard deviation, reflecting how much a prompt's sampled answers disagree. When such a model is trained, it answers each problem many times, and an automatic checker marks every answer right or wrong. The standard deviation of those marks measures the disagreement: largest when the answers split evenly between right and wrong, and zero when they all agree. Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) divides by this number, GRPO Done Right (Dr. GRPO) drops the division, and Decoupled Clip and Dynamic Sampling Policy Optimization (DAPO) discards the groups where it is zero. Each is presented as its own fix, yet this paper proves they are three settings of one dial. That dial is not cosmetic: for right-or-wrong rewards, the disagreement is exactly the size of the training update, the group-standard-deviation identity. A split group teaches the most, while a unanimous group teaches nothing and falls silent. The same result says which problems deserve the most weight and how many tries each one needs. This paper confirms the intuition on a large real difficulty dataset (Big-Math) and in a controlled training run. What looks like a harmless normalization step is the dial that decides where learning happens and how strongly.

MedAgentBench: A Virtual EHR Environment to Benchmark Medical LLM Agents

tldr: As AI agents become increasingly capable of complex, long-horizon reasoning, rigorous and holistic evaluation is essential for measuring progress toward real-world healthcare applications. We introduc…
abstract: As AI agents become increasingly capable of complex, long-horizon reasoning, rigorous and holistic evaluation is essential for measuring progress toward real-world healthcare applications. We introduce HealthAgentBench, a suite of 54 agentic healthcare tasks across 7 categories each with its unique environment. The benchmark suite spans diverse workflows throughout the patient journey and a broad range of modalities. Each task is designed to replicate an end-to-end clinical workflow: given minimal instructions, an agent must explore raw healthcare data, operate within a complex environment, and execute multi-step solutions that go beyond naive prompting. A final task success rate is reported to provide a single, interpretable metric for HealthAgentBench overall performance for each agent. Evaluating frontier agents on HealthAgentBench, we find that overall task success rate remains low, underscoring the difficulty of the suite. The strongest and the most cost effective agent, Codex GPT-5.5, achieves only approximately 42% success rate. Beyond aggregate performance, HealthAgentBench reveals nuanced strengths and weaknesses across task categories. Frontier agents show promise in automatically developing research modeling pipelines over EHR data, but medical imaging remains especially challenging, particularly for Claude Code models, while Codex GPT-5.5 shows emerging capability. Tasks that combine large search spaces with compositional reasoning requirements remain difficult for all current agents. Together, these results suggest that HealthAgentBench provides a challenging and realistic benchmark with substantial room for future progress. We release our benchmark at https://github.com/microsoft/HealthAgentBench.

title: Perceive-to-Reason: Decoupling Perception and Reasoning for Fine-Grained Visual Reasoning
tldr: Fine-grained visual reasoning remains challenging for vision-language models, especially when small but critical visual cues are buried in high-resolution images. Existing approaches rely on repeated …
abstract: Fine-grained visual reasoning remains challenging for vision-language models, especially when small but critical visual cues are buried in high-resolution images. Existing approaches rely on repeated cropping or test-time visual search to introduce local evidence, but they typically do not explicitly distinguish perception from reasoning. In this paper, we propose Perceive-to-Reason (P2R), a unified framework that formulates fine-grained visual reasoning as a two-stage process: the model first localizes question-relevant evidence as a Perceiver, and then answers the question as a Reasoner based on the annotated image and cropped regions. To better align training with this decoupled formulation, we further introduce Perception-Reasoning Alternating GRPO (PRA-GRPO), a role-aware reinforcement learning strategy that alternates between perception-focused and reasoning-focused updates using only final-answer supervision. Built on top of Qwen3-VL-Instruct-2B/4B/8B, P2R consistently improves performance across model scales. In particular, P2R-4B achieves 93.2% on V-Star, 81.9% on HR-Bench-4K, and 80.5% on HR-Bench-8K, substantially outperforming its corresponding backbone. Further experiments show that the benefits of P2R extend beyond high-resolution benchmarks to broader multimodal reasoning tasks. These results suggest that explicitly decoupling perception from reasoning provides an effective framework for fine-grained visual reasoning.

tldr: Vision-language dataset distillation (VLDD) compresses a large image-text paired dataset into a small set of synthetic pairs that can efficiently train contrastive vision-language models under strict …
abstract: Vision-language dataset distillation (VLDD) compresses a large image-text paired dataset into a small set of synthetic pairs that can efficiently train contrastive vision-language models under strict data and compute budgets. Most existing methods match expert trajectories or cross-modal statistics, yet still enforce full-dimensional alignment in a Euclidean embedding space. This is often overly restrictive due to rank-deficient image--text correlation, with shared semantics concentrated in a low-dimensional range and remaining variation spread across a weakly correlated residual subspace. LoRS relaxes alignment at the similarity level by low-rank factorization, but does not explicitly control dominant alignment capacity and structure in the representation space. We thus propose a rank-aware hyperbolic alignment (RAHA) that combines hierarchical geometry with explicit alignment-capacity control. RAHA lifts multimodal representations to hyperbolic space and optimizes distilled pairs with asymmetric objectives that enforce geodesic alignment in the shared range while regularizing the residual subspace to preserve modality-private diversity and improve transfer robustness. Experiments on benchmarks show that RAHA demonstrates competitive cross-modal retrieval and improved transfer indicators under fixed budgets.

title: PixelEyes: Decoupling Perception and Reasoning for Pinpoint Visual Evidence Seeking
tldr: PixelEyes separates “what to look for” from “where it is” to reduce redundant multi-turn visual reasoning and improve pinpoint evidence seeking.
abstract: The paper studies why multi-turn visual reasoning with multimodal LLMs often degenerates into long, repetitive trajectories: the model is forced to reason and localize at the same time, so localization errors trigger more rounds of unnecessary reasoning. PixelEyes addresses this by decoupling the two roles: a reasoner determines what evidence is needed, and a specialized perception module performs precise localization. The method combines mask-guided visual search using referring segmentation and a semantic-region BFS strategy to avoid looping over wrong crops. The authors also create PixelEyes-6K for training and Pinpoint-Bench for zero-hint visual search evaluation with separate localization and reasoning supervision.
introduction: The work targets multi-turn visual reasoning settings where MLLMs must find evidence in images without explicit location hints. The authors observe a common failure mode: the model repeatedly misses the target, producing long trajectories with redundant turns because perception and reasoning are tightly coupled. PixelEyes re-architects the process into a two-stage agent, where reasoning selects the search objective and a dedicated perception tool provides mask-level localization. To make this work in practice, the system uses referring segmentation for precise mask-guided search and BFS over semantic regions to reduce wasted exploration. They further distill these behaviors into a new training set and introduce a benchmark designed to disentangle localization errors from reasoning errors.
key_contributions: Proposes PixelEyes, a multi-turn visual reasoning agent that explicitly decouples reasoning from perception.
Introduces mask-guided visual search via a referring segmentation model to provide precise localization.
Introduces semantic-region breadth-first search (BFS) to reduce redundant loops from repeatedly cropping incorrect regions.
Builds PixelEyes-6K by resynthesizing expert trajectories to embed the search and BFS logic into model training.
Creates Pinpoint-Bench, a zero-hint benchmark with instance-level masks and bounding boxes for fine-grained analysis of reasoning vs. localization failures.
why_it_matters: For Vision AI and Physical AI-style perception systems, the paper highlights that precise localization is often the bottleneck in multi-step reasoning, not just language or planning. The benchmark and decoupled agent design provide a clearer way to diagnose and improve failure modes in evidence-seeking tasks.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A["Query / question"] --> B["Reasoner: decide evidence needed"]
B --> C["Perception module: precise localization"]
C --> D["Mask-guided visual search"]
D --> E["Referring segmentation"]
D --> F["Semantic-region BFS"]
F --> G["Avoid wrong crop loops"]
C --> H["Pinpoint evidence for answer"]

tldr: PerceptionRubrics proposes a stricter, human-aligned multimodal evaluation framework that exposes brittleness hidden by saturated benchmark scores.
abstract: PerceptionRubrics is a rubric-based evaluation framework designed to close the gap between high benchmark scores and poor real-world robustness in multimodal models. It evaluates models by auditing atomic visual facts rather than relying on broad semantic matching, using 1,038 dense images and more than 12,000 instance-specific rubrics. The rubrics are derived from golden captions produced through a Circular Peer-Review consensus pipeline and split into essential "Must-Right" facts and finer-grained "Easy-Wrong" details. A gated scoring rule penalizes failures on mandatory facts sharply instead of averaging them away.
introduction: The paper argues that standard multimodal benchmarks can be misleading because models may score well while still failing on strict, conjunctive perceptual requirements. To address this, the authors introduce PerceptionRubrics, a rubric-based evaluation setup that turns each image-caption pair into a set of atomic criteria. These criteria are generated from consensus golden captions and organized into two tiers: essential facts that must be correct and more fragile details that are easy to get wrong. The evaluation uses gated scoring so that missing required visual facts causes a strong penalty, making the metric more sensitive to perceptual brittleness.
key_contributions: Introduces PerceptionRubrics, a rubric-based multimodal evaluation framework for atomic auditing of visual understanding.
Constructs a dataset of 1,038 information-dense images with over 12,000 instance-specific rubrics.
Proposes a Circular Peer-Review consensus pipeline to create golden captions used for rubric derivation.
Defines a dual-stream rubric structure with Must-Right and Easy-Wrong criteria.

Implements Gated Scoring, which applies binary penalties when essential perceptual facts are missed, and reports that this reveals a reliability gap and an approximately 8% perception deficit between open-source and proprietary models.
why_it_matters: For Physical AI and Vision AI, evaluation that rewards near-misses can hide failure modes that matter in real deployment, especially when exact perceptual fidelity is required. This work offers a stricter testing lens that better surfaces brittleness in dense visual reasoning settings.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A["Dense images"] --> B["Golden captions"]
B --> C["Circular peer-review consensus"]
C --> D["Atomic rubrics"]
D --> E["Must-Right facts"]
D --> F["Easy-Wrong details"]
E --> G["Gated scoring"]
F --> G
G --> H["Penalize missed facts"]

title: AI translation of literary texts is "fine", but readers still prefer human translations
tldr: Readers judged AI translations of literary novels as acceptable but still preferred human translations, and standard automatic metrics failed to capture those preferences.
abstract: This paper studies how avid readers experience machine-translated literary fiction compared with recent human translations. Using excerpts from 15 novels translated from French, Polish, and Japanese into English, the authors ask readers to evaluate both immersive reading of whole passages and close reading of aligned sentence chunks. Readers generally found the AI translations "fine," but preferred human translations for clarity, ease, and immersion. The study also finds that readers cannot reliably distinguish the two, and that automatic metrics, including LLM-as-a-judge methods, do not align with reader preference.
introduction: The authors focus on a gap in machine translation evaluation: literary quality, immersion, and reader experience are not well measured by standard adequacy or fluency metrics. To address this, they compare human translations with machine translations produced by an agentic LLM-based pipeline across 15 recent novels in French, Polish, and Japanese. Fifteen avid readers evaluated around 8,000-word excerpts in two settings: holistic reading of full excerpts and close reading of aligned translation chunks. The design allows the authors to probe not just correctness, but how the translations feel to readers in practice.
key_contributions: Conducted a reader-centered evaluation comparing human translations and LLM-based machine translations for 15 literary novels.
Collected two types of judgments: immersive excerpt-level comparisons and close-reading chunk-level comparisons, with alternating presentation order.
Found that readers preferred human translations overall, especially at the chunk level, while describing machine translations as generally "fine" but less clear, easy, and immersive.
Showed that readers could not reliably tell human and machine translations apart, and tended to prefer the version they believed was human.
Released LAIT (Literary AI Translation), including reader comments, judgments, preference ratings, span-level annotations, the evaluation protocol, and an interface.
why_it_matters: For NLP and Vision/Language-adjacent evaluation work, the study shows that benchmark-style automatic metrics and even LLM judges can miss the qualities that matter to end users in literary translation. It highlights the need for reader-centered evaluation when assessing AI systems in creative, high-subtlety domains.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A["15 novels\n(French/Polish/Japanese→English)"] --> B["Compare AI vs human\ntranslations"]
B --> C["Reader evaluation\nImmersive + close reading"]
C --> D["AI translation: 'fine'"]
C --> E["Human preferred\nclarity, ease, immersion"]
B --> F["Readers often can't tell\napart the two"]
B --> G["Automatic metrics\n& LLM judges"]
G --> H["Poor alignment with\nreader preference"]

title: Cross-Domain Generalization Failure in Lightweight Intrusion Detection Models for IIoT Networks
tldr: Lightweight IIoT intrusion detectors can look strong in-domain yet fail badly across networks, with their apparent robustness driven by brittle port-category shortcuts rather than transferable signal.
abstract: This study tests whether lightweight machine learning intrusion detectors trained on one industrial IoT network generalize to other, structurally different IIoT networks without retraining. Using only features shared across all datasets, the authors train four lightweight models on one dataset and evaluate them on two unseen target datasets. They find that the models rely heavily on coarse port-category features, and that this shortcut is much more common in the source attack traffic than in the target domains. The work also shows that class imbalance in evaluation can change which target network appears harder, and that adversarial robustness does not predict cross-network generalization.
introduction: The paper addresses a deployment gap in IIoT intrusion detection: many lightweight models are evaluated only on the same network they were trained on, even though real deployments must handle unseen networks with different traffic structure. To probe this, the authors train four lightweight architectures on one IIoT dataset and test them directly on two structurally distinct datasets, using only feature attributes available across all three sources. They then examine model explanations, adversarial robustness, and limited adaptation to understand why performance changes across domains. The central goal is to determine whether high within-domain accuracy actually reflects readiness for edge deployment in different IIoT environments.
key_contributions: Trains four lightweight intrusion detection architectures on one IIoT dataset and evaluates them, without retraining, on two structurally distinct target datasets.
Restricts feature use to attributes shared across all three datasets to enable cross-domain comparison.
Uses explainability analysis to show that the best-performing models rely overwhelmingly on coarse port-category features.
Finds that the dominant port-category shortcut is far more prevalent in source-domain attack traffic than in the target domains, suggesting coarsening relocates rather than eliminates the shortcut.

Shows that evaluation under naturally imbalanced class distributions can reverse which target domain appears more difficult, and that adversarial robustness is not correlated with cross-network generalization.
why_it_matters: For Physical AI and IIoT security, this is a cautionary result: models that look deployable in one network may not transfer to another, even when they are lightweight and seemingly robust. It argues for cross-network testing under realistic class imbalance as a prerequisite for deployment, not just within-dataset accuracy.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A["Train on one IIoT network"] --> B["Shared features only"]
B --> C["4 lightweight IDS models"]
C --> D["Test on 2 unseen networks"]
D --> E["Generalization failure"]
C --> F["Shortcut: port-category features"]
F --> G["Different attack traffic patterns"]
D --> H["Imbalance changes difficulty"]
D --> I["Adversarial robustness not predictive"]

title: Personalization as Inverse Planning: Learning Latent Design Intents for Agentic Slide Generation via Structural Denoising
tldr: SPIRE casts page-level slide personalization as inverse planning and learns latent design intent via multi-agent structural denoising, improving agentic slide generation without requiring tool-specific assumptions.
abstract: The paper addresses page-level slide personalization, where both deck themes and individual layouts need to be tailored to user preferences. Existing AI agent approaches rely too much on fixed templates or verbose instructions, so they miss latent design intent. The authors reformulate the problem as inverse planning and propose SPIRE, which learns by corrupting clean slide structures and training agents to denoise them into executable designs using reinforcement learning. They claim structural denoising is a consistent surrogate for personalization and show that the multi-agent setup reduces policy-gradient variance.
introduction: Personalizing slide generation is harder than choosing a theme: it also requires adjusting the layout and visual structure of each page in ways that reflect implicit user intent. Current agentic systems generally depend on prespecified templates or explicit instructions, which limits their ability to infer finer-grained preferences. This work reframes page-level slide personalization as an inverse planning problem, aiming to recover latent design intent from observed slide structure without assuming knowledge of the downstream toolchain. To make optimization tractable, the authors introduce SPIRE, a framework that deliberately corrupts clean slides and trains agents to denoise the corruption into valid designs, using reinforcement learning to coordinate the process.
key_contributions: Formulates page-level slide personalization (PSP) as an inverse planning problem.
Introduces SPIRE, a structural denoising framework that learns latent design intent without assuming specific execution tools such as PowerPoint or Beamer.
Uses a multi-agent reinforcement learning setup in which agents collaboratively refine executable slide designs.
Provides a proof that structural denoising is a consistent surrogate for PSP.
Shows that the multi-agent formulation strictly reduces policy-gradient variance in RL.
why_it_matters: For Vision AI and agentic design systems, this suggests a way to infer implicit layout preferences rather than depending on rigid templates or overly detailed prompts. The structural-denoising and multi-agent RL setup may be useful for other generation tasks where intent is latent but the output must remain executable.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A["Personalized slide design"] --> B["Inverse planning"]
B --> C["SPIRE"]
C --> D["Corrupt clean structure"]
D --> E["RL denoising to design"]
E --> F["Executable slides"]
C --> G["Multi-agent setup"]
G --> H["Lower policy-gradient variance"]

title: Multimodal Continuous Reasoning via Asymmetric Mutual Variational Learning
tldr: AMVL tackles train-inference mismatch in continuous multimodal reasoning by jointly calibrating prior and posterior with asymmetric KL losses, yielding strong gains on BLINK and other reasoning tasks.
abstract: The paper studies continuous latent reasoning for multimodal large language models, where reasoning happens in a latent space instead of being compressed into discrete tokens. A key challenge is that training often uses a posterior conditioned on the ground-truth answer, which can leak answer-specific shortcuts and create a mismatch with test-time inference. The proposed Asymmetric Mutual Variational Learning (AMVL) uses a bidirectional KL objective to align the inference-time prior with the posterior while also regularizing the posterior to stay compatible with inference. Experiments on a latent-integrated MLLM show consistent improvements over discrete and latent-reasoning baselines, especially on the BLINK benchmark.
introduction: This work addresses a central limitation of multimodal large language models: complex visual reasoning is often forced through a discrete language bottleneck, which can discard useful perceptual detail. The authors instead pursue continuous latent reasoning, aiming to learn implicit reasoning pathways that connect the input query to the final answer. However, standard variational training creates a train-inference mismatch because the training posterior can rely on the ground-truth answer, while the inference prior cannot. AMVL is introduced to resolve this by combining a forward KL term that aligns the prior to the posterior with a reverse KL term that prevents the posterior from drifting into answer-dependent regions. The paper also provides a theoretical analysis of this issue as prior contamination and shows empirically that the method improves both accuracy and latent-space stability.
key_contributions: Introduces Asymmetric Mutual Variational Learning (AMVL) for continuous multimodal reasoning.
Uses a bidirectional calibration objective: forward KL for prior-to-posterior alignment and reverse KL to regularize the posterior.
Formally analyzes train-inference mismatch as answer leakage / prior contamination and shows the dual-KL objective reduces it.
Instantiates AMVL in a latent-integrated MLLM and reports consistent gains over strong discrete and latent-reasoning baselines.
Achieves +10.83 average score on BLINK, with up to +32.00 improvement on individual reasoning tasks, alongside improved latent-space stability.
why_it_matters: For Vision AI and multimodal agents, this is a practical step toward reasoning in richer continuous latent spaces without collapsing into brittle token-level shortcuts. The method directly targets a common failure mode in latent-variable training, which could make multimodal reasoning systems more reliable at inference time.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A["Multimodal input"] --> B["Latent reasoning space"]
B --> C["Posterior w/ answer"]
B --> D["Prior for inference"]
C <--> E["Bidirectional KL"]
D <--> E
E --> F["Aligned latent inference"]
F --> G["Improved MLLM results"]

title: ASPIRE: Agentic /Skills Discovery for Robotics
tldr: ASPIRE is a continual, code-as-policy robot learning system that autonomously discovers, repairs, and reuses skills, improving performance across simulation, long-horizon tasks, and even real-robot transfer.
abstract: ASPIRE (Agentic Skill Programming through Iterative Robot Exploration) is a continual learning framework for robotics that writes and refines control programs autonomously. It combines closed-loop execution traces, automatic failure diagnosis and repair, a growing reusable skill library, and evolutionary search over task sequences and programs. The system is designed to discover skills that transfer across tasks, embodiments, and simulation-to-real settings.
introduction: Programming robots is difficult because it requires coordinating perception, contact-rich manipulation, and recovery from failures across many task variants. ASPIRE addresses this by treating robot control as code that can be iteratively generated, executed, debugged, and improved from multimodal execution traces. Its loop includes a robot execution engine for diagnosing and repairing failures, a skill library that accumulates validated fixes, and evolutionary search to explore diverse programs and task sequences. The method is meant to go beyond refining a single trajectory and instead build reusable skills over time.
key_contributions: Introduces ASPIRE, a continual learning system for agentic robot skill discovery in a code-as-policy paradigm.
Uses closed-loop multimodal execution traces to support autonomous failure diagnosis, repair synthesis, and validation.
Builds a continually expanding skill library that distills validated fixes into reusable and transferable knowledge.
Employs evolutionary search to generate diverse task sequences and control programs for broader exploration.

Reports strong benchmark gains: up to 77% on LIBERO-Pro, 72% on Robosuite bimanual handover, and 32% on BEHAVIOR-1K; also improves zero-shot long-horizon generalization and shows evidence of sim-to-real transfer.
why_it_matters: For Physical AI, ASPIRE points to a scalable way to turn robot experience into reusable skills rather than one-off solutions. Its ability to generalize across tasks, embodiments, and some real-robot settings is especially relevant for reducing programming effort in long-horizon manipulation.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A["Task / Robot"] --> B["Closed-loop execution"]
B --> C["Trace + failure diagnosis"]
C --> D["Repair / refine program"]
D --> E["Skill library"]
E --> F["Evolutionary search"]
F --> G["New task sequence / control program"]
G --> B

title: Learning to Watch: Active Video Anomaly Understanding via Interleaved Policy Optimization
tldr: Anom-π turns video anomaly understanding into an active decision-making problem, learning when to inspect, backtrack, and sample more evidence rather than passively watching fixed clips.
abstract: The paper argues that video anomaly understanding is limited by static observation, which can miss or confuse sparse, context-dependent cues. To address this, the authors propose Anom-π, a closed-loop framework that combines internal reasoning with active evidence acquisition through temporal actions like backtracking, expanding the time window, and fine-grained sampling. Because the task is weakly supervised at the video level, they introduce Interactive Direct Preference Optimization (iDPO) to align the policy at the trajectory level, using an Active Evidence Inquiry (AEI) utility that trades off task success, informative evidence, and interaction cost. The authors report that a 2B-parameter model achieves strong results and outperforms state-of-the-art large-scale VAU models on complex scenarios.
introduction: Video anomaly understanding is difficult because anomalous events are often sparse and highly dependent on context, so simply sampling a video passively can lead to observational aliasing. The proposed Anom-π framework reframes the problem as an active sequential decision process in which the model decides how to gather evidence over time while reasoning about the current hypothesis. It borrows ideas from human video review, using interleaved policy behavior with temporal atomic operators such as local backtracking, temporal expansion, and fine-grained sampling. To train these interaction strategies under weak supervision, the authors design iDPO for trajectory-level policy alignment and use AEI to balance usefulness against interaction cost.
key_contributions: Proposes Anom-π, a closed-loop active video anomaly understanding framework instead of passive fixed-window video analysis.
Introduces an interleaved policy that combines reasoning with active evidence gathering via temporal atomic operators: local backtracking, temporal expansion, and fine-grained sampling.
Designs Interactive Direct Preference Optimization (iDPO) to learn trajectory-level policies from weak video-level supervision.
Defines Active Evidence Inquiry (AEI) utility to balance task success, evidence informativeness, and interaction cost.
Reports that a 2B-parameter model achieves competitive performance and outperforms prior large-scale VAU methods in complex scenarios.
why_it_matters: This work suggests that anomaly understanding can benefit from agentic, interactive perception rather than purely feedforward video classification. That is relevant to Vision AI and physical-world monitoring systems where targeted inspection and evidence seeking can improve robustness under sparse cues.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A["Static video view"] --> B["Sparse, context-dependent cues"]
B --> C["Anom-π closed loop"]
C --> D["Reason + active evidence inquiry"]
D --> E["Temporal actions: backtrack / expand / fine-sample"]
E --> F["iDPO trajectory alignment"]
F --> G["AEI utility: success vs info vs cost"]
G --> H["Better anomaly understanding"]

title: MindEdit-Bench: Benchmarking Object-Level Counterfactual Spatial Reasoning in VLMs from In-the-Wild Photos
tldr: MindEdit-Bench shows that current VLMs struggle badly with object-level counterfactual spatial reasoning, especially when the answer is not explicitly visible in any input view.
abstract: The paper introduces MindEdit-Bench, a benchmark for testing whether vision-language models can reason about the effects of hypothetically moving or rotating objects in indoor scenes. Unlike standard spatial benchmarks that focus on describing visible relations or changing the observer viewpoint, this benchmark includes two counterfactual tasks where the correct answer is absent from all provided images. The benchmark is built from newly captured smartphone photo triplets and an automatic 3D scene-graph extraction pipeline, with 1,003 human-verified multiple-choice questions across 120 private indoor scenes. Results show a large gap between humans and 15 evaluated VLMs, with models performing especially poorly on the new editing tasks.
introduction: MindEdit-Bench addresses a gap in existing VLM evaluation: most spatial reasoning benchmarks measure what is already observable, rather than whether a model can predict the consequences of hypothetical object edits. The benchmark uses three-photo indoor scene triplets captured in the wild and converts them into structured scene representations for six spatial reasoning tasks. Four tasks test perception and perspective transformation, while two new tasks—spatial editing and cross-view visibility editing—require object-level counterfactual reasoning. Because answers are selected from 8–24 structured options, the benchmark can diagnose both spatial reasoning mistakes and fallback behaviors.
key_contributions: Introduces MindEdit-Bench, a six-task benchmark for spatial reasoning in VLMs built from in-the-wild indoor photo triplets.
Adds two counterfactual tasks (L4 spatial editing and L5 cross-view visibility editing) where the correct answer is not visible in any input image.
Constructs the benchmark from 120 newly captured private indoor scenes using an automatic 3D scene-graph extraction pipeline, reducing overlap with public pretraining data.
Provides 1,003 human-verified multiple-choice questions with structured answer sets of 8–24 choices for fine-grained error analysis.
Shows that 15 VLMs achieve only 8%–31% mean task accuracy, while human majority-vote accuracy is 81%–97%, revealing a 53 percentage-point pooled human-best-VLM gap.
why_it_matters: For Physical AI and Vision AI, the benchmark exposes a major weakness in current VLMs: they can often describe visible layouts but fail to reason about how scenes change under hypothetical object transformations. This matters for embodied interaction, robotics, and spatial planning, where models must infer unobserved consequences rather than just recognize what is present.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A["Smartphone photo triplets"] --> B["3D scene-graph extraction"]
B --> C["MindEdit-Bench"]
C --> D["Visible relation tasks"]
C --> E["Counterfactual edit tasks"]
E --> F["Move or rotate object"]
F --> G["Answer absent from images"]
C --> H["Human vs VLM evaluation"]

title: StochasT: Learning with Stochastic Turn Depth for Visual Instruction Tuning
tldr: StochasT randomizes conversational turn depth during visual instruction tuning to better align training with single-turn evaluation and improve LVLM robustness.
abstract: Large vision-language models are often trained with multi-turn visual instruction data, but are typically evaluated in isolated single-turn settings. The authors argue that this mismatch can cause visual attention decay and contextual overfitting during training. They propose StochasT, which stochastically groups tasks for the same image into clusters of varying turn depth while keeping their original order, so the model sees diverse conversational structures without dropping data. They also introduce a Balanced Latin Square-based evaluation scheme to test robustness under different contextual dependencies.
introduction: The paper addresses a training-evaluation mismatch in visual instruction tuning for large vision-language models: training data is often conversational and multi-turn, while benchmarks usually probe single-turn performance. This mismatch may encourage models to rely too much on context from earlier turns or lose focus on the image over longer dialogues. To mitigate this, the authors propose StochasT, a stochastic turn-depth strategy that reorganizes tasks for the same image into variable-sized clusters while preserving their sequence. The method is designed to improve robustness without discarding any training examples, and it is paired with a benchmark-agnostic evaluation protocol based on the Balanced Latin Square.
key_contributions: Identifies a mismatch between multi-turn visual instruction tuning and single-turn evaluation for LVLMs.
Proposes StochasT, which stochastically varies turn depth by grouping tasks for the same image into clusters of different sizes while preserving order.
Frames the method as analogous to dropout/stochastic depth in spirit, but without removing any training data.
Introduces a Balanced Latin Square-based evaluation mechanism to assess robustness under varying contextual dependencies.
Reports that StochasT improves LVLM performance in both single-turn and multi-turn settings.
why_it_matters: For Vision AI, this offers a simple way to make instruction-tuned LVLMs less brittle when faced with different dialogue structures. More broadly, it highlights that benchmark design and training format can materially affect perceived multimodal reasoning ability.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A["Multi-turn visual instruction data"] --> B["StochasT grouping"]
B --> C["Stochastic turn depth clusters"]
C --> D["Keep original order"]
D --> E["Train on diverse conversations"]
E --> F["Reduce attention decay & overfitting"]
F --> G["Balanced Latin Square eval"]
G --> H["Robustness under context shifts"]

title: DART: Difficulty-Adaptive Routing for Zero-Shot Video Temporal Grounding
tldr: DART routes easy video grounding queries to a fast path and complex ones to a reasoning-heavy slow path, improving zero-shot temporal localization while using far fewer frames.
abstract: This work addresses zero-shot video temporal grounding, where a model must localize the time span of an event in an untrimmed video from a text query without task-specific training. The authors identify a reasoning gap: frame-query matching works for simpler events but struggles with multi-stage queries that require temporal order and causal understanding. DART introduces difficulty-adaptive routing using a query-conditioned Determinantal Point Process to select diverse evidence frames and estimate query difficulty. Simple queries use a fast direct prediction path, while harder ones are processed by a slow path with structured temporal reasoning prompts for large vision-language models.
introduction: Zero-shot temporal grounding asks a model to find when a queried event occurs in a video without training on the target task. Existing approaches mainly compare frame and query features, which is effective for straightforward events but weak on queries that involve multiple steps, ordering, or causal relationships. DART tackles this by using a query-conditioned DPP both to pick diverse keyframes and to measure how difficult the query is. Based on that difficulty estimate, the system routes samples either to a fast prediction branch or to a slow branch that performs structured reasoning before extracting temporal boundaries.
key_contributions: Introduces DART, a difficulty-adaptive routing framework for zero-shot video temporal grounding.
Uses a query-conditioned Determinantal Point Process to select diverse, query-relevant keyframes as temporal evidence.
Derives a spectral entropy-based difficulty indicator from the DPP to decide between fast and slow inference paths.
Proposes Temporal Markup Prompting for the slow path, decomposing grounding into global event analysis, temporal role annotation, and boundary extraction.
Reports state-of-the-art zero-shot results on Charades-STA and ActivityNet Captions, with up to 3.5 mIoU improvement over the strongest baseline and over 7x fewer frames used.
why_it_matters: The method shows how Physical AI systems can combine efficient perception with conditional reasoning rather than applying heavyweight inference uniformly. For vision-language grounding, it improves robustness on hard temporal queries while substantially reducing computation.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A["Text query + untrimmed video"] --> B["Frame-query matching"]
B --> C["Query difficulty estimate"]
C --> D["Select diverse frames (DPP)"]
D --> E{"Difficulty-adaptive routing"}
E --> F["Fast path: direct prediction"]
E --> G["Slow path: structured temporal reasoning"]
F --> H["Temporal span output"]
G --> H

title: AdaBoosting Text Prompts for Vision-Language Models
tldr: Text Prompt Boosting (TPB) uses an AdaBoost-style sequence of text-prompt classifiers to better exploit few-shot labels and transfer prompt gains across vision-language models.
abstract: The paper studies how the quality of text prompts affects the classification accuracy of pretrained vision-language models. Existing few-shot prompt construction methods do not explicitly emphasize misclassified examples, so their gains remain limited even when more labeled shots are available. The authors propose Text Prompt Boosting (TPB), which builds an ensemble of prompt-based classifiers sequentially and focuses each step on hard examples, similar to AdaBoost. Experiments on eleven benchmarks show improved accuracy on the source model and better preservation of few-shot gains when transferring prompts to larger VLMs.
introduction: Pretrained vision-language models often classify images by matching image features to text prompts, so prompt quality is central to performance. Handcrafted or LLM-generated prompts are attractive because they are interpretable and can be reused across models, but few-shot prompt learning methods typically do not directly target the examples they misclassify. This limits how much they benefit from additional labeled data. TPB addresses this by treating each prompt-based classifier as a weak learner and combining them in a boosting-style procedure that explicitly emphasizes hard examples, producing prompts that retain task-specific cues in text space and transfer more robustly across VLMs.
key_contributions: Introduces Text Prompt Boosting (TPB), an AdaBoost-inspired framework for few-shot text prompt construction in VLMs.
Treats each text-prompt-based classifier as a weak learner and sequentially aggregates them into a stronger ensemble.
Explicitly focuses on misclassified or hard examples during prompt construction, addressing a limitation of prior few-shot prompting methods.
Shows that TPB preserves task-intrinsic, model-agnostic cues in text space, supporting cross-model transfer.
Demonstrates improvements on eleven classification benchmarks, including better source-model accuracy and more durable few-shot gains on larger VLMs.
why_it_matters: For Vision AI, this suggests a simple way to make prompt-based classification more sample-efficient and more transferable across different pretrained VLMs. The boosting perspective is also relevant for building reusable prompt assets that keep their value when moving to stronger models.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A["Labeled shots"] --> B["Build prompt classifier"]
B --> C["Score examples"]
C --> D["Find hard cases"]
D --> E["Update prompts"]
E --> F["Next prompt classifier"]
F --> G["Ensemble output"]
G --> H["Better accuracy"]

title: LeVLJEPA: End-to-End Vision-Language Pretraining Without Negatives
tldr: LeVLJEPA is a fully non-contrastive vision-language pretraining method that learns dense semantic features without negatives, temperatures, momentum encoders, or teacher-student schedules.
abstract: The paper argues that vision-language pretraining has remained overly dependent on contrastive learning, even as downstream use increasingly relies on dense patch-level features rather than a single pooled embedding. To address this, the authors propose LeVLJEPA, an end-to-end non-contrastive method based on cross-modal prediction with stop-gradient targets and per-modality distributional regularization. The method is reported to train stably at large scale and to produce stronger dense semantic representations than evaluated contrastive baselines.
introduction: Vision-language encoders are no longer used only for zero-shot classification; they are often frozen and plugged into multimodal models or dense prediction pipelines that need the full spatial grid of tokens. This makes dense semantic quality more important than global retrieval-style objectives alone. LeVLJEPA introduces a non-contrastive alternative to standard vision-language pretraining by predicting across modalities with stop-gradient targets and regularizing each modality’s output distribution. The authors position this as the first fully non-contrastive end-to-end vision-language pretraining approach, and evaluate it as a backbone for downstream tasks.
key_contributions: Introduces LeVLJEPA, described as the first fully non-contrastive end-to-end vision-language pretraining method.
Uses cross-modal prediction with stop-gradient targets and per-modality distributional regularization, without negatives, temperature scaling, a momentum encoder, or a teacher-student schedule.
Reports stable large-scale training despite the absence of contrastive machinery.
Shows the learned encoder is strongest among evaluated encoders as a frozen vision-language backbone on GQA, VQAv2, and POPE under two language models.
Outperforms contrastive baselines on semantic segmentation while remaining comparable on global readout tasks such as linear probing.
why_it_matters: For Vision AI systems that rely on frozen backbones, this suggests non-contrastive objectives can yield better dense patch-level semantics than contrastive pretraining. That is relevant for multimodal assistants and dense prediction models that need spatially rich visual features, not just global image embeddings.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A["Vision-language pairs"] --> B["Cross-modal prediction"]
B --> C["Stop-gradient targets"]
C --> D["Per-modality regularization"]
D --> E["End-to-end training"]
E --> F["Dense semantic features"]

title: GMO-E$^2$DIT: Grounded Multi-Operation Editing for E-Commerce Images
tldr: GMO-E$^2$DIT is an agentic e-commerce image editor that decomposes underspecified multi-operation requests into grounded sub-edits, executes them iteratively, and reflects on intermediate results to improve completion and fidelity.
abstract: The paper studies e-commerce image editing tasks that require multiple localized changes while preserving the rest of the image. To handle underspecified instructions and reduce partial execution failures, the authors propose GMO-E$^2$DIT, which combines a vision-language model for planning with a mask-conditioned editor for rendering. The system uses a reflection loop to inspect intermediate outputs, preserve successful edits, retry unfinished ones, and recover from errors. The authors also introduce a unified training data pipeline and EComEditBench for evaluation, and report competitive results against strong closed-source models.
introduction: Real-world product-image editing in e-commerce is often compositional: a single request may involve several localized operations that must be applied accurately and audibly without altering unrelated content. Existing one-shot editing systems tend to merge instruction understanding, spatial grounding, and image synthesis into a single pass, which can lead to missed steps or incorrect edits. GMO-E$^2$DIT addresses this by separating planning from rendering: a VLM first builds a region-grounded edit agenda, then a mask-conditioned editor executes each sub-operation. The framework adds reflection to inspect intermediate states and decide what to keep, retry, or continue, improving robustness for multi-turn task completion.
key_contributions: Introduces GMO-E$^2$DIT, an agentic framework for grounded multi-operation e-commerce image editing.
Decouples cognitive planning from generative editing by using a VLM to produce a region-grounded edit agenda.
Uses operation-aware masks/references and a reflection-driven loop to execute, inspect, retry, and preserve partial progress across sub-edits.
Develops a unified data pipeline with aligned supervision for planning, execution, and reflection.
Proposes EComEditBench for instruction-driven evaluation and reports competitive performance with improved instruction accuracy and edit fidelity.
why_it_matters: For Vision AI in commercial settings, the work targets a common failure mode: incomplete or mislocalized edits when instructions are complex or underspecified. The agentic, iterative design is relevant to broader multi-step visual task automation where reliability and auditability matter.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A["E-commerce image + instruction"] --> B["VLM planner"]
B --> C["Local edit plan"]
C --> D["Mask-conditioned editor"]
D --> E["Intermediate result"]
E --> F["Reflection loop"]
F -->|"keep done"| D
F -->|"retry missing"| B
F -->|"recover errors"| D
E --> G["Final edited image"]

title: EFlow: Learning Evidence Flow for Long-Video Reasoning with Adaptive Reflection
tldr: EFlow tackles long-video reasoning by forcing evidence retrieval before answer inference and adding a confidence-aware reflection step when evidence looks insufficient.
abstract: The paper argues that long-video reasoning is limited by how models collect and use visual evidence, especially when grounding and answering are mixed in one trajectory. This can cause premature semantic commitment, where early guesses bias what evidence gets retrieved, and that evidence then reinforces the wrong answer. To fix this, EFlow separates temporal grounding from reasoning using explicit chains of thought and adds a reflection mechanism that can revisit the full video if the retrieved evidence seems inadequate. The authors train the system with supervised fine-tuning, reinforcement learning, and reinforcement fine-tuning, and report consistent gains on five video understanding benchmarks.
introduction: Long-video question answering is challenging because models must first locate the right moments in a long sequence and then reason over them. The authors identify a common failure mode in existing tool-augmented video systems: grounding and reasoning happen in a single loop, so an early hypothesis can distort evidence selection. EFlow addresses this by making the process evidence-first, with separate chains of thought for temporal grounding and for reasoning. It also adds adaptive reflection, which allows the model to re-check the whole video when confidence in the retrieved evidence is low.
key_contributions: Introduces EFlow, an evidence-first framework for long-video reasoning built on Qwen3-VL.
Separates temporal grounding and logical reasoning into two explicit stages: CoT for Temporal Grounding and CoT for Reasoning.
Adds a confidence-aware reflection mechanism that can re-evaluate the full video when evidence retrieval appears insufficient.
Constructs dedicated trajectory datasets and trains the system using supervised fine-tuning, reinforcement learning, and reinforcement fine-tuning.
Reports consistent improvements across five video understanding benchmarks.
why_it_matters: For Vision AI, the work targets a core bottleneck in long-context video understanding: getting the right evidence before committing to an answer. The evidence-first design and adaptive reflection could make tool-augmented video agents more reliable on long-horizon tasks.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A["Long video"] --> B["Temporal grounding"]
B --> C["Evidence retrieval"]
C --> D["Reasoning"]
D --> E["Answer"]
D --> F["Adaptive reflection"]
F --> A
B --> G["Separate grounding and reasoning"]

title: LongVQUBench: Benchmarking Long-Term Video Quality Understanding of Vision-Language Models
tldr: LongVQUBench is a new benchmark showing that state-of-the-art vision-language models struggle as video length and reasoning complexity increase in long-term video quality assessment.
abstract: The paper introduces LongVQUBench to evaluate how well large vision-language models understand video quality over long durations. Unlike existing benchmarks that focus on short clips or isolated distortions, this dataset covers more than 1200 videos from varied domains and includes 1500 multiple-choice and open-ended questions. The benchmark tests three levels of reasoning—local, cross-event, and global quality understanding—and also embeds sparse needle distortion questions to measure fine-grained detection. Results on 14 LVLMs show clear performance drops as videos get longer and reasoning becomes more complex.
introduction: Long-term video quality understanding is difficult because quality issues in extended videos can be cumulative, temporally distributed, and require linking evidence across distant moments. The authors argue that current benchmarks are too narrow, since they mostly examine short clips and single distortions rather than sustained perceptual reasoning. To address this, they propose LongVQUBench, which spans diverse video domains and evaluates models at three hierarchical levels: local event quality understanding, cross-event quality reasoning, and global quality understanding. They further add needle distortion QA tasks to test whether models can detect sparse artifacts and attribute them correctly over time. The benchmark is used to probe 14 state-of-the-art LVLMs.
key_contributions: Introduces LongVQUBench, a benchmark for long-term video quality understanding with over 1200 videos across multiple content domains.
Provides 1500 multiple-choice and open-ended questions for validation and testing.
Defines a երեք-level evaluation hierarchy: local event quality understanding (LQU), cross-event quality reasoning (CQR), and global quality understanding (GQU).
Adds a needle distortion QA (NDQA) paradigm to test sparse spatial and temporal artifact detection.
Reports extensive experiments on 14 LVLMs, showing notable degradation with longer videos and deeper reasoning demands.
why_it_matters: This benchmark exposes a major gap in current LVLM evaluation: models may handle short, localized video quality judgments but fail on long-horizon perceptual integration. It is relevant for vision AI systems that need reliable analysis of surveillance, egocentric, and cinematic video streams.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A["LongVQUBench"] --> B["1200+ long videos"]
A --> C["1500 questions"]
C --> D["Reasoning levels"]
D --> D1["Local"]
D --> D2["Cross-event"]
D --> D3["Global"]
A --> E["Needle distortions"]
A --> F["Evaluate 14 LVLMs"]
F --> G["Longer videos hurt performance"]

title: FurnitureVLA: Learning Long-Horizon Bimanual Furniture Assembly with Vision-Language-Action Model
tldr: FurnitureVLA tackles real-scale bimanual furniture assembly with a progress-aware vision-language-action model that improves long-horizon execution in simulation and transfers to a real Kinova Gen3 system.
abstract: The paper studies real-scale bimanual furniture assembly, moving beyond toy-scale or single-arm settings. It introduces FurnitureVLA, a vision-language-action approach paired with a simulation pipeline and a VR teleoperation system for collecting expert demonstrations. To handle long-horizon tasks with many subtasks and control steps, the method adds a continuous progress signal to help with subtask transitions and reduce compounding errors. The system shows strong gains in simulation and limited performance drop on a real robot.
introduction: Furniture assembly is a long-horizon manipulation problem that requires precise perception, coordination between two arms, and reliable progression through many subtasks. This work formalizes the task for real-scale bimanual assembly and builds the infrastructure needed to study it systematically, including scalable simulation for data generation/evaluation and VR teleoperation for high-quality real demonstrations. The proposed FurnitureVLA model is finetuned on semantically grounded subtasks and predicts both actions and progress, allowing it to decide when to move to the next subtask automatically. The authors also analyze design factors in perception and control that matter for success in real-scale assembly.
key_contributions: Introduces FurnitureVLA, the first systematic study of real-scale bimanual furniture assembly with VLAs.
Develops a scalable simulation pipeline for expert data generation and evaluation.
Builds a VR teleoperation system for single-operator bimanual control to collect real-world demonstrations.
Proposes a progress-enhanced VLA that predicts both actions and a continuous progress signal to support automatic subtask transitions and reduce compounding errors.

Reports improved performance: simulation success rises from 48% to 80% over baselines across three furniture types, with a further 21% gain from design-factor studies, and only a 16% drop on the hardest real-world task.
why_it_matters: This work pushes Vision AI and Physical AI toward realistic long-horizon bimanual manipulation, a setting closer to practical home/industrial assembly than prior toy-scale benchmarks. Its progress-aware control formulation and data collection pipeline may be useful for other multi-step robotic tasks that need reliable subtask switching and strong sim-to-real transfer.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A["Bimanual furniture assembly"] --> B["VR teleop demos"]
B --> C["Simulation pipeline"]
C --> D["FurnitureVLA"]
D --> E["Vision-language-action policy"]
E --> F["Continuous progress signal"]
F --> G["Long-horizon subtask transitions"]
G --> H["Real robot transfer"]

title: GenAU: Language-Grounded Industrial Anomaly Understanding with Vision-Language Models
tldr: GenAU unifies industrial anomaly detection, segmentation, multi-type detection, and defect explanation in one instruction-following vision-language model with language-grounded segmentation tokens.
abstract: The paper argues that industrial inspection needs more than a yes/no anomaly score: it should also localize defects, identify defect types, and explain them in language. GenAU is a generalist vision-language framework that adds two special segmentation tokens to a VLM so their hidden states can query multi-scale visual features for pixel-level localization, while the decoder also produces text-based defect judgments and structured explanations. The model is trained with a joint language-modeling and segmentation objective, enabling image-level detection, pixel-level segmentation, multi-type anomaly detection, and defect analysis in one architecture.
introduction: Industrial anomaly inspection is usually treated as either detection or segmentation, but practical deployment needs a more complete understanding of what is wrong and where. GenAU addresses this by integrating a vision-language model with language-grounded segmentation queries, using [SEG_defect] and [SEG_normal] tokens to produce masks from multi-scale visual features. The same model also emits image-level normal/defect decisions and structured textual descriptions, making the system instruction-following and more interpretable. The training recipe combines language modeling and segmentation supervision so the model can cover four related tasks in a unified framework. The paper evaluates this across cross-dataset benchmarks and positions the approach as a generalist alternative to separate specialized models.
key_contributions: Introduces GenAU, a unified vision-language framework for industrial anomaly understanding across detection, segmentation, multi-type detection, and defect analysis.
Proposes two segmentation tokens, [SEG_defect] and [SEG_normal], whose hidden states act as language-grounded queries for pixel-level localization over multi-scale visual features.
Combines the segmentation map with the decoder’s textual normal/defect decision to produce an image-level anomaly score.
Trains the model with a joint language-modeling and segmentation objective to support instruction-following defect descriptions alongside localization.
Reports strongest image-level detection among CLIP-based zero-shot methods on VisA and Real-IAD, with segmentation performance approaching but not exceeding specialized CLIP baselines.
why_it_matters: For Physical AI and industrial Vision AI, the work points toward inspectors that can both see and explain defects rather than only classify them. The unified formulation is useful when downstream users need interpretable, language-grounded outputs alongside localization.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A["Industrial image"] --> B["VLM + 2 seg tokens"]
B --> C["Multi-scale feature query"]
C --> D["Pixel-level localization"]
B --> E["Text decoder"]
E --> F["Defect type + explanation"]
D --> G["Joint LM + seg training"]
F --> G["Joint LM + seg training"]

title: Perceive-to-Reason: Decoupling Perception and Reasoning for Fine-Grained Visual Reasoning
tldr: P2R separates fine-grained visual reasoning into evidence localization and answer generation, and this decoupling boosts high-resolution VLM performance across scales.
abstract: The paper argues that fine-grained visual reasoning is hard because small but crucial cues are often hidden in high-resolution images. To address this, it proposes Perceive-to-Reason (P2R), which first localizes question-relevant evidence and then answers using both the annotated image and cropped regions. The authors also introduce PRA-GRPO, an alternating reinforcement-learning strategy that updates perception and reasoning roles separately using only final-answer supervision. Built on Qwen3-VL-Instruct models, the approach improves performance on several benchmarks and appears to help beyond high-resolution tasks as well.
introduction: Fine-grained visual reasoning in vision-language models often requires finding subtle visual evidence before answering, but many existing methods blur the line between locating evidence and reasoning over it. P2R explicitly decomposes the task into two stages: a Perceiver that identifies relevant regions and a Reasoner that uses those regions to answer the question. To train this setup, the authors propose PRA-GRPO, a role-aware RL method that alternates between perception-oriented and reasoning-oriented updates while relying only on final-answer labels. The framework is evaluated on Qwen3-VL-Instruct models of multiple sizes and shows consistent gains, especially on high-resolution benchmarks.
key_contributions: Introduces Perceive-to-Reason (P2R), a two-stage framework that explicitly decouples evidence localization from answer reasoning.
Proposes PRA-GRPO, an alternating reinforcement-learning strategy that separately updates perception and reasoning behaviors using only final-answer supervision.
Demonstrates consistent improvements over the corresponding Qwen3-VL-Instruct backbones across 2B, 4B, and 8B model scales.
Reports strong results on high-resolution benchmarks, including 93.2% on V-Star, 81.9% on HR-Bench-4K, and 80.5% on HR-Bench-8K for P2R-4B.
Finds that the benefits of P2R extend beyond high-resolution visual reasoning to broader multimodal reasoning tasks.
why_it_matters: This work suggests that explicitly separating perception from reasoning can make vision-language models better at handling small, critical visual cues in dense images. That is relevant for Vision AI systems that need reliable evidence extraction before final decision-making.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A["High-res image + question"] --> B["Localize relevant evidence"]
B --> C["Annotated image + crops"]
C --> D["Reason over evidence"]
D --> E["Final answer"]
F["PRA-GRPO"] --> B
F --> D
G["Answer-only supervision"] --> F

title: QCA: Query- and Content-Aware Keyframe Selection for Long Video Understanding
tldr: QCA selects a small, query-aware set of video keyframes without extra training, improving long-video understanding efficiency and accuracy in Video-LLMs.
abstract: The paper addresses temporal redundancy in long video understanding, where dense frame sampling is computationally expensive and often includes many irrelevant frames. QCA is a query- and content-aware keyframe selection method that partitions a video into segments, estimates each segment’s importance using both query relevance and content change, and allocates keyframe budget accordingly. It then picks an initial query-relevant frame per segment and adds diverse frames while preserving semantic relevance. The method is training-free, can plug into existing Video-LLMs, and shows strong results across long-video benchmarks.
introduction: Long video understanding is difficult because most frames are redundant, yet only a few may matter for answering a specific query. QCA tackles this by using both the query and the video content to decide which temporal segments deserve more keyframes, rather than treating all parts of the video equally. Within each segment, it starts from the most query-relevant frame and expands the selected set to increase diversity without losing relevance. The approach is designed to work without additional training and to integrate directly into existing Video-LLM pipelines.
key_contributions: Introduces QCA, a query- and content-aware framework for selecting compact keyframe subsets from long videos.
Uses segment-level estimation of information contribution based on query relevance and content deviation to dynamically allocate keyframe budgets.
Selects frames within each segment by anchoring on the most query-relevant frame and iteratively adding diverse yet relevant frames.
Requires no additional training and can be seamlessly integrated into existing Video-LLMs.
Reports state-of-the-art performance on multiple long-video understanding benchmarks, including 67.8% on LongVideoBench with 128 frames vs. GPT-4o’s 66.7% with 256 frames.
why_it_matters: This work improves the efficiency-accuracy tradeoff in long video reasoning by reducing frame count while preserving query-relevant information. That is directly useful for Vision AI systems that need to scale to long contexts, especially Video-LLMs.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A["Long video"] --> B["Segment video"]
B --> C["Score segments\n(query + content)"]
C --> D["Allocate keyframe budget"]
D --> E["Pick query-relevant frame"]
E --> F["Add diverse frames"]
F --> G["Keyframes to Video-LLM"]

title: Towards Developing a Multimodal Chat Assistant for University Stakeholders: RAG-based Approach
tldr: A multimodal university chatbot uses RAG, vision-language inputs, and quantized deployment to answer stakeholder queries more reliably while sharply reducing hallucinations.
abstract: The paper presents a multimodal chatbot for university stakeholders, aimed at improving access to timely and reliable institutional information. It combines retrieval-augmented generation with a large language model so answers are grounded in university resources such as the handbook, and it supports both text and image queries through a vision-language model. The system is designed for practical deployment with quantized inference, a FastAPI backend, and a Next.js frontend. Evaluation suggests strong user satisfaction for both text and image queries, while hallucination drops substantially with RAG.
introduction: University stakeholders often need quick answers to policy and institution-specific questions, but rule-based chatbots struggle with complex queries and changing documents. To address this gap, the authors build a multimodal assistant that retrieves relevant passages from university-centric sources and uses an LLM to generate context-based responses. The system accepts both text and image inputs, enabling users to query content such as document images through a vision-language model. The implementation emphasizes deployability on constrained hardware and real-time use through a modern web stack. Experiments show that retrieval grounding improves response reliability, though visual queries incur longer latency.
key_contributions: Built a multimodal university chatbot that supports both text and image queries.
Integrated retrieval-augmented generation to ground responses in institution-specific documents such as the university handbook.
Used a vision-language model for image-based querying and quantized inference for lightweight deployment.
Implemented a scalable FastAPI backend and Next.js frontend for real-time usability.
Reported a reduction in hallucination from 31.7% to 6.6% with the proposed RAG-based system.
why_it_matters: For university-facing assistants, grounding answers in authoritative documents is critical, and this work shows a practical path to reduce hallucinations while supporting multimodal queries. The deployment-oriented design is also relevant for real-world institutional AI systems that must run efficiently on limited hardware.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A["University resources"] --> B["Retriever"]
B --> C["RAG + LLM"]
D["Text query"] --> E["Vision-Language model"]
F["Image query"] --> E
E --> C
C --> G["Grounded answer"]
C --> H["FastAPI / Next.js app"]

title: MoHallBench: A Benchmark for Motion Hallucination in Video Large Language Models
tldr: MoHallBench is a new benchmark showing that VideoLLMs often hallucinate human motion, especially when they rely on priors, sequential expectations, or lookalike motions rather than visual evidence.
abstract: This paper introduces MoHallBench, a benchmark for measuring motion hallucination in video large language models. The benchmark targets a video-specific failure mode where models infer human motions that are not actually present, and it evaluates three common sources of this error: co-occurrence priors, sequential inference, and similarity confusion. It includes 11,306 clips and 40,493 question-answer pairs across binary, multiple-choice, and generative formats. Experiments on ten open-source VideoLLMs show that good action recognition does not necessarily mean low hallucination, and that sequential inference is the most problematic setting.
introduction: VideoLLMs have improved at understanding video, but they can still produce answers that conflict with the actual visual content. The authors argue that prior benchmarks underemphasize motion hallucination, where a model infers a human action or motion that never occurs in the clip. To address this gap, they propose MoHallBench, which systematically tests hallucination arising from co-occurrence priors, sequential inference, and motion similarity confusion. They also introduce a bi-directional questioning protocol with bias-aware metrics to reduce affirmation bias in binary evaluations. Their experiments reveal that hallucination resistance is not well captured by standard action-recognition performance.
key_contributions: Introduces MoHallBench, a benchmark focused on motion hallucination in VideoLLMs.
Covers three hallucination sources: co-occurrence priors, sequential inference, and similarity confusion.
Provides 11,306 video clips and 40,493 QA pairs across binary-choice, multiple-choice, and generative evaluation settings.
Proposes a bi-directional questioning protocol with bias-aware metrics to reduce affirmation bias.
Shows that strong action recognition and hallucination resistance can be decoupled, with sequential inference being the most severe failure mode.
why_it_matters: For Vision AI and multimodal assistants, this benchmark highlights a concrete reliability issue: models may sound confident while inventing motions not supported by the video. That matters for downstream systems that need faithful temporal grounding, especially in embodied or safety-sensitive settings.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
  A["MoHallBench"] --> B["11,306 clips"]
  A --> C["40,493 QA pairs"]
  A --> D["Motion hallucination"]
  D --> E["Co-occurrence priors"]
  D --> F["Sequential inference"]
  D --> G["Similarity confusion"]
  A --> H["Test 10 VideoLLMs"]
  H --> I["Action skill != low hallucination"]

title: ROSA: A Robotics Foundation Model Serving System for Robot Factories
tldr: ROSA is a robotics foundation model serving system that shifts robot inference from per-robot edge GPUs to a shared server GPU pool, boosting factory productivity by up to 12.06x.
abstract: ROSA is a serving system for robotics foundation models designed for robot factories, where many robots share server-class GPUs over the network. Unlike existing systems that optimize single-model, single-robot latency, ROSA supports multi-model pipelines, robotics-aware task requirements, and failure handling. Its scheduler targets overall factory productivity under service-level objectives rather than minimizing individual inference latency.
introduction: Robotics foundation models are making general-purpose robots more viable in factory settings, but the infrastructure for serving these models has largely assumed one robot and one model running on nearby edge hardware. ROSA is proposed to better match factory-scale deployments, where many robots compete for compute and tasks may involve multiple models and reliability requirements. The system combines shared GPU-pool serving, a robotics-aware programming abstraction, and scheduling that optimizes factory-level productivity. ROSA is implemented with Ray Serve and supports backends including vLLM, PyTorch, and JAX.
key_contributions: Introduces ROSA, an RFM serving system designed specifically for robot factories.
Uses shared GPU-pool serving so a fleet of robots can access server-class GPUs over the network.
Provides a robotics-aware abstraction for multi-model pipelines, task-specific performance requirements, and failure handling.
Implements factory-objective-driven scheduling that optimizes SLO-qualified productivity instead of per-request latency.
Demonstrates up to 12.06x higher factory productivity than conventional dedicated serving systems in real-robot and synthetic evaluations.
why_it_matters: For Physical AI deployments, the work shows that robot-serving infrastructure should be optimized at the factory level, not the single-robot level. This could improve throughput, GPU utilization, and battery life while supporting more complex multi-model robotic workflows.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A["Robot Factory"] --> B["Shared GPU Servers"]
B --> C["Multi-Model Pipelines"]
C --> D["ROSA Scheduler"]
D --> E["Factory Productivity"]
D --> F["Service-Level Objectives"]
D --> G["Failure Handling"]

title: ABot-M0.5: Unified Mobility-and-Manipulation World Action Model
tldr: ABot-M0.5 is a unified world action model for mobile manipulation that improves long-horizon control by aligning temporal granularity, disentangling navigation vs. manipulation actions, and training under autoregressive conditions.
abstract: Mobile manipulation is difficult because robots must coordinate navigation and arm control over long horizons, but many existing embodied learning methods are either too reactive or poorly matched to the structure of the task. ABot-M0.5 addresses this by introducing intermediate latent actions for finer temporal modeling, a dual-level Mixture-of-Transformers to separate heterogeneous action spaces, and a dream-forcing training scheme that better matches training to autoregressive inference. The paper reports state-of-the-art results on challenging mobile and fine-grained manipulation benchmarks in both task success and control accuracy.
introduction: The paper starts from the observation that mobile manipulation requires more than reactive policies: the agent must maintain an internal world model while coordinating base motion and arm actions over extended sequences. Existing World Action Models often operate on overly coarse video chunks, entangle navigation and manipulation, and train inverse dynamics in a way that differs from how the model is used at inference time. ABot-M0.5 is proposed as a unified world action model that explicitly addresses these mismatches at three levels: temporal granularity, action-space structure, and train-test consistency. Its design uses intermediate latent actions, a dual-level transformer architecture, and dream-forcing to improve robustness during autoregressive rollout.
key_contributions: Introduces ABot-M0.5, a world action model tailored to mobile manipulation rather than generic video prediction or reactive policy learning.
Proposes intermediate latent actions to bridge video latents and robot controls, capturing finer local visual state transitions.
Designs a dual-level Mixture-of-Transformers architecture to disentangle modality representations and separate action subspaces such as base movement and arm manipulation.
Introduces dream-forcing, a training strategy that progressively exposes inverse dynamics learning to model-predicted videos to better match autoregressive inference.
Reports state-of-the-art performance on mobile and fine-grained manipulation benchmarks, especially for long-horizon success and fine-grained control accuracy.
why_it_matters: For Physical AI, the work highlights that performance gains come not just from larger models, but from matching representation and training choices to the structure of embodied control. For multi-component robot systems, its explicit separation of navigation and manipulation plus train-test alignment could help reduce rollout errors in long-horizon tasks.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A["Mobile manipulation"] --> B["Intermediate latent actions"]
B --> C["Dual-level Mixture-of-Transformers"]
C --> D["Separate nav and arm actions"]
C --> E["Dream-forcing training"]
E --> F["Autoregressive inference"]
F --> G["Better task success and control"]

title: Autonomous Scientific Discovery via Iterative Meta-Reflection
tldr: DiscoPER is an open-ended LLM-driven scientific discovery system that combines code execution, statistical testing, and meta-reflection to uncover patterns in multimodal datasets.
abstract: This paper introduces DiscoPER, an autonomous framework for scientific discovery that generates and tests hypotheses without requiring predefined research questions. Unlike prior systems that search within constrained spaces, DiscoPER dynamically writes and runs code, and every proposed discovery must pass statistical tests. It also adds a second-order reasoning loop that reviews prior discoveries to identify patterns, confounds, and gaps, steering exploration toward unexplored hypotheses. In evaluation on the multimodal iNatDisco benchmark, it recovered most known patterns and outperformed classical causal discovery and LLM-based baselines.
