introduction: The work addresses a core limitation of current autonomous science systems: they can iteratively generate hypotheses, but they usually lack open-ended exploration and a mechanism for synthesizing their own accumulated findings. DiscoPER is designed to operate without pre-specified research objectives, using an LLM to dynamically generate and execute code over datasets. To maintain scientific rigor, candidate discoveries are only accepted if they survive statistical testing. A second-order meta-reflection step periodically analyzes prior discoveries as data, helping the system detect structural patterns, confounds, and missing areas to explore next. The framework also expands search beyond tabular metadata by using tools to extract information from multimodal sources such as images.
key_contributions: Introduces DiscoPER, an autonomous LLM-based framework for open-ended scientific discovery without predefined research questions.
Adds a second-order meta-reflection mechanism that reuses prior discoveries to guide future hypothesis generation.
Requires statistical testing for every proposed discovery to improve scientific validity.
Extends discovery beyond structured metadata by using tools to process multimodal inputs such as images.
Evaluated on iNatDisco, where it recovered 8 of 9 known patterns and achieved a 72.7% hypothesis support rate, outperforming classical causal discovery and LLM-guided baselines.
why_it_matters: For Physical AI and Vision AI, this suggests a path toward systems that can autonomously mine multimodal data for scientifically meaningful patterns rather than only answering fixed questions. The meta-reflection loop is especially relevant for multi-step research agents that need to revise their own search strategy as evidence accumulates.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A["Unseeded data"] --> B["Hypothesis generation"]
B --> C["Write & run code"]
C --> D["Statistical test"]
D -->|pass| E["Accepted discovery"]
D -->|fail| B
E --> F["Meta-reflection loop"]
F --> G["Find gaps & confounds"]
G --> B

title: Domain Arithmetic: One-Shot VLA Adaptation under Environmental Shifts
tldr: DART adapts vision-language-action models to new cameras or similar robots using only one demonstration, via weight-space arithmetic plus subspace alignment.
abstract: Vision-language-action models often break when the environment changes, such as when the camera pose shifts or the robot embodiment changes slightly. The paper proposes DART, an analogy-based adaptation method that adds domain-specific information in weight space and aligns singular subspaces to filter noise. The key claim is that this enables effective one-shot adaptation, avoiding the need for multiple demonstrations per task.
introduction: The problem addressed is that VLA policies learned in one setting can degrade sharply under environmental shifts, including changes in viewpoint and transfer to a different but similar robot. Existing adaptation methods typically require multiple demonstrations for each new task, which is expensive to collect and curate. DART tackles this by using a single demonstration to extract domain-specific information and transfer it through weight vector arithmetic. To make this addition more reliable, it aligns singular subspaces of weight vectors so that noisy components are suppressed before adaptation.
key_contributions: Introduces DART (Domain ARiTmetic), an analogy-based one-shot adaptation method for VLA models.
Uses weight vector arithmetic to inject domain-specific information from a single demonstration into the target model.
Applies subspace alignment between singular components in weight vectors to reduce noise during transfer.
Reports superior performance over existing VLA adaptation methods in one-shot settings across simulated and real-world environmental shifts.
Demonstrates adaptability across both visual shifts and embodiment shifts, including camera pose changes and transfer between similar robots.
why_it_matters: For Physical AI, this reduces the data and labor needed to retarget robot policies when the sensing setup or robot platform changes. For Vision AI and Multi-Agent Systems, it highlights a lightweight representation-level adaptation strategy that avoids full retraining or multi-demo fine-tuning.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A["Pretrained VLA"] --> B["One-shot demo"]
C["Domain shift"] --> D["DART"]
B --> D
D --> E["Add domain info in weight space"]
D --> F["Align singular subspaces"]
E --> G["Noise filtering"]
F --> G
G --> H["Adapted VLA"]

title: TurboServe: Serving Streaming Video Generation Efficiently and Economically
tldr: TurboServe is a serving system for streaming video generation that jointly schedules sessions and GPUs to cut both tail latency and GPU cost.
abstract: Streaming video generation behaves differently from offline generation and standard LLM serving because long-lived user sessions must keep state across active and idle periods while producing video chunk by chunk under tight latency constraints. TurboServe addresses this with an online scheduling framework that jointly manages session placement and GPU provisioning. The system uses migration-aware rebalancing and load-driven autoscaling, plus runtime mechanisms for batching, suspension/resumption, and GPU-to-GPU migration. On production traces from Shengshu Technology, TurboServe improves worst-case per-chunk latency and reduces GPU operating cost relative to baseline serving setups.
introduction: This paper targets the emerging workload of streaming video generation, where users interact with a session over time and receive video progressively in chunks. The serving problem is harder than offline video generation because the system must preserve session state, repeatedly schedule active sessions, and meet strict per-chunk latency targets. It also faces two forms of heterogeneity: some sessions run much longer than others, and demand fluctuates sharply between bursts and idle periods. TurboServe models this as an online scheduling problem and coordinates session placement with GPU provisioning through closed-loop control.
key_contributions: Introduces TurboServe, described as the first serving system designed specifically for streaming video generation workloads.
Formulates serving as an online scheduling problem that jointly optimizes session placement and GPU provisioning.
Uses a migration-aware placement controller to rebalance sessions across GPUs and reduce maximum per-chunk latency.
Uses a load-driven autoscaling controller to adapt GPU allocation to changing demand and improve cost efficiency.
Implements runtime support including coalesced chunk processing, GPU-CPU offloading for suspension/resumption, and NCCL-based GPU-GPU migration.
why_it_matters: As video generation moves toward interactive, long-lived sessions, serving efficiency becomes a key systems bottleneck for Physical AI and Vision AI products. TurboServe shows that joint scheduling and autoscaling can materially improve both latency and operating cost on real production workloads.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A["Streaming video sessions"] --> B["Online scheduling"]
B --> C["Session placement"]
B --> D["GPU provisioning"]
C --> E["Migration-aware rebalancing"]
D --> F["Load-driven autoscaling"]
E --> G["Batching, suspend/resume, GPU migration"]
F --> G
G --> H["Lower chunk latency and cost"]

title: Valdi: Value Diffusion World Models
tldr: Valdi combines a latent diffusion world model with online MPC training, showing that a one-step diffusion model can match a deterministic baseline on CarRacing while exposing a multimodality-control trade-off.
abstract: The paper studies how to use world models for model predictive control when dynamics must be both fast and able to represent uncertainty. Diffusion models are attractive for uncertain futures, but their usual multi-step sampling is too slow for low-latency planning. Valdi addresses this by pairing online MPC training with a latent diffusion dynamics model, and in preliminary CarRacing experiments it finds that a single diffusion step at training and inference can match a deterministic MLP baseline. The results also highlight a trade-off between richer multimodal prediction and control performance.
introduction: World models are useful for planning and control, but practical MPC needs dynamics predictions that are efficient enough for online use. At the same time, many real environments have uncertain or multimodal futures that deterministic models cannot capture well. Valdi proposes Value Diffusion World Models, which integrate a latent diffusion dynamics model into an end-to-end online training setup for MPC. The key idea is to keep the diffusion process to a single step during both training and inference, aiming to preserve some uncertainty modeling while avoiding the latency of iterative sampling. In preliminary CarRacing experiments, this setup matches a deterministic MLP baseline and reveals an important tension between predictive diversity and control quality.
key_contributions: Introduces Value Diffusion World Models (Valdi), combining online MPC training with a latent diffusion dynamics model.
Uses a single diffusion step at both training and inference to reduce latency for planning.
Demonstrates preliminary results on CarRacing where Valdi matches a deterministic MLP baseline.
Shows an observed trade-off between multimodal prediction capability and control performance.
Releases code publicly at the provided GitHub repository.
why_it_matters: For Physical AI and model-based control, the work explores a path toward world models that are both uncertainty-aware and fast enough for online MPC. It suggests that diffusion-based dynamics may be viable in low-latency planning, but only with careful attention to the prediction-versus-control trade-off.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A["World model for MPC"] --> B["Latent diffusion dynamics"]
B --> C["Uncertain future rollouts"]
C --> D["Online MPC training"]
D --> E["Single-step sampling"]
E --> F["Low-latency control"]
F --> G["CarRacing: MLP-level performance"]
G --> H["Trade-off: multimodality vs control"]

title: RepoRescue: An Empirical Study of LLM Agents on Whole-Repository Compatibility Rescue
tldr: RepoRescue shows that LLM agents can sometimes recover broken legacy repositories for modern environments, but success is limited by test-editing loopholes and hard cross-file coordination cases.
abstract: This paper studies “compatibility rescue,” where an old repository that once worked now fails because its runtime or dependencies have changed. The authors introduce RepoRescue, a benchmark of 193 Python and 122 Java repositories, and evaluate several deployed LLM agent systems on their ability to diagnose failures and restore the original test suite. They add stricter evaluation modes that ignore test-file edits or block them at runtime, plus practical validation for rescued repositories. The results show that agents can recover a meaningful fraction of repositories, but performance varies widely and many successes still need further validation.
introduction: Open-source software often becomes unusable over time as runtimes and dependencies evolve, especially when maintainers are no longer active. The paper asks whether LLM agents can take a repository that historically worked and adapt it to a modern environment without direct human guidance. To study this, the authors create RepoRescue, where agents receive only the repository and a failing modern setup and must produce source changes that make the historical test suite pass again. They then evaluate multiple agent systems across Python and Java, including more stringent audits that separate genuine source repair from test manipulation.
key_contributions: Introduces RepoRescue, a benchmark for whole-repository compatibility rescue with 193 Python and 122 Java repositories.
Defines compatibility rescue as distinct from bug repair: the code was historically correct but breaks under ecosystem drift.
Evaluates five deployed agent systems on Python and three on Java, reporting both full-patch and source-only outcomes.
Adds two stronger evaluation settings: rerunning patches after removing test-file edits, and a runtime-enforced regime that blocks test edits.
Finds that agent systems are complementary: the union of systems reaches 62.7% rescue rate, 10.9 points above the best single system.
Shows that cross-file coordination is a major difficulty, with GPT-5.2 through Codex solving all 14 coordinated-change cases while Claude Code systems solve at most two.
Validates that passing tests are not sufficient by checking rescued Python repositories in realistic scenarios and through bug-hunt patches.
why_it_matters: For maintenance-heavy software ecosystems, this suggests LLM agents could help revive abandoned repositories, but only if evaluation guards against superficial fixes. The benchmark also highlights where current systems struggle most: coordinated, repository-wide changes rather than local edits.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A["Old repo"] --> B["Runtime/deps changed"]
B --> C["Compatibility failure"]
C --> D["RepoRescue benchmark"]
D --> E["LLM agents diagnose"]
E --> F["Restore test suite"]
F --> G["Stricter eval + validation"]
G --> H["Success varies; needs checking"]

title: VideoSearch-R1: Iterative Video Retrieval and Reasoning via Soft Query Refinement
tldr: VideoSearch-R1 turns video retrieval into an iterative agentic process, using latent-space query refinement to improve both corpus search and downstream temporal grounding.
abstract: The paper addresses a gap in video understanding systems: retrieval is usually treated as a fixed preprocessing step, so if the first search misses the right video, later fine-grained reasoning fails. VideoSearch-R1 proposes an agentic framework that repeatedly interacts with a video search engine, refining the query in a continuous latent space instead of rewriting it as text. The system is trained with GRPO using reward signals from both retrieval and downstream reasoning tasks, and it reports state-of-the-art results on three Video Corpus Moment Retrieval datasets. The authors also find that soft query refinement uses far fewer generated tokens than explicit text-based refinement.
introduction: As video corpora grow larger and tasks become more complex, systems need to do two things well: retrieve relevant videos from a large corpus and then reason precisely within those videos, such as locating moments in time. Existing pipelines often separate these stages, which makes them brittle when retrieval fails early. VideoSearch-R1 tackles this by framing video search as a multi-turn agentic process that can refine its own query after seeing retrieval results. Its core mechanism, Soft Query Refinement, adjusts query tokens in latent space for finer, more efficient updates than discrete rewriting, and the whole process is optimized with GRPO using task-level rewards from retrieval and grounding outcomes.
key_contributions: Introduces VideoSearch-R1, an agentic framework for iterative video retrieval and reasoning via multi-turn interaction with a video search engine.
Proposes Soft Query Refinement (SQR), which refines queries in continuous latent space rather than discrete text space.
Trains SQR and the reasoning policy with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) using task-level rewards from retrieval and downstream tasks.
Reports state-of-the-art performance on three Video Corpus Moment Retrieval datasets.
Shows that SQR produces effective refinements with significantly fewer generated tokens than explicit text-based query rewriting.
why_it_matters: This is relevant for vision AI and physical-world video understanding because retrieval quality directly affects downstream temporal grounding and event localization. The iterative agentic setup is also a useful pattern for multi-step systems that must search, revise, and reason under uncertainty.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A["User query"] --> B["Soft query state"]
B --> C["Video search engine"]
C --> D["Retrieved clips"]
D --> E["Downstream reasoning"]
E --> F["Reward signals"]
F --> B
G["GRPO training"] --> B

tldr: BioInsight is a multi-agent biomedical system that turns protein-disease evidence into provenance-preserving interactive interfaces instead of static text reports.
abstract: BioInsight addresses a common limitation of AI-generated biomedical analyses: static reports are hard to inspect, question, and refine during research. The system takes a disease name, a protein association table, and optional cohort metadata, then produces structured intermediate outputs such as ranked pathways, literature evidence packets, reasoning notes, citation-grounded reports, dashboard schemas, and rendered interactive interfaces. The authors evaluate it on biomedical QA, protein-function reasoning, and end-to-end evidence synthesis, reporting best results and arguing for a shift toward interactive, evidence-centered biomedical AI.
introduction: Biomedical researchers increasingly rely on AI to interpret protein-level signals, but the resulting reports often do not support the kinds of iterative decision-making needed in practice. BioInsight reframes this task as multi-agent orchestration over typed intermediate artifacts, separating evidence retrieval from mechanistic reasoning and grounding outputs in deterministic citation handling. The same structured evidence used for reporting is also converted into an interactive interface, enabling users to inspect evidence, uncertainty, and mechanisms more directly. The work targets disease-specific biomedical discovery workflows and emphasizes provenance-preserving outputs rather than text-only summaries.
key_contributions: Introduces BioInsight, a multi-agent system for interactive biomedical knowledge discovery from disease, protein association, and cohort inputs.
Uses typed intermediate artifacts including ranked pathways, literature evidence packets, protein-level reasoning notes, citation-grounded reports, dashboard schemas, and rendered interfaces.
Separates evidence retrieval from mechanistic reasoning, with deterministic components for citation normalization.
Converts structured evidence into an interactive interface, not just a static report.
Reports best results on biomedical QA, challenging protein-function reasoning, and end-to-end biomedical evidence synthesis.
why_it_matters: For biomedical AI, the key advance is moving from one-shot report generation to interactive, provenance-aware evidence exploration. This better matches how researchers validate hypotheses, compare mechanisms, and decide what to trust.

flowchart TD

B --> D["Gather literature evidence"]
B --> E["Reasoning notes"]

D --> F
E --> F
F --> G["Interactive dashboard / UI"]

tldr: AtomiMed is a hierarchical, clinical-aware evaluation framework for medical report generation that checks atomic facts rather than relying on n-gram overlap.
abstract: The paper argues that standard metrics for medical report generation miss clinically important factual errors because they focus on surface text similarity. AtomiMed addresses this by breaking reports into a hierarchy of atomic clinical facts, including disease entities and descriptive attributes such as location, morphology, and severity. It then uses an agentic cross-verification process between ground-truth and generated reports to simulate multi-radiologist review and separately assess detection and description quality. The authors also release a toolkit and benchmark to support standardized evaluation across multiple imaging modalities.
introduction: Medical report generation needs evaluation methods that reflect clinical correctness, not just textual similarity. The authors propose AtomiMed, a universal, modality-agnostic framework that decomposes reports into Atomic Clinical Facts organized at disease and attribute levels. A cross-verification loop compares predicted and reference reports to check clinical consistency in a way intended to mimic peer review by radiologists. To support practical use, they introduce MRGEvalKit for automated extraction and OmniMRG-Bench spanning X-ray, CT, MRI, and Ultrasound.
key_contributions: Introduces AtomiMed, a hierarchical evaluation framework for medical report generation based on atomic clinical facts.
Separates evaluation into disease-level detection and attribute-level descriptive accuracy.
Uses an agentic cross-verification loop between reference and predicted reports to simulate multi-radiologist clinical review.
Releases MRGEvalKit for automated hierarchical extraction and standardized evaluation.
Curates OmniMRG-Bench, a multi-modal benchmark covering X-ray, CT, MRI, and Ultrasound.
why_it_matters: For medical Vision AI, this moves evaluation closer to what matters clinically: whether a report gets diagnoses and details right. A more faithful metric can help researchers better compare models and avoid rewarding fluent but unsafe hallucinated reports.

F --> G["Toolkit + benchmark"]

tldr: SpheRoPE generates 360° panoramas and videos without finetuning or test-time optimization by injecting spherical positional priors directly into pretrained diffusion transformers.
abstract: The paper proposes a zero-shot, training-free framework for panoramic generation that works by modifying positional encoding rather than retraining models. It addresses the mismatch between pretrained generative models and the strict topology of equirectangular panoramas by replacing standard rotary position embeddings with a spherical variant and adding guidance that steers geometry. The method is reported to generalize across several diffusion transformer backbones and panorama generation settings, while avoiding the latency and data demands of prior approaches.
introduction: Generating 360° panoramic images and videos is difficult because standard generative models do not naturally respect the spherical geometry and periodic boundary conditions required by equirectangular projection. Existing solutions typically either fine-tune on limited panoramic data or rely on iterative optimization at inference, both of which are costly and can reduce generality. SpheRoPE instead injects spherical priors directly into pretrained diffusion transformers at inference time, using a spherical re-parameterization of rotary position embeddings together with geometry-aware classifier-free guidance. This lets the model preserve its pretrained creative capacity while better matching the topological constraints of panoramic outputs.
key_contributions: Introduces SpheRoPE, a zero-shot and optimization-free framework for 360° panorama and video generation.
Replaces standard RoPE with spherical RoPE, using low-frequency channels as 3D Cartesian coordinates to encode the spherical manifold.
Uses harmonically quantized high-frequency channels to enforce exact periodicity required by equirectangular panoramas.
Adds Semantic Distortion classifier-free guidance to explicitly steer geometry during generation.
Demonstrates the approach across multiple backbones and modalities, including text-to-panorama with Flux.1, Flux.2, and LTX-Video, with competitive results against baselines.
why_it_matters: This is practically useful for Vision AI because it enables panoramic generation without expensive panoramic training data or slow inference-time optimization. More broadly, it shows that architectural priors can unlock new geometric capabilities in pretrained generative models with minimal modification.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A["Pretrained Diffusion Transformer"] --> B["Swap RoPE"]
B --> C["Spherical RoPE"]
C --> D["Panorama Topology"]
D --> E["Geometry Guidance"]
E --> F["Zero-shot 360 Panorama"]
F --> G["No Training / No Fine-tuning"]

title: 2026 BAIR Graduate Showcase
tldr: BAIR’s 2026 graduate showcase is a celebratory roundup of Ph.D. graduates whose work spans robotics, LLMs, vision, generative modeling, safety, HCI, and AI for science/healthcare.
abstract: This post celebrates the 2026 class of Ph.D. graduates from Berkeley AI Research (BAIR). It highlights that the cohort has contributed across a broad range of modern AI topics, including robotics and embodied intelligence, large language models and reasoning, computer vision, generative modeling, AI safety, human-AI interaction, and applications in science and healthcare. The graduates are moving on to faculty roles, postdoctoral positions, industry research, startups, and other paths.
introduction: The article is a graduation showcase rather than a technical research paper. It introduces BAIR’s 2026 Ph.D. graduates and emphasizes the breadth of their research impact across core areas of AI and machine learning. The post frames their accomplishments in terms of publications, systems with real-world impact, mentorship, and community contributions. It also notes that the graduates are heading to academia, industry, and entrepreneurship.
key_contributions: Celebrates the BAIR class of 2026 and their research accomplishments.
Highlights the cohort’s coverage of major AI areas: robotics, embodied intelligence, LLMs, reasoning, vision, generative modeling, safety, HCI, and AI for science/healthcare.
Notes that graduates have published influential research and built systems with real-world impact.
Recognizes their mentorship and contributions to the BAIR community.
Mentions diverse next steps including faculty positions, postdoctoral roles, industry research labs, and startups.
why_it_matters: While not a technical result, the showcase signals where influential work in Physical AI, Vision AI, and related areas is coming from. It also provides a snapshot of the research pipeline feeding academia, industry labs, and startups.

tldr: SWE-Interact shows that strong single-turn coding benchmarks substantially overestimate performance on realistic multi-turn software engineering workflows with evolving user requirements.
abstract: The paper introduces SWE-Interact, a benchmark for evaluating coding agents in interactive, user-driven software engineering sessions. Unlike existing SWE benchmarks that give all requirements up front, SWE-Interact uses a user simulator that begins with vague instructions and gradually reveals constraints, feedback, and revisions while inspecting the agent’s workspace. The authors report that models that do well on single-turn tasks often degrade sharply in this setting: top models solve about half of baseline tasks but only about a quarter of the corresponding interactive tasks. The results suggest that interactive intent discovery and iterative refinement are distinct capabilities from autonomous code completion.
introduction: SWE-Interact reframes software engineering evaluation around how real developers work: requirements are often incomplete at the start, clarified over multiple turns, and revised as the agent produces code. To capture this, the benchmark places coding agents in a simulated dialogue where a user progressively discloses the task, gives targeted feedback, and introduces new constraints until the full goal is reached. The setup is grounded in large-scale studies of real coding-agent interactions and is designed to test whether models can infer intent, adapt to changing requirements, and preserve prior work across turns. The authors compare frontier and open-weight models and find that strong single-turn performance does not reliably transfer to this interactive regime.
key_contributions: Introduces SWE-Interact, a multi-turn benchmark for user-driven coding sessions with progressive requirement revelation.
Uses a user simulator that starts with vague instructions and iteratively provides feedback, revisions, and additional constraints.
Shows a large performance gap between single-turn SWE benchmarks and interactive workflows: best models drop from about 50% success to about 25%.
Finds that stronger models such as Opus 4.8 and GPT 5.5 better handle ambiguity and requirement accumulation, but still exhibit over-agentic behavior, forgetting, and technical errors.
Argues that interactive goal discovery and iterative refinement form an orthogonal evaluation axis for coding agents.
why_it_matters: For Physical AI, Multi-Agent Systems, and Vision AI, many real deployments involve incomplete goals, iterative feedback, and continual adaptation rather than one-shot execution. SWE-Interact provides a more realistic measure of whether agents can sustain long-horizon collaboration with a user in the loop.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A["Vague initial task"] --> B["User simulator"]
B --> C["Gradual constraints\nfeedback revisions"]
C --> D["Agent workspace"]
D --> E["Interactive coding session"]
E --> F["Benchmark outcome"]
F --> G["Lower solve rate\nthan single-turn"]

tldr: TRIAGE improves agentic RL by adding role-typed, segment-level credit to outcome-based GRPO, reducing miscrediting of exploration and regression.
abstract: The paper addresses a weakness in standard GRPO for agentic reinforcement learning: it applies the final verifier outcome uniformly to all action tokens, which can over-penalize useful exploration and over-reward redundant or regressive actions. TRIAGE adds a semantic role axis, where a judge labels each segment as decisive progress, useful exploration, no-progress infrastructure, or regression, and these labels are converted into bounded segment-level rewards. The authors argue this role-conditioned credit is the best correction available from role labels alone and can reduce advantage estimation error. In experiments on ALFWorld, Search-QA, and WebShop, TRIAGE improves success rates over GRPO and cuts environment-facing turns on completed rollouts.
introduction: This work focuses on credit assignment in agentic reinforcement learning, where policies must choose environment-facing actions such as searching, clicking, editing, navigating, and interacting with objects. The baseline GRPO method uses only the final outcome from a verifier, spreading that signal uniformly across actions, which can distort learning in multi-step trajectories. TRIAGE introduces a structured judge that assigns each trajectory segment a semantic role, then maps those roles to fixed process rewards to better align credit with what each segment actually contributed. The method is designed to preserve the verifier outcome as the optimization target while correcting two specific failure modes of outcome-only credit: punishing useful exploration in failures and rewarding regressions in successes.
key_contributions: Introduces TRIAGE, a role-typed credit assignment framework for agentic reinforcement learning.
Uses a structured judge to label segments as decisive progress, useful exploration, no-progress infrastructure, or regression.
Defines a fixed role-conditioned mapping from labels to bounded segment-level process rewards.
Argues that role-conditioned credit is the optimal segment-level correction expressible from role labels alone, equivalent to projecting the per-segment advantage residual onto the role variable.
Shows empirical gains over GRPO, a scalar judge-derived process reward, and an outcome-supervised shared-backbone value baseline on ALFWorld, Search-QA, and WebShop.
why_it_matters: For Physical AI, web agents, and other multi-step embodied or tool-using systems, better credit assignment can make learning more sample-efficient and less brittle. TRIAGE is notable because it targets the semantics of actions, not just final success, helping agents learn from both exploration and mistakes.
mermaid:
flowchart TD

B --> C["Judge assigns roles"]
C --> D["Progress / exploration / infrastructure / regression"]
D --> E["Bounded segment rewards"]
E --> F["Role-typed credit assignment"]
F --> G["Lower advantage error"]
G --> H["Better success + fewer turns"]

tldr: Play2Perfect shows that task-agnostic dexterous play can learn reusable manipulation priors that make precise assembly dramatically more sample-efficient and enable zero-shot sim-to-real transfer.
abstract: The paper addresses the challenge of precise assembly with multi-fingered robots, where contact-rich interactions make both imitation learning and reinforcement learning difficult. The authors propose Play2Perfect, an RL framework that first pretrains a robot through diverse “play” on objects and goals to learn reusable skills like grasping, in-hand reorientation, and pose reaching, then fine-tunes on precise assembly. They systematically study which aspects of play matter most and report large gains in sample efficiency and sim-to-real performance.
introduction: Precise assembly remains difficult for dexterous robots because the relevant interactions are contact-rich, sparse-reward, and hard to collect data for directly. The paper argues that robots should first learn broad manipulation priors through task-agnostic play before specializing on assembly. Play2Perfect implements this idea with RL pretraining over diverse objects and goals, followed by finetuning that concentrates learning on the final high-precision contact steps. The study examines design choices in play pretraining, including object diversity, objective choice, trajectory diversity, and goal precision.
key_contributions: Introduces Play2Perfect, an RL pretraining-and-finetuning framework for dexterous manipulation and precise assembly.
Frames task-agnostic play as a way to learn reusable priors such as grasping, in-hand reorientation, and pose reaching.
Systematically studies key play-pretraining factors: object diversity, training objective, trajectory diversity, and goal precision.
Reports a 33x improvement in sample efficiency over training from scratch, even compared against dense, multi-stage rewards.
Demonstrates zero-shot sim-to-real transfer with 60% success on tight insertions with 0.5 mm clearance and over 50% success on multi-part assembly and screwing.
why_it_matters: For Physical AI, this suggests a practical path to mastering fine assembly by separating broad dexterous skill acquisition from task-specific precision. The results are especially relevant for robotic manipulation systems that need robust sim-to-real transfer and high success under tight geometric tolerances.

C --> F["RL fine-tune for assembly"]
D --> F
E --> F
F --> G["Precise assembly"]

title: Hierarchical Experimentalist Agents
tldr: HExA is a training-free framework that helps LLM agents learn by actively experimenting in novel physical environments, dramatically improving success on hard PHYRE-based tasks.
abstract: The paper argues that LLM agents fail in novel domains when they rely only on parametric knowledge, retrieval, or fixed search, especially in complex physical systems. To address this, the authors propose Hierarchical Experimentalist Agents (HExA), which iteratively design experiments, refine them based on outcomes, and build reusable skills from experience. They evaluate the method on Interphyre, a tool-calling benchmark built on the PHYRE 2D procedural physics environment. Results show large gains over standard agent baselines, including strong improvements for both closed and open-weight models.
introduction: Large language models are increasingly being used as agents for action and decision-making, but they often struggle when a task cannot be solved from prior knowledge alone. This is especially true in novel physical domains, where understanding general laws does not directly translate into solving specific long-horizon problems. HExA addresses this gap by enabling agents to learn through active experimentation rather than relying solely on static knowledge or offline training. The framework is training-free, works with black-box models, and does not require supervision, oracles, or offline data. The authors also introduce Interphyre to test this capability in a simulated physics setting where agents must propose interventions and verify hypotheses through API calls.
key_contributions: Introduces Hierarchical Experimentalist Agents (HExA), an in-context self-improvement framework for learning from active experimentation.
Proposes a hierarchical loop that designs experiments, refines them, and distills reusable composable skills from experience.
Introduces Interphyre, a tool-calling benchmark based on the PHYRE 2D procedural physics environment.
Shows that HExA substantially improves agent performance on hard physical reasoning tasks, e.g. raising Claude Sonnet 4.6 from 2% to up to 77% success.
Demonstrates transfer of learned skills without further experimentation, achieving 44% success on harder levels using skills learned from easier ones.
why_it_matters: This work suggests a practical path for Physical AI agents to acquire task-specific knowledge in novel environments without retraining. The idea of learning through experimentation and reusing discovered skills is relevant for long-horizon embodied reasoning and tool-using agents.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A["LLM agent"] --> B["Novel physical domain"]
B --> C["HExA"]
C --> D["Design experiments"]
D --> E["Run and observe outcomes"]
E --> F["Refine experiments"]
F --> G["Build reusable skills"]
G --> H["Interphyre / PHYRE eval"]

title: Mastering Agentic Techniques: AI Agent Reinforcement Learning
tldr: NVIDIA’s post introduces reinforcement learning as a core technique for building more capable AI agents and aligning language models, with emphasis on agentic workflows.
abstract: The post frames reinforcement learning as a central method for improving and aligning language models, especially in the context of AI assistants and newer agentic systems. It positions RL as part of a broader set of agentic techniques used to make models more effective at multi-step tasks and decision-making. The excerpt does not provide technical results, but suggests the article is an overview of RL’s role in AI agents.
introduction: This item is a developer-facing overview from NVIDIA on reinforcement learning for agentic AI. It highlights RL as important both for alignment methods such as RLHF and for newer agentic techniques that help models act over multiple steps. Based on the available text, the piece appears to introduce the motivation and landscape rather than present a new algorithm or benchmark result. The emphasis is on how RL fits into modern AI agent training and deployment.
key_contributions: Frames reinforcement learning as central to aligning and improving language models.
Connects RLHF to broader agentic AI techniques.
Positions RL as relevant to AI assistants and multi-step agent behavior.
Appears to serve as an introductory guide rather than a research paper with experimental claims.
why_it_matters: Reinforcement learning remains a key tool for making AI agents more reliable, useful, and aligned with user intent. For Physical AI and multi-agent systems, this kind of framing is relevant because agentic behavior often depends on learning from feedback across sequences of actions.

title: Does VLA Even Know the Basics? Measuring Commonsense and World Knowledge Retention in Vision-Language-Action Models
tldr: Act2Answer measures whether vision-language-action models retain commonsense and world knowledge by turning knowledge benchmarks into simple action-based tabletop selection tasks.
abstract: The paper asks how much commonsense and factual knowledge survives when pretrained vision-language models are fine-tuned into embodied vision-language-action (VLA) systems. To avoid confusing knowledge loss with low-level control failures, the authors propose Act2Answer, which converts each question into a short tabletop episode where the model answers by performing a single object-placement action among candidates. They evaluate a suite of knowledge categories and also probe internal layers to see where answer-relevant information lives in the VLA stack.
introduction: Embodied VLA models are often built by adapting strong pretrained VLMs to robotics data, but it is not clear whether they retain the broad knowledge encoded in the base model after this adaptation. Standard evaluations can be misleading because a failed answer may reflect poor control rather than missing knowledge. Act2Answer addresses this by reframing knowledge benchmarks as action-grounded tabletop tasks that require one simple placement action to indicate the answer, reducing control confounds. The paper also introduces layerwise intent probing to trace where knowledge signals are preserved or lost across the VLM backbone and action head.
key_contributions: Introduces Act2Answer, a lightweight protocol that adapts VLM knowledge benchmarks to VLA evaluation through action-based answer selection.
Curates a test suite of tabletop environments spanning diverse commonsense and world-knowledge categories.
Proposes layerwise intent probing to localize answer-relevant representations across the VLM backbone and action head.
Evaluates 7 VLA models and 9 VLM baselines, providing a systematic ranking across knowledge categories.

Finds that VLAs do relatively well on simple concepts but show larger drops on richer semantic categories compared with their source VLMs; VQA co-training is associated with better retention; answer-relevant signals peak in middle VLA layers and weaken in upper layers.
why_it_matters: For Physical AI, this clarifies whether embodied agents preserve the semantic knowledge that pretrained VLMs already have, rather than merely learning control policies. For benchmarking, Act2Answer offers a more faithful way to measure knowledge retention in VLAs without conflating reasoning with motor execution.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A["Pretrained VLM"] --> B["Fine-tune to VLA"]
B --> C["Act2Answer"]
C --> D["Tabletop episode"]
D --> E["Single placement action"]
E --> F["Answer inferred from action"]
B --> G["Layer probing"]
G --> H["Knowledge retention"]

tldr: MatMMExtract turns compound materials-science figures into a large open multimodal dataset, MatSciFig, with 391,606 panel-level image-text pairs and strong retrieval gains.
abstract: The paper tackles a core bottleneck in using materials science literature for AI: scientific figures are often compound, so a single caption refers to multiple sub-panels and cannot be reliably paired with one image. The authors propose MatMMExtract, an open-source pipeline that decomposes figures into sub-panels and uses an LLM plus a curated taxonomy to generate grounded annotations. Applied to 14,810 open-access papers, this yields MatSciFig, a large panel-level dataset with sub-captions, visualization categories, and summaries. They also release MaterialScope for panel detection and show that a retrieval baseline trained on MatSciFig substantially outperforms zero-shot CLIP.
introduction: Materials science papers contain a large amount of visual experimental knowledge, but most of it is embedded in complex multi-panel figures that are difficult to align with text at scale. To address this, the authors build MatMMExtract, an end-to-end pipeline that first localizes individual panels and then generates structured annotations grounded in a materials-science taxonomy. The pipeline is applied to a large open-access corpus, producing MatSciFig with hundreds of thousands of panel-level image-text pairs. The work is paired with a detection dataset, MaterialScope, to improve panel localization, and includes benchmarking of language models for annotation quality.
key_contributions: Introduces MatMMExtract, an open-source pipeline for decomposing compound scientific figures into sub-panels and generating grounded annotations.
Releases MatSciFig, a large-scale dataset with 391,606 panel-level image-text pairs from 180,571 figures across 14,810 open-access articles.
Annotates each panel with a sub-caption, a two-level visualization category spanning 19 classes and over 100 subtypes, and a scientific summary.
Introduces MaterialScope, a manually annotated materials-science figure detection dataset with 2,811 figures; a fine-tuned YOLO12-m reaches mAP_50 of 0.9227.

Finds that Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite offers the best cost-quality trade-off among six tested LMs, with 82% good outputs and a 4.8% hallucination rate; a retrieval baseline on MatSciFig improves R@1 by 4.4x over zero-shot CLIP.
why_it_matters: This work makes a previously inaccessible visual record in materials science usable for vision-language learning and retrieval, which is directly relevant to scientific VLMs and multimodal data curation. The open datasets and pipeline lower the barrier to building domain-specific AI systems for materials discovery and analysis.

tldr: Goku introduces a 2M-pair video-editing dataset plus a benchmark and model aimed at instruction following beyond simple appearance edits, including structural and motion changes.
abstract: The paper argues that current instruction-based video editing datasets are too narrow, mostly covering single-task appearance edits and missing more complex real-world editing needs. To address this, the authors present Goku, a 2 million pair dataset spanning multi-task and structural manipulations, supported by a synthesis pipeline that breaks difficult edits into subproblems and filters data progressively for reliability. They also propose Goku-Edit, a model that uses an MLLM text encoder and a dual-branch architecture to separate structural control from appearance rendering. In addition, they introduce Goku-Bench with 1,000 human-verified test cases and 7 editing-specific metrics, and report that Goku-Edit improves instruction following by up to 8% over other open-source models on this benchmark.
introduction: Instruction-based video editing has largely been limited to basic appearance changes, which makes it hard to evaluate or train models for the more complex edits needed in creative workflows. Goku expands the task scope to include multi-task and structural manipulations such as controlling subject movement, and it does so at a million-scale with 2 million high-quality instruction-aligned video editing pairs. To build this data, the authors design a synthesis pipeline that decomposes complex edits into manageable sub-tasks and applies progressive filtering to improve reliability. On top of the dataset, they explore network design choices and propose Goku-Edit, which uses an MLLM-based text encoder and a decoupled dual-branch architecture to better handle instruction understanding and structural editing.
key_contributions: Introduces Goku, a 2 million-pair instruction-aligned video editing dataset.
Extends video editing beyond appearance-only changes to multi-task and structural manipulations, including motion control.
Presents an efficient synthesis pipeline that decomposes complex edits into sub-problems and applies progressive filtering for data quality.
Proposes Goku-Edit, using an MLLM text encoder and a dual-branch design with a dedicated mask branch for structural control.
Releases Goku-Bench, a benchmark with 1,000 human-verified test cases and 7 new editing-specific metrics, where Goku-Edit improves instruction following by up to 8% over open-source baselines.
why_it_matters: This work moves instruction-based video editing closer to practical creative and production use by addressing structural edits, not just superficial appearance changes. For Vision AI, it provides both a large training resource and a more demanding benchmark for measuring whether models truly follow complex editing instructions.
mermaid:

A["Narrow video-edit data"] --> B["Goku dataset"]
B --> C["2M pairs"]
C --> D["Synthesis pipeline"]
D --> E["Decompose edits"]
D --> F["Progressive filtering"]
B --> G["Goku-Edit model"]
G --> H["MLLM text encoder + dual branch"]
H --> I["Structure control + appearance render"]

title: QVal: Cheaply Evaluating Dense Supervision Signals for Long-Horizon LLM Agents
tldr: QVal is a training-free benchmark for comparing dense supervision signals by how well they align with reference Q-values, and it reveals that simple prompting baselines often beat recent methods.
abstract: The paper studies dense supervision for long-horizon LLM agents, where outcome-only rewards are too sparse to guide intermediate actions. Instead of evaluating these signals by full downstream training runs, the authors propose QVal, a training-free testbed that directly measures whether a method’s score orders actions in a way that matches a strong reference policy’s Q-values. They instantiate this as QVal-v1.0 and benchmark 21 methods across multiple environments, model backbones, and methodological families. The main finding is that simple prompting baselines often outperform recent dense supervision methods, with results clustering strongly by method family.
introduction: Long-horizon LLM agents may execute hundreds or thousands of actions, making sparse end-of-trajectory rewards insufficient for learning good intermediate behavior. Dense supervision methods try to provide step-level guidance using signals such as confidence estimates, self-distillation, or embedding similarity, but they are usually judged only through downstream training pipelines. That evaluation style is costly and mixes together signal quality with training-specific design choices, which makes comparisons across different method families difficult. QVal addresses this by evaluating dense supervision signals directly, asking whether they are Q-aligned with the action preferences implied by a strong reference-policy’s Q-values.
key_contributions: Introduces QVal, a training-free framework for evaluating dense supervision signals directly at the state-action level.
Defines a Q-alignment criterion: a supervision signal is better if it orders actions similarly to reference-policy Q-values.
Instantiates QVal-v1.0 and benchmarks 21 dense supervision methods across four environments, seven families, six open-weight backbones, and over 1.2K experiments.
Finds that simple prompting baselines consistently outperform several recent dense supervision methods from the literature.
Shows that results cluster strongly by methodological family and remain consistent across model sizes, environments, and observation modalities.
why_it_matters: For Physical AI and long-horizon agent settings, the quality of intermediate supervision matters as much as final reward design. QVal offers a cheaper, more comparable way to test dense supervision ideas before investing in full training runs.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A["Dense supervision for long-horizon agents"] --> B["Need intermediate action evaluation"]
B --> C["QVal: training-free testbed"]
C --> D["Compare method scores to reference Q-values"]
D --> E["Check action ordering alignment"]
E --> F["Benchmark 21 methods"]
F --> G["Find: prompting baselines often win"]

title: MuSViT: A Foundation Vision Model for Sheet Music Representation
tldr: MuSViT is a ViT foundation model pre-trained on 9.7M sheet-music pages that outperforms general vision backbones on multiple music-notation tasks, especially when frozen.
abstract: The paper introduces MuSViT, a vision transformer foundation model tailored to sheet music. It is pre-trained with masked autoencoding on 9.7 million pages from IMSLP, using a two-stage curriculum that starts with synthetic typeset scores and then scales to real-world scores. Across recognition, detection, and difficulty classification tasks, MuSViT performs better than modern vision encoders under linear probing and usually improves on task-specific state-of-the-art methods after fine-tuning. The authors also report that MuSViT’s embeddings correlate with symbolic music content, unlike those from general-purpose encoders.
introduction: Sheet music is a visually structured symbolic language, but it has lacked a domain-specific foundation model comparable to those used in natural images and text. MuSViT addresses this gap by training a ViT encoder with masked autoencoding on a very large corpus of scanned and typeset music scores from IMSLP. To better adapt to the complexity of real scores, the training uses a curriculum that first warms up on synthetic typeset data before moving to the full corpus. The model is then evaluated on full-page and staff-level score recognition, symbol detection, and difficulty classification in both frozen-encoder and fine-tuning settings.
key_contributions: Introduces MuSViT, described as the first foundation vision model for sheet music representation.
Pre-trains a ViT encoder with masked autoencoders on 9.7 million IMSLP pages.
Uses a two-stage curriculum: synthetic typeset warm-up followed by large-scale training on real sheet-music pages.
Shows that MuSViT outperforms modern vision encoders under linear probing on four downstream tasks.
Reports that fine-tuning generally improves over task-specific state-of-the-art methods and that MuSViT embeddings encode symbolic music structure more directly than other encoders.
why_it_matters: This work provides a domain-specific visual backbone for sheet music, which could improve downstream systems for optical music recognition and related music-understanding tools. More broadly, it illustrates that generic vision backbones may miss important structured symbolic properties in specialized visual domains.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A["IMSLP sheet music pages"] --> B["Two-stage pretraining"]
B --> C["Synthetic scores"]
B --> D["Real scores"]
C --> E["Masked autoencoding"]
D --> E["Masked autoencoding"]
E --> F["MuSViT embeddings"]
F --> G["Recognition / detection / difficulty"]
F --> H["Symbolic content correlation"]

title: DataEvolver: Self-Evolving Multi-Agent Data Construction for Text-Rich Image Generation
tldr: DataEvolver turns rejected text-rich image samples into feedback for iterative, multi-agent data construction, yielding stronger downstream generation than static pipelines.
abstract: Text-rich image generation is difficult because models must generate both realistic visuals and accurate, well-aligned text. The paper argues that standard data pipelines waste information by discarding rejected samples after one filtering pass, even though those failures can reveal useful signals like OCR mistakes and semantic mismatches. DataEvolver addresses this by using a multi-agent loop where retrieval, verification, critique, and synthesis continually update the data construction policy. In experiments, this iterative approach produced more useful training data than fixed baselines and improved downstream OCR-F1 on multiple benchmarks and generators.
introduction: The work focuses on data construction for text-rich image generation, where success depends on both visual fidelity and correct textual rendering. Traditional pipelines follow a static crawl-filter-freeze process, which means rejected samples are thrown away and the underlying failure patterns may reappear in later rounds. DataEvolver reframes data construction as an evolving, feedback-driven process. It uses a Retriever to gather candidates, a Verifier to score quality and identify rejection causes, a Critic to convert round-level outcomes into semantic feedback, and a Generator to synthesize data that fills under-covered regions. The resulting feedback memory guides subsequent rounds of construction.
key_contributions: Introduces DataEvolver, a self-evolving multi-agent framework for text-rich image data construction.
Uses four roles—Retriever, Verifier, Critic, and Generator—to iteratively refine data collection and synthesis.
Treats rejected samples as useful feedback rather than discarded noise, enabling feedback-driven policy evolution.
Shows that, under matched data budgets, DataEvolver yields more useful training data than static fixed-dataset baselines.
Reports large OCR-F1 gains on TextScenesHQ and LongTextBench at the 0.75M scale on PixArt-alpha, with improvements also transferring to Show-o2.
why_it_matters: For Vision AI, this suggests that data quality can be improved by learning from failure cases instead of filtering them away, which is especially valuable for hard multimodal tasks like text-rich image generation. The multi-agent construction loop may generalize to other data-centric pipelines where rare errors and coverage gaps limit performance.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A["Text-rich image generation"] --> B["Reject samples"]
B --> C["Multi-agent loop"]
C --> D["Retrieve"]
C --> E["Verify"]
C --> F["Critique"]
C --> G["Synthesize"]
D --> H["Update data policy"]
E --> H
F --> H
G --> H
H --> I["Better training data"]
I --> J["Improved OCR-F1"]

tldr: This paper turns unposed multi-view reconstruction into object-centric 3D token groups, enabling segmentation, editing, and retrieval from a single feed-forward model.
abstract: The paper argues that 3D scenes should be represented as objects rather than as unstructured primitives like points or Gaussians. It introduces a feed-forward method that decomposes a scene into instance-structured 3D token groups from unposed multi-view images, where each group combines an instance token for object identity with anchor tokens for local geometry and appearance. These groups are decoded into 3D Gaussians and learned with differentiable rendering plus joint reconstruction and segmentation supervision, without requiring 3D annotations. The resulting representation supports class-agnostic instance segmentation, competitive novel view synthesis, instance-level editing, and efficient open-vocabulary retrieval.
introduction: This work targets the gap between how 3D scenes are typically reconstructed and how humans naturally understand them. Existing feed-forward methods usually produce dense point clouds or Gaussians, which are effective for reconstruction but do not encode object-level structure directly. The proposed approach instead learns instance-structured 3D token groups from unposed multi-view images, using a two-level factorization that separates object identity from local appearance and geometry. By making instances a first-class part of the representation, the model supports downstream tasks such as segmentation, editing, and retrieval without additional 3D supervision.
key_contributions: Introduces a feed-forward framework that decomposes scenes into instance-structured 3D token groups from unposed multi-view images.
Uses a two-level representation: an instance token for entity-level identity and anchor tokens for local geometry/appearance, decoded into 3D Gaussians.
Trains with differentiable rendering and joint reconstruction/segmentation supervision, without requiring 3D annotations.
Reports better class-agnostic instance segmentation than per-scene optimization baselines while staying competitive on novel view synthesis.
Enables instance-level scene editing and open-vocabulary 3D instance retrieval with complexity that scales with the number of instances rather than primitives.
why_it_matters: For Physical AI and Vision AI, the key advance is a representation that is inherently object-centric, not just geometry-centric. That makes 3D scenes easier to segment, edit, and query in a way that aligns with downstream embodied and interactive tasks.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A["Unposed multi-view images"] --> B["Feed-forward scene tokenizer"]
B --> C["Instance-structured 3D token groups"]
C --> D["Instance token"]
C --> E["Anchor tokens"]
D --> F["3D Gaussians"]
E --> F
F --> G["Differentiable rendering"]
G --> H["Reconstruction + segmentation supervision"]

title: SkillHone: A Harness for Continual Agent Skill Evolution Through Persistent Decision History
tldr: SkillHone is a continual skill-evolution harness for agents that preserves decision history, enabling better cross-session refinement and strong benchmark gains without a prebuilt search stack.
abstract: SkillHone is a harness for improving language-model agent skills over time by keeping persistent decision histories instead of only the final skill artifact. It couples skill revisions with evaluation-side evidence, so agents can see prior diagnoses, revisions, evidence, and outcomes when proposing new updates. The system uses role-separated subagents that execute candidate skills on practice probes with redacted reporting, helping revisions build on past rationale rather than rediscover it. The authors report gains on deep-research benchmarks and internal tool-mediated analysis tasks.
introduction: The paper addresses a practical limitation of current agent skill-evolution methods: they often improve skills in isolated runs but discard the intermediate reasoning and evaluation traces needed for future refinement. SkillHone is proposed as a harness for continual agent skill evolution that stores structured histories of diagnoses, revisions, evidence, and outcomes. It pairs this history with practice feedback from evaluation-side evidence, allowing later revisions to be informed by prior decisions. The setup uses role-separated subagents to run candidate skills on practice probes with redacted reporting, supporting cross-session improvement without losing context.
key_contributions: Introduces SkillHone, a harness for continual agent skill evolution based on persistent decision history.
Stores structured histories of diagnoses, revisions, evidence, and outcomes to preserve rationale across sessions.
Uses role-separated subagents and redacted practice probes to generate feedback and propose informed skill revisions.
Reports improved performance on deep-research benchmarks without a pre-integrated search stack, including +15.8 on GAIA and +3.2 on WebWalkerQA-EN versus the commercially backed deep-research agent.
Shows gains in internal tool-mediated analysis scenarios, improving accuracy by an average of 18.8 points across seven settings.
why_it_matters: For Physical AI, Multi-Agent Systems, and Vision AI, persistent decision traces can make agent skill updates more robust and auditable as tasks and environments change. SkillHone suggests a concrete mechanism for continual agent improvement that preserves the reasoning needed for reliable long-term adaptation.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A["Persistent decision history"] --> B["Prior diagnoses + revisions + evidence"]
B --> C["Agent proposes skill update"]
C --> D["Role-separated subagents"]
D --> E["Execute on practice probes"]
E --> F["Redacted reports"]
F --> B
B --> G["Continual skill evolution"]

tldr: RT-2 shows that co-training a vision-language model on robot trajectories and web-scale vision-language data can produce a robotic policy with better generalization and emerging semantic reasoning.
abstract: This work explores how internet-trained vision-language models can be used directly for end-to-end robot control. The authors co-fine-tune a state-of-the-art vision-language model on both robotic trajectory data and web-scale tasks like visual question answering. They represent robot actions as text tokens so actions and language can be learned in a shared format. The resulting RT-2 model reportedly improves generalization to novel objects and supports rudimentary and multi-stage semantic reasoning in robot behavior.
introduction: The paper addresses a key limitation in robotic learning: policies trained only on robot data often generalize poorly outside their training distribution. To overcome this, the authors propose vision-language-action (VLA) models that jointly learn from robot trajectories and Internet-scale vision-language data. Their recipe is deliberately simple: encode actions as text tokens and include them in the same training stream as natural language. They instantiate this approach as RT-2 and evaluate it extensively on robot control tasks, emphasizing both task performance and emergent capabilities from web pretraining.
key_contributions: Introduces a vision-language-action (VLA) framework that unifies robot actions and language tokens in a single end-to-end model.
Proposes co-fine-tuning a vision-language model on robotic trajectory data plus internet-scale vision-language tasks such as visual question answering.
Demonstrates improved robotic generalization to novel objects and commands not present in robot training data.
Reports emergent semantic and rudimentary reasoning behaviors, including selecting objects by size or relational cues.
Shows that chain-of-thought prompting can support multi-stage semantic reasoning in robot command following.
why_it_matters: For Physical AI, this suggests a practical path to transferring broad web knowledge into robot policies without separate perception, language, and control modules. For Vision AI and multi-agent settings, it highlights how shared token-based representations can enable richer instruction following and reasoning from multimodal pretraining.

tldr: Segment Anything introduces a promptable segmentation foundation model and the SA-1B dataset, scaling image masks to over 1 billion annotations for zero-shot transfer.
abstract: The paper presents a new segmentation task, model, and dataset aimed at making image segmentation broadly usable across image types and downstream tasks. By using the model in a data collection loop, the authors assembled SA-1B, the largest segmentation dataset reported, with more than 1 billion masks over 11 million licensed and privacy-respecting images. The model is trained to respond to prompts, enabling zero-shot transfer to new distributions and tasks. The reported zero-shot performance is strong and often matches or exceeds prior fully supervised methods.
introduction: Segment Anything (SA) is framed as a foundation-model effort for image segmentation: instead of training for a narrow benchmark, the goal is to build a promptable model that can generalize to many segmentation settings. To support this, the authors created a new dataset collection pipeline and used it to scale up supervision dramatically. The resulting Segment Anything Model (SAM) is designed to accept prompts and produce masks, making it flexible for diverse uses. The paper evaluates SAM on multiple tasks and reports that its zero-shot performance is often competitive with or better than existing fully supervised approaches.
key_contributions: Introduces the Segment Anything project: a new segmentation task, model, and dataset.
Builds SA-1B, containing over 1 billion masks across 11 million images, using a data collection loop.
Designs SAM to be promptable, enabling zero-shot transfer to new image distributions and tasks.
Reports strong zero-shot performance across many tasks, often competitive with or superior to prior fully supervised results.
Releases the SAM model and SA-1B dataset to encourage further computer vision foundation-model research.
why_it_matters: For Vision AI, this is a landmark step toward general-purpose segmentation models that can adapt via prompts rather than task-specific retraining. The scale and zero-shot behavior make it especially relevant for building reusable perception components in downstream robotics and physical AI systems.

D --> G["New images/tasks"]

tldr: A memory-driven LLM agent architecture can produce surprisingly believable individual and social human-like behavior in an interactive town simulation.
abstract: The paper introduces generative agents, computational agents designed to simulate believable human behavior in interactive environments. Each agent uses a large language model augmented with a natural-language memory store, reflection over past experiences, and dynamic memory retrieval for planning. In a small-town sandbox with 25 agents, the system produces emergent behaviors such as social coordination, invitation spreading, and date planning around a user-specified event. Ablations show that observation, planning, and reflection are all important for making the behavior seem believable.
introduction: This work addresses the problem of creating software agents that behave in ways people perceive as realistic and socially coherent. The authors propose an architecture that extends an LLM with a full experiential memory in natural language, plus a mechanism for synthesizing reflections and retrieving relevant memories when deciding what to do next. They deploy these agents in an interactive environment inspired by The Sims, where users can observe and interact with a small town of 25 agents. The resulting behaviors include routine daily activities as well as emergent multi-agent social interactions, suggesting a path toward more believable simulations of human behavior.
key_contributions: Introduces generative agents: LLM-based agents designed to simulate believable human behavior.
Proposes an architecture with three core components: observation, reflection, and planning using natural-language memory.
Demonstrates the agents in an interactive sandbox town environment with 25 agents.
Shows emergent social behavior, including party coordination, invitation spreading, acquaintance formation, and date planning.
Provides ablation evidence that observation, planning, and reflection each contribute to believability.
why_it_matters: For Physical AI and multi-agent systems, this paper shows how LLMs can be coupled with memory and reflection to produce more realistic, socially grounded agent behavior. That makes them useful for simulation, human interaction rehearsal, and interactive world-building applications.

title: Q&A: What is agentic AI today, and what do we want it to be?
tldr: Phillip Isola offers a grounded take on agentic AI, separating current capabilities from the more ambitious systems people hope to build.
abstract: This piece is a Q&A with computer scientist Phillip Isola about agentic AI. It aims to clarify what AI agents are today and what they might become as the field advances. The content emphasizes cutting through hype and explaining how these systems work in practice.
introduction: The item frames agentic AI as a rapidly advancing area that is easy to overstate and hard to define precisely. Phillip Isola discusses how AI agents function today, with an emphasis on separating present-day reality from future aspirations. The discussion appears oriented toward clarifying terminology and expectations rather than reporting a new technical result. It is a perspective piece intended to help readers understand where the field stands and where it may be headed.
key_contributions: Provides a conceptual explanation of how AI agents work today.
Distinguishes current agentic AI capabilities from longer-term ambitions.
Cuts through hype around agentic AI with a research-informed perspective.
Offers a forward-looking view on what the future of agentic AI might hold.
why_it_matters: Agentic AI is increasingly relevant for Physical AI and multi-agent systems, but public discussion often blurs what is real versus aspirational. A clear framing from a computer scientist helps researchers and practitioners calibrate expectations and identify meaningful next steps.

tldr: Walking in the Implicit reframes interactive video generation as rollout over a fixed scene state, separating stochastic world evolution from deterministic rendering.
abstract: The paper proposes a scene-centric alternative to camera-controlled video generation. Instead of repeatedly generating frame latents, it rolls out a fixed-length neural implicit scene (NIS) that can be rendered from arbitrary camera poses. The authors instantiate this idea in NeuWorld, where a transformer VAE learns locally anchored scene states from sparse posed frames and a diffusion transformer updates those states using future camera trajectories and geometry-aware history retrieval.
introduction: Interactive world exploration systems typically entangle scene dynamics with per-frame synthesis, which can make long-horizon generation unstable and inefficient. This work changes the rollout variable from frame latents to a compact, renderable implicit scene representation, so that evolution of the world state is handled separately from image rendering. NeuWorld implements this with a transformer VAE for learning NIS from sparse posed views and a diffusion transformer for predicting future NIS conditioned on camera paths and retrieved history. The model also reuses the VAE encoder as a unified conditioner for camera, reference-image, and history inputs, avoiding the need for heterogeneous external encoders.
key_contributions: Introduces the "Walking in the Implicit" paradigm: interactive generation over a fixed-length neural implicit scene rather than frame latents.
Defines Neural Implicit Scene (NIS) as a compact, renderable scene state that enables factorization into stochastic state transition and deterministic pose-conditioned rendering.
Proposes NeuWorld, combining a transformer VAE for learning locally anchored NIS from sparse posed frames with a diffusion transformer for future NIS evolution.
Uses geometry-aware retrieved history and future camera trajectories to condition scene-state rollout.
Trains from scratch on public posed-view data without pretrained video backbones or auxiliary 3D reconstructors, while reporting strong long-horizon consistency and favorable inference efficiency.
why_it_matters: For Physical AI and vision systems that need coherent exploration across viewpoints, separating world-state evolution from rendering is a promising way to improve long-range consistency. The approach also reduces reliance on pretrained video or 3D reconstruction modules, which may simplify deployment and research on interactive scene generation.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A["Sparse posed frames"] --> B["Transformer VAE"]
C["Future camera trajectories"] --> D["Diffusion Transformer"]
E["Geometry-aware history retrieval"] --> D
B --> F["Locally anchored scene states"]
D --> F
F --> G["Fixed-length NIS rollout"]
G --> H["Render arbitrary views"]

tldr: DreamForge-World 0.1 Preview is a low-compute, real-time controllable world model built for consumer GPUs, emphasizing interactive capability over frontier-scale simulation quality.
abstract: DreamForge-World 0.1 Preview is presented as a foundational world model for interactive real-time world simulation. It adapts an autoregressive video stack derived from LongLive 1 and Wan2.1-T2V-1.3B, adding a residual action pathway inspired by Matrix-Game models. The system is designed to run efficiently on consumer hardware, supporting live keyboard and mouse control, multimodal initialization, mid-stream reprompting, dual-view operation, and minute-scale rollouts at native 480p resolution. The authors report up to 14–15 FPS on a single RTX 4090 with a low memory footprint, while noting it is not yet memory-complete or frontier-quality.
introduction: This work targets the problem of making controllable world models practical on everyday GPUs rather than focusing only on large-scale, frontier simulators. DreamForge-World 0.1 Preview is built by adapting an existing autoregressive video generation stack and adding a residual action pathway to improve interaction and control. The system is positioned as a low-compute preview model that supports several interactive features, including keyboard/mouse input, multimodal starting conditions, and mid-stream prompt updates. The authors emphasize that the goal is broad interactive capability and cost-efficiency, not full fidelity or complete long-horizon memory.
key_contributions: Adapts the LongLive 1 autoregressive video stack, which is derived from Wan2.1-T2V-1.3B, into a controllable world-model preview.
Introduces a residual action pathway inspired by the Matrix-Game family to support interactive control.
Demonstrates real-time consumer-GPU operation, reporting up to 14–15 FPS on a single RTX 4090 at native 480p resolution.
Supports live keyboard and mouse control, multimodal initialization, mid-stream reprompting, and dual-view operation.
Shows minute-scale interactive rollouts with a low memory footprint, using open video backbones and targeted adaptation runs for cost-efficient development.
why_it_matters: For Physical AI and Vision AI, this suggests a practical path to interactive world simulation without requiring frontier-scale infrastructure. It could lower the barrier for experimentation with controllable environment models on consumer hardware.

tldr: InnerZoom matches or beats two-pass GUI grounding methods by preserving target evidence inside a single forward pass, improving accuracy while cutting latency and compute.
abstract: The paper studies GUI grounding with multimodal large language models, where the model must localize UI targets by generating coordinates. The authors find that evidence about the target region appears in intermediate decoder layers but is not well preserved until the final coordinate output, which hurts accuracy. To fix this without the extra cost of crop-and-rerun methods, they introduce InnerZoom, a single-forward framework that converts target cues into a compact evidence state and reinjects it across later layers. Experiments show strong gains across six GUI grounding benchmarks, with improved accuracy and lower latency than two-pass baselines.
introduction: GUI grounding with MLLMs is often cast as autoregressive coordinate generation, which leverages instruction following and semantic reasoning but makes precise clicking difficult. The challenge is that the model must keep region-level evidence alive while decoding coordinate tokens, and the authors’ analysis suggests this evidence emerges internally but fades before the final prediction. Prior ZoomIn-style approaches recover accuracy by rerunning the model on an external crop, but that adds latency and compute. InnerZoom addresses this by bridging evidence across layers within a single forward pass, so the model can preserve and refine target cues as coordinates are generated.
key_contributions: Diagnoses that target-region awareness appears in intermediate decoder layers but is not retained through final coordinate prediction.
Introduces InnerZoom, a single-forward cross-layer evidence bridging framework for GUI grounding.
Transforms target-related cues into a compact evidence state and reinjects it through later decoding layers to guide localization.
Reports state-of-the-art results for InnerZoom-4B on six GUI grounding benchmarks.
Shows improvements over the same SFT+RL baseline and over two-pass ZoomIn, while reducing latency and TFLOPs.
why_it_matters: For GUI agents in Physical AI and Vision AI, better grounding accuracy usually comes with higher inference cost; InnerZoom shows those tradeoffs can be improved simultaneously. A single-pass method that preserves localization evidence could make interactive UI agents faster and more practical to deploy.

G --> H["Lower latency vs crop-rerun"]
