tldr: LiveEdit is a causal, diffusion-based streaming video editor that preserves non-edited content while reaching real-time speed through multi-stage distillation and mask caching.
abstract: The paper addresses two major barriers to practical streaming video editing: keeping backgrounds and untouched regions stable over time, and reducing latency enough for interactive use. It proposes a frame-by-frame causal editing framework that distills editing ability from a strong bidirectional foundation model into a lightweight unidirectional streaming editor. To make deployment faster, the method also caches region-related computations with an AR-oriented mask cache. The authors report state-of-the-art quality among streaming baselines and inference speed of 12.66 FPS, and they introduce a benchmark for the task.
introduction: Streaming video editing is difficult because the model must both preserve most of the scene consistently across time and respond quickly enough for real-time interaction. Existing streaming video generation methods mainly target synthesis, so they do not directly satisfy the stricter preservation and region-control requirements of editing. LiveEdit tackles this with a causal, frame-by-frame diffusion editing pipeline built around a three-stage distillation process that transfers capability from a bidirectional foundation model to an efficient streaming editor. The system is further optimized with an AR-oriented mask cache that reduces redundant per-frame computation, and the paper also contributes a dedicated benchmark for streaming video editing.
key_contributions: Proposes a causal, frame-by-frame streaming video editing framework for real-time interactive scenarios.
Introduces a three-stage distillation pipeline to transfer editing capability from a bidirectional foundation model to a unidirectional streaming editor.
Adds an AR-oriented mask cache to reuse region-related computation across frames and reduce latency.
Establishes a dedicated benchmark for streaming video editing.
Reports state-of-the-art visual quality among streaming baselines and 12.66 FPS inference speed.
why_it_matters: This work moves streaming video editing closer to deployable interactive systems by addressing both temporal consistency and latency. That makes it relevant for augmented reality, real-time content creation, and other Physical AI settings where edits must track live video reliably.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A["Bidirectional foundation model"] --> B["Causal distillation"]
B --> C["Lightweight streaming editor"]
D["AR-oriented mask cache"] --> C
C --> E["Frame-by-frame editing"]
E --> F["Stable background & regions"]
E --> G["Low-latency output"]
G --> H["12.66 FPS + benchmark"]

tldr: Tailor-Bench exposes a long-tail generalization gap in visual world models, showing that they struggle as physical interactions become less conventional or outright impossible.
abstract: The paper argues that current image and video world models look strong on standard benchmarks because those benchmarks mostly contain common physical interactions. To test whether models have learned more general physical principles, the authors introduce Tailor-Bench, which focuses on irregular interactions and evaluates models across regular, unconventional, and impossible scenarios. They also compare predictive generation, where the model must infer outcomes, with descriptive generation, where the target outcome is specified. Results show performance drops sharply as scenarios become less typical, suggesting limited physical generalization and revealing failures such as incorrect state changes in image models and temporal inconsistencies in video models.
introduction: Visual world models are increasingly evaluated on their ability to generate realistic images and videos, but existing benchmarks mostly reflect frequent, ordinary interactions from human experience. This can hide weaknesses in how well models internalize physical principles and handle rare or out-of-distribution cases. The authors address this by proposing Tailor-Bench, a benchmark built to systematically probe irregular physical interactions through a set of progressively harder scenario modes. Their evaluation protocol also separates prediction from faithful realization, allowing a clearer view of model reasoning and generation behavior.
key_contributions: Introduces Tailor-Bench, a benchmark for evaluating visual world models on rare and irregular physical interactions.
Defines three scenario modes: Regular, Unconventional, and Impossible, to test increasingly difficult forms of physical reasoning.
Proposes two evaluation settings under one protocol: predictive generation and descriptive generation.
Finds a clear long-tail performance gap, with results degrading from Regular to Unconventional to Impossible scenarios.
Shows failure modes differ by modality: image models often miss correct state changes, while video models additionally exhibit temporal inconsistencies.
why_it_matters: For Physical AI and Vision AI, the benchmark highlights that strong performance on common cases does not imply robust understanding of physical affordances or constraints. This matters for building models that can generalize to rare, novel, or safety-critical interactions in the real world.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A["Standard benchmarks"] --> B["Common interactions"]
B --> C["Models seem strong"]
C --> D["Tailor-Bench"]
D --> E["Regular / unconventional / impossible"]
E --> F["Predictive vs descriptive"]
F --> G["Performance drops on rare cases"]
G --> H["Physical generalization gaps"]

tldr: Video-MME-Logical is a controlled video benchmark that isolates temporal-logical reasoning in MLLMs and shows a large human-model gap that persists even after large-scale fine-tuning.
abstract: The paper introduces Video-MME-Logical, a diagnostic benchmark for testing whether multimodal large language models can reason over changing visual evidence across video frames. Unlike existing benchmarks that mix reasoning with scene complexity or static recognition, this benchmark focuses on five temporal-logical operations: state tracking, sequential counting, temporal ordering, dynamic spatiality, and structural composition. It includes 25 fine-grained task categories with controlled temporal dependencies and supports both final-answer evaluation and intermediate-state diagnostics. Experiments show that current state-of-the-art MLLMs lag far behind humans, and supervised fine-tuning on up to 500K generated samples improves results but does not close the gap.
introduction: The work addresses a core limitation in video understanding: whether MLLMs can truly reason over evolving visual evidence rather than just recognize objects or events in isolated frames. To isolate this capability, the authors design Video-MME-Logical as a controlled benchmark with explicit temporal-logical operations and carefully generated task variations. The benchmark spans 25 categories and allows the difficulty to be systematically adjusted by changing temporal horizon and reasoning complexity. It also checks not only the final answer, but whether models can recover the intermediate reasoning trace needed to reach it.
key_contributions: Introduces Video-MME-Logical, a controlled benchmark for video temporal-logical reasoning in MLLMs.
Defines five target reasoning operations: state tracking, sequential counting, temporal ordering, dynamic spatiality, and structural composition.
Constructs 25 fine-grained task categories with controlled object states, transitions, temporal dependencies, and logical compositions.
Supports both difficulty-controlled final-answer evaluation and intermediate-state diagnostic evaluation.
Reports a substantial human-model gap on state-of-the-art MLLMs, with fine-tuning on up to 500K samples improving performance but not closing the gap.
why_it_matters: This benchmark gives researchers a cleaner way to measure whether video models can reason over time, which is important for Physical AI and embodied perception. It can help separate true temporal reasoning from shortcut performance on visually complex but logically simple video tasks.
mermaid:
flowchart TD

C --> D["25 task categories"]
D --> E["Final answer eval"]
D --> F["Intermediate-state diagnostics"]
A --> G["MLLMs vs humans"]
G --> H["SFT on 500K samples"]

tldr: ViDiHand shows that pretrained video diffusion models can be repurposed to reconstruct metric-scale 4D two-hand motion from full egocentric frames without detectors or test-time optimization.
abstract: The paper addresses 4D hand motion reconstruction from egocentric video, where image-based methods often fail under occlusion and video-based methods are limited by scarce hand-pose supervision. The authors argue that large video generative models implicitly learn the motion, occlusion reasoning, and hand-object interaction needed for this task. Based on this idea, they introduce ViDiHand, which adapts a pretrained video diffusion model using a hand-overlay rendering objective and then decodes metric-scale pose from the resulting features. The method works directly on full frames, without a detector, infiller, or test-time optimization, and reports strong performance on ARCTIC, HOT3D, and HOI4D.
introduction: Reconstructing 4D two-hand pose from egocentric video is difficult because hands are frequently occluded, interact with objects, and move rapidly across frames. Existing image-based pipelines often depend on hand detectors that break under heavy occlusion, while prior video-based approaches typically learn temporal reasoning from limited hand-pose annotations. The paper’s central hypothesis is that pretrained video diffusion models already contain richer motion and interaction priors because they are trained to synthesize coherent video at internet scale. ViDiHand adapts such a model for hand reconstruction using a hand-overlay rendering objective, then uses a decoder to recover metric-scale pose from the adapted features.
key_contributions: Introduces ViDiHand, a method for 4D two-hand pose reconstruction from egocentric video using a pretrained video diffusion model.
Adapts video diffusion representations with a hand-overlay rendering objective to specialize them for hand motion while retaining general world priors.
Uses a decoder to recover metric-scale hand pose directly from full-frame video features.
Eliminates the need for a hand detector, infiller, and test-time optimization.
Reports substantially improved performance over prior methods on ARCTIC, HOT3D, and HOI4D.
why_it_matters: This work suggests that foundation video generative models can serve as strong backbones for embodied perception tasks beyond synthesis. For physical AI and vision AI, it points to a scalable route for robust hand motion reconstruction in cluttered, in-the-wild settings.
mermaid:
flowchart TD

C --> D["ViDiHand adaptation"]
D --> E["Hand-overlay rendering objective"]
E --> F["Metric hand pose decode"]
F --> G["4D hand reconstruction"]

tldr: FLISP is a mapless UGV-UAV tunnel inspection planner that uses one LiDAR-IMU source to rapidly generate synchronized paths with 100% success and 7 ms latency in a 1.2 km hydropower tunnel.
abstract: The paper addresses the difficulty and risk of manually inspecting hydropower tunnels and proposes FLISP, a fast mapless planner for cooperative ground and aerial robots. FLISP uses a single LiDAR-IMU system mounted on the UGV to synchronize path generation for both the UGV and UAV, avoiding the overhead and instability of map-based planning. It combines platform-specific optimization methods and a hierarchical refinement step to produce kinematically feasible trajectories without state-estimation drift. In tunnel experiments, FLISP outperforms map-based baselines in both speed and reliability.
introduction: Hydropower tunnel inspection is an important infrastructure task, but manual inspection is slow, inefficient, and hazardous. This work targets collaborative inspection with a UGV and UAV in a feature-degraded tunnel environment where traditional map-based planning can be brittle or expensive. FLISP is presented as a mapless framework that relies on a single UGV-mounted LiDAR-IMU setup to generate synchronized paths for both robots. The method uses an enhanced Firefly Algorithm for UGV obstacle avoidance, a dynamic iterative optimizer for UAV motion, and a hierarchical refinement stage to maintain kinematic feasibility. The authors validate the system in a 1.2 km operational tunnel and report strong performance against Fast-LIO2 + A* and LIO-SAM + RRT* baselines.
key_contributions: Introduces FLISP, a mapless cooperative planning framework for UGV-UAV tunnel inspection.
Uses a single UGV-mounted LiDAR-IMU suite to drive synchronized path planning for both platforms.
Combines an enhanced Firefly Algorithm for UGV obstacle avoidance with a dynamic iterative optimizer for UAV flight.
Adds a hierarchical refinement strategy to preserve kinematic feasibility without state-estimation drift.
Reports 100% success rate and 7 ms latency in a 1.2 km hydropower tunnel, outperforming map-based baselines in efficiency and robustness.
why_it_matters: This is relevant for Physical AI because it demonstrates fast, robust multi-robot planning in a difficult real-world infrastructure setting with limited perceptual features. For Vision/robotics systems, it suggests that mapless, sensor-synchronized planning can reduce computational overhead and improve reliability in long, constrained environments.

G --> H["Faster, more reliable than map-based baselines"]

tldr: RaysUp is a lightweight, VFM-agnostic feature upsampling method that reconstructs high-resolution feature maps in a geometry-aware ray space, improving dense prediction accuracy while being far smaller and faster than prior work.
abstract: Pre-trained vision foundation models often produce low-resolution patch or pooled features, which limits their use in pixel-level tasks. RaysUp addresses this by upsampling features through a geometry-aware ray representation rather than standard interpolation or heavy attention-based modules. The method is designed to be task-agnostic and compatible with different VFMs, and it reconstructs features at arbitrary output resolutions. According to the paper, it achieves state-of-the-art results on dense prediction tasks with much lower parameter count and faster inference than competing methods.
introduction: The paper tackles the problem that modern vision foundation models, while semantically strong, output features that are too coarse for dense prediction tasks. Prior upsampling approaches often trade away semantic quality or require retraining and expensive model-specific designs. RaysUp proposes to lift feature reconstruction into a ray-based representation that encodes geometric structure directly. Its architecture combines a spatially decoupled guidance encoder, any-resolution cross-attention, Ray Positional Encoding using 6D Plücker ray coordinates, and a geometry-aware neighborhood attention module to preserve content and geometric consistency during upsampling.
key_contributions: Introduces RaysUp, an ultra-light, task-agnostic, and VFM-agnostic feature upsampling framework for arbitrary-resolution reconstruction.
Moves feature upsampling into a geometry-aware ray domain instead of relying on standard 2D interpolation.
Proposes a Spatially Decoupled Guidance Encoder to produce direction-aware guidance features.
Introduces Any-Resolution Cross-Attention for flexible reconstruction at different output resolutions.
Adds Ray Positional Encoding based on 6D Plücker ray coordinates and a Geometry-Aware Neighborhood Attention module for content-adaptive, geometry-consistent aggregation.
why_it_matters: Dense prediction in vision depends on preserving fine spatial detail from foundation models, and RaysUp offers a practical way to recover resolution without heavy retraining or large models. Its reported accuracy-efficiency gains make it relevant for scalable Vision AI systems that need high-resolution outputs with low compute overhead.

flowchart TD

B --> C["Ray-based upsampler"]
C --> D["Arbitrary-resolution features"]
D --> E["Dense prediction tasks"]
F["Task-agnostic, VFM-compatible"] --> C
G["Lightweight + fast"] --> C

tldr: Agentic abstention is a sequential stop-vs-act problem, and the paper shows many LLM agents fail to stop at the right time, sometimes wasting many unnecessary tool calls.
abstract: The paper studies when LLM agents should stop interacting with an environment instead of continuing to act under uncertainty. Unlike standard abstention, this is a sequential decision problem where the need to abstain may only become clear after several tool calls. Across web shopping, terminal tasks, and QA, the authors evaluate 13 agent systems and 2 scaffolds on over 28,000 tasks, finding that the main issue is often not whether agents can abstain, but whether they do so early enough. They also propose CONVOLVE, a context-engineering method that learns reusable stopping rules from full trajectories and improves timely abstention on WebShop without changing model weights.
introduction: LLM-based agents are increasingly expected to solve tasks over multiple turns using tools such as search, browsers, and terminals. However, some tasks are underspecified or impossible in the given environment, so a competent agent should recognize when further action is unlikely to help and stop. This paper formalizes that behavior as agentic abstention: a sequential decision problem where the agent may answer, abstain, or continue gathering information at each step. The authors benchmark this setting across multiple environments and agent designs, then introduce a lightweight method, CONVOLVE, to improve stopping behavior by distilling interaction trajectories into context-level rules.
key_contributions: Defines agentic abstention as a sequential stop/act/continue problem for LLM agents.
Benchmarks 13 LLM-as-agent systems and 2 agent scaffolds on more than 28,000 tasks across web shopping, terminal environments, and question answering.
Shows that many agents either fail to abstain when they should or abstain only after unnecessary interactions, especially when infeasibility is revealed only by the environment.
Finds that model scale, reasoning, and scaffolding influence abstention in different ways, and larger models can sometimes be worse at timely abstention.
Introduces CONVOLVE, a context engineering method that extracts reusable stopping rules from full trajectories and improves timely abstention on WebShop without parameter updates.
why_it_matters: For Physical AI and agentic systems, knowing when to stop is as important as knowing how to act, especially in real-world settings where tasks may be impossible or underspecified. Better abstention can reduce wasted actions, lower tool-use costs, and improve reliability in multi-step autonomy.

flowchart TD
A["LLM Agent"] --> B["Interact with environment"]
B --> C["Uncertainty grows over steps"]
C --> D["Decide: act or stop?"]
D --> E["Timely abstention"]
E --> F["Evaluate on WebShop, terminal, QA"]
F --> G["CONVOLVE"]
G --> H["Learn stopping rules from trajectories"]

tldr: ZooClaw-FashionSigLIP2 improves fashion retrieval by distilling and fine-tuning SigLIP2 on curated in-domain data, then interpolating weights with the base model to retain generalization.
abstract: The paper addresses a common problem in adapting foundation vision-language encoders: improving performance on a specific retrieval task without losing the model’s broader capabilities. The authors propose ZooClaw-FashionSigLIP2, a fashion-specialized SigLIP2-base model trained with full fine-tuning and knowledge distillation on curated in-domain data, then combined with the original base model using weight interpolation. They report that this simple recipe outperforms LoRA, larger backbones up to 1B parameters, and methods using external training data under fair evaluation.
introduction: Fashion retrieval is a demanding specialization of vision-language retrieval, where gains on the target domain often degrade a foundation model’s general-purpose behavior. This work studies that tradeoff and proposes a practical solution: full fine-tuning with knowledge distillation on curated fashion data, followed by WiseFT-style weight interpolation with the original SigLIP2 base model. The resulting model, ZooClaw-FashionSigLIP2, is evaluated against several baselines and is reported to win across all benchmarks in the authors’ suite. Beyond the model, the paper also introduces ZooClaw-Fashion, a new benchmark, and analyzes quality issues in existing public benchmarks.
key_contributions: Propose ZooClaw-FashionSigLIP2, a fashion-specialized SigLIP2-base retrieval model.
Use full fine-tuning plus knowledge distillation on curated in-domain data, then WiseFT weight interpolation with the base model.
Report stronger performance than LoRA, larger backbones up to 1B parameters, and methods using external training data under fair evaluation.
Release ZooClaw-Fashion, a new high-quality fashion retrieval benchmark.
Provide a systematic analysis of benchmark quality and public ground-truth biases, along with open-sourced model weights and evaluation artifacts.
why_it_matters: For Vision AI retrieval systems, the work suggests a straightforward way to specialize foundation encoders while preserving generalization. The benchmark and quality analysis may also help make fashion retrieval evaluation more reliable and reproducible.

tldr: A2World pretrains an action-conditioned diffusion world model on robot manipulation data and adapts it into both a simulator surrogate and a video-action policy model, showing transferable dynamics priors for robot learning.
abstract: The paper studies action-conditioned world modeling as a way to learn reusable dynamics priors for robotics. It pretrains a multi-view interactive diffusion world model, A2World, on large-scale robot manipulation data with real action annotations so it can predict how actions change visual scenes. The authors then adapt the same pretrained model in two directions: as A2World-sim for long-horizon simulator-like rollouts, and as A2World-policy for joint video-action prediction under visual and instruction conditioning. Experiments in simulation and on real robots suggest these pretrained dynamics priors help both simulator-based evaluation and policy learning.
introduction: This work focuses on learning transferable dynamics priors from action-conditioned world modeling, with the goal of improving robot learning beyond narrow appearance-based video generation. The proposed A2World model is pretrained on large-scale robot manipulation data that includes real action annotations, enabling it to learn how actions drive scene evolution across multiple views. After pretraining, the model is adapted in two ways: one as a task- or scene-specific real-world simulator for long-horizon rollout and what-if analysis, and another as a video-action joint prediction model for policy-related prediction. This dual use is meant to test whether the learned dynamics are reusable for both simulator-centric and policy-centric robotics settings.
key_contributions: Pretrains a multi-view interactive diffusion world model, A2World, on large-scale robot manipulation data with real action labels.
Demonstrates that action-conditioned world modeling can learn transferable dynamics priors beyond appearance-level video generation.
Adapts the pretrained model into A2World-sim, a task/scene-specialized simulator surrogate for long-horizon rollouts and what-if analysis.
Adapts the same pretrained weights into A2World-policy for video-action joint prediction conditioned on visual input and instructions.
Shows in simulation benchmarks and real-robot experiments that the learned priors benefit both simulator-centric and policy-centric robot learning.
why_it_matters: For Physical AI, this suggests a scalable path to learning dynamics models that can support both planning/evaluation and policy prediction from the same pretrained backbone. The idea of replacing some real-robot rollouts with world-model rollouts could reduce the cost of experimentation and speed up robot development.

tldr: ILLUME-X is a unified multimodal model for generating free-form interleaved text and images, with a training recipe and evaluation metric designed to improve data efficiency and training stability.
abstract: The paper presents ILLUME-X, a unified multimodal framework for producing interleaved sequences of text and images. The authors focus on enabling free-form generation, where the model can autonomously alternate between modalities rather than being restricted to fixed patterns. To support this, they propose an expanded data pipeline, a progressive training strategy with self-adaptive objectives for variable-length multimodal token sequences, and a new evaluation method called ILScore. The model is reported to outperform prior unified models on tasks including style transfer, image decomposition, and storytelling.
introduction: Generating both text and images in an interleaved, free-form manner is an important step toward more capable multimodal systems. The central challenge is not just producing each modality, but training a model that can alternate between them naturally while remaining stable and data-efficient. ILLUME-X addresses this by combining a specialized data pipeline with a progressive training scheme that adapts to free-length multimodal sequences. The paper also introduces ILScore to evaluate interleaved text-image outputs more comprehensively.
key_contributions: Introduces ILLUME-X, a unified multimodal paradigm for free-form interleaved text-image generation.
Proposes an expanded training data pipeline tailored to interleaved text-image generation.
Uses a progressive training strategy with self-adaptive objectives for free-length multimodal token sequences.
Introduces ILScore, an evaluation method for interleaved text-image sequences.
Reports improved performance over prior unified models on style transfer, image decomposition, and storytelling tasks.
why_it_matters: This work is relevant to Vision AI and multimodal foundation models because it targets more flexible generation beyond fixed-format captioning or image synthesis. Better interleaved generation could support richer human-AI interaction, narrative visual generation, and multimodal content creation workflows.

tldr: The paper argues that monocular depth models encode a learned choice among multiple valid depth interpretations in layered scenes, and introduces a benchmark to measure that ambiguity.
abstract: This work studies a fundamental limitation of monocular depth estimation: a single pixel can correspond to multiple geometrically valid surfaces in layered scenes such as transparent objects. The authors argue that standard depth supervision often resolves this ambiguity by annotation convention rather than scene truth, and that depth models therefore learn a preferred layer. To measure this effect, they introduce MultiDepth-3k, a sparse two-layer ordinal benchmark with a metric for multi-layer spatial relationship accuracy (ML-SRA). They report that leading depth foundation models show different layer preferences on the same RGB input, and that Laplacian Visual Prompting can change which layer a frozen model predicts.
introduction: Monocular depth estimation typically compresses a potentially layered 3D ray into a single scalar depth value, which becomes problematic in scenes with transparent or otherwise ambiguous geometry. In such cases, both a foreground surface and a background surface can be valid explanations of the observed pixel, but current models must still output one depth. The paper frames this as a question of depth-layer preference learned from data and supervision conventions. To probe this, the authors propose MultiDepth-3k, a sparse benchmark designed to evaluate two-layer ordinal depth relationships and how well models capture multi-layer spatial structure. They then test foundation models under standard RGB input and with a training-free spectral prompt transformation.
key_contributions: Introduces MultiDepth-3k (MD-3k), a sparse benchmark for evaluating two-layer ordinal depth ambiguity in layered scenes.
Defines multi-layer spatial relationship accuracy (ML-SRA) to measure how well models preserve layered geometry.
Shows that leading monocular depth foundation models exhibit different layer preferences on the same transparent-scene inputs.
Finds that Laplacian Visual Prompting (LVP), a training-free spectral input transformation, can substantially alter the reported layer for some frozen models.
Reports that the strongest RGB/LVP pair, DAv2-L, achieves 75.5% ML-SRA.
why_it_matters: For Physical AI and Vision AI, this work highlights that depth estimation is not always about recovering a single ground-truth surface, but about handling genuine geometric ambiguity. The benchmark and findings suggest models may encode complementary 3D hypotheses, motivating ambiguity-aware training and evaluation.

D --> E["MultiDepth-3k benchmark"]

F --> G["Foundation models show layer preferences"]

tldr: Delayed verification in multi-agent LLMs can destabilize belief dynamics, and the paper derives closed-form delay thresholds plus a greedy rule for placing corrector agents.
abstract: The paper studies how verifier and critic agents affect multi-agent LLM systems when verification is delayed. It models belief propagation as delayed consensus on a graph with grounded corrector nodes and shows that overly strong or overly delayed correction can cause oscillations instead of stabilizing agreement. The authors derive a stability threshold from grounded Laplacian spectral analysis, identify the most unstable case when communication and verification delays match, and report a special threshold of the inverse golden ratio for delay two. They also show how to place a limited number of correctors using a supermodular objective with a greedy approximation guarantee, and validate the oscillation prediction on five open models.
introduction: This work addresses a failure mode in multi-agent LLM systems where verifier or critic agents are not instantaneous, allowing false beliefs to spread before correction arrives. The authors model the system as delayed consensus on a graph with grounded corrector nodes, using spectral decomposition of the grounded Laplacian to analyze stability. Their analysis yields a closed-form threshold that separates stable correction from oscillatory instability, and the threshold depends on both the magnitude of verification and the delay. They also study where to place a limited number of corrector agents, showing the resulting objective is supermodular and can be optimized greedily with a (1-1/e) approximation. Experiments on five open models confirm the predicted dose-delay oscillations, while grounded factual answering behaves differently because truth acts as an absorbing boundary.
key_contributions: Formulated delayed verification in multi-agent LLMs as delayed consensus on a graph with grounded corrector nodes.
Derived a closed-form stability threshold for verification strength using grounded Laplacian spectral decomposition.
Showed that verification can destabilize belief dynamics when correction is too strong or too delayed, leading to oscillations.
Identified the most unstable regime when communication delay equals verification delay, including an inverse-golden-ratio threshold for delay two.
Proposed a supermodular corrector-placement objective with a greedy (1-1/e)-approximation rule for allocating a limited corrector budget.
why_it_matters: For multi-agent LLM systems, this suggests verifier design is not just about adding more correction: delay and placement can qualitatively change system behavior. The result is directly relevant to robust agent orchestration and to understanding when grounded verification helps versus when it can induce oscillatory belief dynamics.

flowchart TD

C --> D["Grounded Laplacian stability"]
D --> E["Oscillations if delay too strong"]
D --> F["Instability threshold"]
F --> G["Greedy corrector placement"]
G --> H["Open-model validation"]

tldr: The paper argues that post-alignment fine-tuning can revert models toward earlier training behaviors along a history-defined “reversion direction,” and shows that blocking this direction reduces harmfulness with little task cost.
abstract: This work studies how benign fine-tuning after alignment can cause models to partially recover previously suppressed behaviors, including safety degradation and latent capability re-emergence. The authors propose a geometric explanation: early training creates a dominant behavioral manifold, and later updates tend to drift back toward it through a persistent “reversion direction” (v_rev). In their experiments, representational drift aligns strongly with this direction, and intervening on it changes both the final alignment and measured harmfulness.
introduction: The paper addresses a post-alignment fragility problem: apparently harmless fine-tuning can undo earlier safety or specialization gains. To explain this, the authors propose the “gravitational interpretation of fine-tuning reversion,” in which late-stage updates are pulled back toward structures set by the dominant early training phase. They identify a history-defined direction, v_rev, and show that drift during subsequent training increasingly aligns with it. They also test causality by blocking motion along v_rev, which substantially alters the final model state and reduces harmfulness with minimal task impact.
key_contributions: Proposes the gravitational interpretation of fine-tuning reversion as a common lens for post-alignment regression phenomena.
Defines a history-dependent reversion direction, v_rev, and reports that representational drift rapidly acquires alignment with it.
Reports strong empirical alignment: cosine similarity with v_rev rises from 0.429 ± 0.052 after the first update to 0.647 ± 0.021 by step 20.
Across 24 run-step pairs, all observed alignments exceed the p99 of an isotropic activation-space null.

Shows that selectively blocking motion along v_rev changes final alignment at T=100 from 0.648 ± 0.009 to -0.211 ± 0.021 and reduces harmfulness from 19.0% ± 4.0% to 8.5% ± 1.5% with little task cost.
why_it_matters: For Physical AI, Multi-Agent Systems, and Vision AI, this suggests that post-training updates can unintentionally resurrect earlier behaviors unless reversion dynamics are understood and controlled. The work points to a concrete geometric mechanism that could inform safer fine-tuning and more robust post-alignment adaptation.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A["Aligned model"] --> B["Benign fine-tuning"]
B --> C["Reversion direction v_rev"]
A --> D["Dominant behavioral manifold"]
C --> E["Drift back to prior behaviors"]
E --> F["Safety degradation / capability return"]
C --> G["Intervention on v_rev"]
G --> H["Changed alignment and harmfulness"]

tldr: Drop-Then-Recovery finds that large language backbones in VLA models are often overprovisioned for robotic manipulation, while vision and action blocks remain more critical.
abstract: This paper asks how much of a Vision-Language-Action (VLA) model is actually needed for closed-loop robotic control. The authors study this by removing transformer blocks from pretrained VLAs and then fine-tuning the truncated models to see whether the lost capacity can be recovered. They also introduce GateProbe, a one-shot metric for ranking blocks by their impact on downstream action loss. Across several architectures and benchmarks, they report that language blocks are highly redundant for standard manipulation tasks, whereas vision and action pathways are less tolerant to removal.
introduction: Vision-Language-Action models reuse large pretrained language backbones, but robotic instructions are usually short and task-specific, raising the question of architectural redundancy. To probe this, the paper introduces Drop-Then-Recovery (DTR), which removes selected transformer blocks from a pretrained VLA model and then measures how well performance can be restored through downstream fine-tuning. A companion method, GateProbe, estimates block sensitivity in a single forward pass to help choose which blocks to drop. The authors evaluate multiple VLA architectures on manipulation benchmarks and real-robot industrial scenarios, focusing on how recoverability differs across language, vision, and action components.
key_contributions: Introduces Drop-Then-Recovery (DTR), a controlled protocol for studying redundancy in pretrained VLA models via block removal followed by fine-tuning.
Proposes GateProbe, a one-shot virtual-gate sensitivity metric that ranks transformer blocks by their contribution to downstream action loss.
Finds a strong asymmetry in recoverability: language backbones are highly redundant for standard robotic manipulation tasks, while vision and action pathways are less tolerant to removal.

Reports that on LIBERO, removing half of the LLM blocks can improve OpenVLA-OFT from 95.0% to 98.3% under the same fine-tuning budget, and that keeping only two language blocks can still recover baseline performance.
Argues that current VLA benchmarks may not strongly pressure deep language grounding or compositional instruction understanding, motivating more deliberate capacity allocation in future models.
why_it_matters: For Physical AI, this suggests that scaling language backbones may not be the best use of model capacity if the target is short-horizon robotic manipulation. It also provides a practical analysis tool for pruning and reallocating compute toward the vision and action pathways that appear more essential.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A["Pretrained VLA"] --> B["Drop blocks"]
B --> C["Truncated model"]
C --> D["Fine-tune"]
D --> E["Action recovery"]
A --> F["GateProbe ranking"]
F --> G["Block impact"]
G --> H["Redundancy finding"]

title: TerraDiT-Ω: Unified Spatial Control for Satellite Image Synthesis with Any Geospatial Primitive
tldr: TerraDiT-Ω is a satellite image generator that can condition on any common geospatial primitive—polygons, polylines, boxes, or points—using a geometry-aware attention mechanism.
abstract: The paper addresses the difficulty of controlling satellite image generation with spatially complex geographic structure. Instead of relying only on dense rasters or limited sparse prompts, TerraDiT-Ω generates imagery directly from native geospatial primitives of varying precision, making it compatible with standard GeoAI data formats. The authors introduce Geometry-Aware Local Attention to inject explicit geometric information into the generation process. Across conditioning types, the method is reported to outperform dense- and sparse-control baselines and to improve downstream tasks when used for synthetic data augmentation.
introduction: Satellite scenes differ from natural images because they are organized by explicit geometry and diverse spatial semantics, which makes control during generation especially challenging. Existing generative approaches often adapt natural-image conditioning schemes, but these either require costly dense annotations or lose compatibility with vector-based geographic representations. TerraDiT-Ω proposes a unified spatial control framework that works directly with geospatial primitives such as polygons, polylines, bounding boxes, and points. The model uses a geometry-aware attention mechanism to better incorporate these cues and supports controllable synthesis across different annotation budgets. This design is intended to fit end-to-end GeoAI workflows and enable applications like urban planning and synthetic data augmentation.
key_contributions: Introduces TerraDiT-Ω, a unified spatial control framework for satellite image synthesis from native geospatial primitives.
Supports multiple conditioning formats, including polygons, polylines, bounding boxes, and points, at different annotation budgets.
Proposes Geometry-Aware Local Attention to inject explicit geometric cues into the generation process.
Reports consistent improvements over both dense-control and sparse-control baselines across conditioning formats.

Shows that a single controllable generator can be used for synthetic data augmentation, improving downstream land-cover segmentation, object detection, road graph extraction, and scene classification.
why_it_matters: This work is useful for Physical AI and Vision AI because it brings controllable generation closer to the data structures actually used in geospatial workflows. It also suggests a practical path to cheaper synthetic data creation for remote sensing tasks without sacrificing compatibility with vector GIS annotations.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A["Geospatial primitives"] --> B["Geometry-aware local attention"]
B --> C["TerraDiT-Ω generator"]
C --> D["Satellite image synthesis"]
A --> E["Any precision control"]
E --> C
C --> F["Synthetic data augmentation"]

tldr: RocketSmith uses an LLM-orchestrated agentic pipeline to automate design-for-additive-manufacturing of high-powered rockets, with four prototypes built and flight-tested successfully.
abstract: RocketSmith is an agentic system for automating the design-for-additive-manufacturing (DFAM) workflow for high-powered rockets. It uses a large language model to coordinate software tools that check design properties like flight stability and generate parametric rocket components, while subagents and skills support iterative optimization in both zero-shot and human-in-the-loop settings. The system was used to develop four rockets with different motors and assemblies, fabricated on FDM printers, manually inspected, and flight tested. All four launched stably, two were recovered in reflyable condition, and measured apogee was about 80% of the system’s predicted value.
introduction: This work addresses the challenge of rapidly designing and validating high-powered rockets that leverage additive manufacturing. RocketSmith combines an LLM-based orchestrator with specialized tools and subagents to automate design checks, parametric component generation, and iterative flight-parameter optimization. The workflow supports both fully automated and human-in-the-loop refinement. The authors demonstrate the system by producing four distinct rocket designs, manufacturing them on FDM printers, and validating them through launch tests.
key_contributions: Introduces RocketSmith, an agentic system for automating DFAM workflows for high-powered rockets.
Uses an LLM to orchestrate tool execution for validating flight stability and generating parametric design components.
Includes subagents and skills for iterative optimization of flight parameters in zero-shot and human-in-the-loop modes.
Demonstrates the system on four rocket variants with different motor and assembly configurations.
Reports successful stable launches for all four rockets, with two recovered in reflyable condition, and measured altitude close to simulation predictions.
why_it_matters: This is a concrete example of Physical AI applied to real hardware design, where agentic workflows bridge simulation, CAD-like parameterization, fabrication, and flight validation. It suggests LLM-orchestrated systems can meaningfully assist complex engineering tasks that require both software reasoning and physical testing.

E --> F["Rocket designs"]
F --> G["FDM print + inspect"]
G --> H["Flight test\nstable launch, apogee"]

title: Dockerless: Environment-Free Program Verifier for Coding Agents
tldr: Dockerless verifies coding-agent patches without running tests, using agentic repository exploration instead of per-repo environments, and matches or beats environment-based post-training.
abstract: Program verifiers are used to filter training trajectories for supervised fine-tuning and to provide rewards for reinforcement learning in coding agents, but standard execution-based verification is expensive because it requires setting up repository-specific environments like Docker. Dockerless is an environment-free verifier that judges whether a generated patch is correct by collecting evidence through agentic exploration of the repository rather than executing the code or merely comparing against a reference patch. In evaluation, it outperforms the strongest open-source verifier by 14.3 AUC points. When used for both SFT filtering and RL reward, it enables a fully environment-free post-training pipeline that matches environment-based post-training on SWE-bench benchmarks.
introduction: This work addresses the bottleneck that execution-based verification imposes on training coding agents: every repository often needs its own environment, which adds significant setup cost and slows large-scale post-training. The proposed Dockerless verifier avoids execution entirely and instead uses agentic repository exploration to gather evidence about whether a patch is correct. It is designed to support both supervised trajectory filtering and reinforcement learning reward assignment. The paper reports that this approach can replace environment-based verification while preserving downstream performance.
key_contributions: Introduces Dockerless, an environment-free agentic patch verifier for coding agents.
Uses repository exploration evidence to assess patch correctness without executing unit tests.
Outperforms the strongest open-source verifier by 14.3 AUC points on a verifier evaluation benchmark.
Enables a fully environment-free post-training pipeline when used for both SFT filtering and RL reward.
Achieves 62.0% / 50.0% / 35.2% resolve rate on SWE-bench Verified, Multilingual, and Pro, respectively, surpassing the Qwen3.5-9B baseline by 2.4, 8.7, and 2.9 points.
why_it_matters: For coding agents, verifier cost and environment setup are major scaling bottlenecks; an environment-free verifier can substantially simplify and accelerate post-training. The result is practically important because it shows competitive performance without Docker-based execution, which could reduce infrastructure overhead for large-scale agent training.
mermaid:
flowchart TD

C --> D["Environment-free verifier"]
D --> E["Patch correct?"]
E --> F["SFT filtering"]
E --> G["RL reward"]
F --> H["Post-training pipeline"]

H --> I["SWE-bench parity"]

tldr: SAM2Matting turns a foundation video tracker into a high-fidelity image/video matting system by decoupling temporal tracking from fine-detail alpha prediction.
abstract: The paper addresses video matting, where methods must combine frame-wise tracking with extremely fine-grained boundary estimation. To avoid relying on costly, narrow video-matting datasets, the authors propose SAM2Matting, a tracker-to-matting framework that augments a foundational tracker such as SAM2 or SAM3 with a region-proposal bridge and dedicated matting heads. Trained only on images, the method reportedly achieves new state-of-the-art video matting performance, supports multiple prompt types, and generalizes well across human-centric and in-the-wild cases.
introduction: Video matting is difficult because temporal consistency and pixel-level detail require very different capabilities. Existing approaches often try to learn both together using specialized datasets, which can limit robustness and out-of-domain generalization. SAM2Matting reframes the problem by keeping the tracker responsible for temporal understanding while adding matting-specific components to recover fine details. This decoupled design is intended to preserve the strengths of foundation trackers while extending them to high-quality image and video matting.
key_contributions: Introduces SAM2Matting, a tracker-to-matting framework for generalized image and video matting.
Decouples video matting into a temporal tracking stage and a fine-detail matting stage using a region-proposal bridge plus matting heads.
Shows that a foundation tracker such as SAM2 or SAM3 can be adapted without compromising temporal consistency.
Reports new state-of-the-art performance on video matting despite training only on images.
Demonstrates support for diverse prompt types and robust generalization in both human-centric and in-the-wild scenarios.
why_it_matters: This is a strong example of reusing foundation tracking models for dense prediction tasks, which is relevant to Vision AI systems that need robust temporal behavior without expensive task-specific video data. The decoupled design may be useful anywhere precise spatial outputs must be paired with stable tracking across frames.

flowchart TD
A["Input image/video"] --> B["Tracker backbone\n(SAM2/SAM3)"]

C --> D["Matting heads"]

F["Image-only training"] --> D
F --> B

E --> H["Video matting\n+ generalization"]

tldr: HeRA aligns individual attention heads in MLLMs to vision features using a topological contrastive objective, improving vision-centric performance and reducing hallucinations.
abstract: This paper proposes Head-Wise Representation Alignment (HeRA), a representation-alignment method for multimodal large language models that operates at the level of individual attention heads rather than a fixed transformer layer. The method is motivated by the idea that useful cross-modal alignment should preserve local neighborhood structure between representations, and it uses a contrastive loss as a differentiable proxy for Mutual K-Nearest Neighbor alignment. The authors report that targeting the least aligned heads gives the biggest gains, and that the approach improves performance across multiple MLLMs and benchmarks while also reducing visual hallucinations.
introduction: Representation alignment has been used to regularize MLLMs by matching their internal states to those of an external vision encoder, but most prior methods align only a single predefined layer. This work argues that transformer internals are more fine-grained than that and that alignment should be applied at the attention-head level. HeRA therefore measures head-wise alignment using an MKNN-based topological criterion and applies a contrastive objective during multimodal training to selected heads. The selection is based on alignment score, and unexpectedly, the least aligned heads are the most beneficial to target.
key_contributions: Introduces Head-Wise Representation Alignment (HeRA), a multimodal training method that aligns individual attention heads instead of a fixed layer.
Uses a topological view of representation alignment, aiming to preserve local neighborhood structure across modalities.
Proposes a contrastive objective as a differentiable proxy for Mutual K-Nearest Neighbor (MKNN) alignment.
Selects attention heads for alignment based on their MKNN alignment score, with the surprising finding that least aligned heads yield the largest improvements.
Reports consistent gains across multiple MLLMs and 18 benchmarks, especially on vision-centric tasks, and reduced visual hallucinations.
why_it_matters: For Vision AI and MLLMs, HeRA offers a more fine-grained way to inject visual grounding than layer-level alignment, which can improve visual understanding without heavy architectural changes. Its hallucination reduction is practically important for building more reliable multimodal systems that depend less on linguistic priors.

G --> H["Fewer hallucinations"]

tldr: DOPD is a dual on-policy distillation method that routes token-level supervision between privileged teacher and student signals to avoid confusing transferable capability gaps with non-transferable information asymmetry.
abstract: The paper studies on-policy distillation, where a student is trained on its own sampled trajectories using dense token-level supervision. It argues that adding privileged information to the teacher or student can create a "privilege illusion," where the model learns from information that cannot actually be transferred to the student. DOPD addresses this by dynamically deciding, for each token, whether supervision should come from a privileged teacher or a privileged student policy based on advantage gaps and relative probabilities. Experiments on LLM and VLM settings show consistent gains over vanilla OPD and related methods, including improvements in stability, robustness, continual learning, and out-of-distribution performance.
introduction: On-policy distillation aims to transfer capability more effectively than standard distillation by supervising student-generated trajectories with token-level signals. The challenge addressed here is that privileged inputs can make supervision look informative even when it reflects information the student cannot truly reproduce, leading to what the authors call privilege illusion. This problem is especially acute because only a subset of tokens appears to carry the critical capability-bearing signal. DOPD proposes an advantage-aware dual distillation scheme that adaptively assigns token-level supervision objectives and strengths between privileged teacher and privileged student policies, with the goal of preserving credible capability transfer while mitigating misleading privileged cues.
key_contributions: Introduces the concept of privilege illusion in on-policy distillation, highlighting the risk of mixing transferable capability gaps with non-transferable information asymmetry.
Proposes DOPD, an advantage-aware dual distillation framework that dynamically routes token-level supervision between privileged teacher and privileged student policies.
Uses token-wise advantage gaps and relative probabilities to determine supervision strength, objective, and strategy for each token.
Reports consistent improvements over Vanilla OPD and other baselines in both large language model and vision-language model settings.
Provides additional evidence of better stability, robustness, continual learning, and out-of-distribution generalization.
why_it_matters: For Physical AI, Multi-Agent Systems, and Vision AI, distillation methods often need to transfer behavior under partial observability or asymmetric information. DOPD is relevant because it explicitly tackles the mismatch between what can be observed during training and what can be executed by the deployed student, which is a common issue in these settings.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A[Student samples own trajectories] --> B[Token-level supervision]
B --> C{Per-token choice}
C -->|Teacher| D[Privileged teacher signal]
C -->|Student| E[Privileged student policy]
D --> F[Advantage gap + rel. prob.]
E --> F
F --> G[DOPD distillation]
G --> H[More stable, robust gains]

tldr: Evolution Fine-Tuning turns evolutionary search traces into training data, helping LLMs learn reusable discovery skills across 371 optimization tasks instead of solving each one from scratch.
abstract: The paper asks whether the ability to iteratively improve solutions during evolutionary search can be taught to the model itself, rather than left to an external search scaffold. To do this, the authors introduce Evolution Fine-Tuning (EFT), a mid-training method that supervises LLMs on evolutionary search trajectories. They also build Finch Collection, a 156K-trajectory dataset spanning 10 domains and 371 optimization tasks, and fine-tune open-source models from 2B to 9B parameters. The resulting models generalize better to held-out tasks and can be further improved with test-time RL.
introduction: Prior work has shown that LLMs combined with evolutionary search can solve a range of optimization problems, from GPU kernel design to mathematical conjectures and combinatorial puzzles. However, those systems typically rely on task-specific scaffolds that do not transfer experience across problems, so the model does not learn the process of evolving solutions itself. EFT addresses this by converting evolutionary search trajectories into supervision during mid-training, with the goal of teaching models when and how to mutate, backtrack, and refine solutions. The authors evaluate this idea on a large collection of tasks and find improved cross-task generalization, suggesting that discovery behavior can be partially internalized in the model.
key_contributions: Introduces Evolution Fine-Tuning (EFT), a mid-training paradigm that learns from evolutionary search trajectories.
Constructs Finch Collection, a 156K-trajectory dataset covering 10 domains and 371 optimization tasks.
Fine-tunes open-source LLMs ranging from 2B to 9B parameters on this dataset.
Reports cross-task generalization on 22 held-out tasks, with EFT models outperforming base models by 10.22% on average.
Shows that EFT combined with test-time RL matches state-of-the-art on two circle-packing tasks and improves performance on the Erdős minimum-overlap problem.
why_it_matters: For Physical AI and scientific discovery systems, this work suggests a route from one-off search heuristics toward reusable model competence in iterative problem solving. That could reduce reliance on bespoke scaffolding and make general-purpose discovery agents more adaptable across domains.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A[Optimization Tasks] --> B[Finch Collection\n156K trajectories]
B --> C[Evolution Fine-Tuning\nmid-training supervision]
C --> D[LLMs learn\niterative improvement]
D --> E[Better generalization\nheld-out tasks]
E --> F[Test-time RL\nmore gains]

title: AVTok: 1D Unified Tokenization for Holistic Audio-Video Generation
tldr: AVTok proposes a unified 1D tokenizer for audio-video pairs, aiming to reduce modality gaps and improve joint audio-video generation efficiency and quality.
abstract: The paper introduces AVTok, a tokenizer for holistic audio-video generation that encodes paired audio and video into a compact one-dimensional latent representation. Unlike prior dual-branch approaches that tokenize each modality separately, AVTok uses a shared encoder-decoder with modality-specific learnable queries and a unified codebook. To address imbalance between audio and visual information, the authors use a hierarchical training strategy that gradually improves reconstruction for each modality. Experiments show strong performance on audio-video reconstruction and downstream tasks such as audio-to-video, video-to-audio, and class-conditional joint generation.
introduction: Audio-video generation requires both high-quality synthesis and tight synchronization between what is seen and what is heard. Existing methods typically rely on separate branches for audio and video, which increases compute and can leave a representation gap between modalities. AVTok addresses this by introducing a unified 1D tokenization scheme built on a dual-stream transformer with shared components and modality-specific queries. The method is trained with a hierarchical strategy to better balance heterogeneous audio and visual information, and it is evaluated both as a tokenizer and as a plug-in for downstream generation pipelines.
key_contributions: Introduces AVTok, a unified 1D tokenizer for paired audio-video data.
Uses a dual-stream transformer with shared encoder-decoder and modality-specific learnable queries.
Employs a unified codebook to encode audio-video pairs into a compact latent representation.
Proposes a hierarchical training strategy to mitigate heterogeneous information imbalance between audio and visual modalities.
Demonstrates improvements in audio-video reconstruction and downstream audio-to-video, video-to-audio, and class-conditional joint generation tasks.
why_it_matters: A unified tokenizer can simplify and potentially scale multimodal generation systems by reducing the need for separate modality-specific pipelines. For Vision AI and multimodal foundation models, this is a step toward more coherent audio-visual representations and better synchronized generation.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Audio + Video] --> B[Shared Encoder-Decoder]
    B --> C[Modality Queries]
    C --> D[Unified 1D Codebook]
    D --> E[Compact Latents]
    E --> F[Hierarchical Training]
    F --> G[Better A/V Reconstruction]
    G --> H[Joint Generation Tasks]

tldr: Orca proposes a frozen world foundation model that learns a unified latent state space from video, language, and VQA supervision, then supports text, image, and action readouts.
abstract: Orca is presented as an initial general world foundation model that learns a shared latent representation of the world from multimodal signals. Instead of predicting tokens, frames, or actions separately, it is trained around next-state prediction, with two learning modes: dense transition learning from continuous video and sparse event learning from language-described events and VQA. The model is pretrained on 125K hours of video and 160M event annotations, then evaluated with lightweight decoders for text generation, image prediction, and embodied action generation.
introduction: The paper targets the problem of learning a general-purpose world model that can support understanding, prediction, and action from multimodal data. Orca centers on next-state prediction as a unified formulation for state-transition modeling, rather than treating language, vision, and action as disconnected prediction tasks. It learns a single world latent space through two complementary paradigms: unconscious learning from dense video transitions and conscious learning from sparse semantic events. After pretraining, the backbone is frozen and only small modality-specific readouts are trained for downstream tasks.
key_contributions: Introduces Orca as an initial instantiation of a general world foundation model with a unified world latent space.
Formulates pretraining around next-state prediction rather than separate next-token, next-frame, or next-action objectives.
Uses two complementary training paradigms: dense transition learning from continuous video and sparse event learning from language-described events plus VQA supervision.
Builds a large-scale pretraining inventory with 125K hours of video and 160M event annotations.

Evaluates the frozen backbone with text generation, image prediction, and embodied action generation readouts, showing stronger latent representations improve downstream performance and outperform similar-sized specialized baselines.
why_it_matters: For Physical AI and Vision AI, this suggests a single latent world model can transfer across perception, prediction, and action without retraining the backbone. For embodied systems, the frozen-backbone plus lightweight-readout setup is attractive because it could reduce task-specific engineering while scaling with more world data.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A[Multimodal data] --> B[Shared latent world model]
B --> C[Next-state prediction]
C --> D[Dense video transitions]
C --> E[Sparse event learning]
D --> F[Pretrain: 125K h video]
E --> G[Pretrain: 160M events]
B --> H[Lightweight decoders]
H --> I[Text / image / action]

title: MemLearner: Learning to Query Context memory for Video World Models
tldr: MemLearner adds learned, adaptive context querying to video world models to improve long-horizon scene consistency, especially under occlusion and dynamic object changes.
abstract: Video world models can generate future frames from past video and actions, but they often lose memory over long rollouts, leading to inconsistent scenes. MemLearner addresses this by learning how to query context memory using query tokens that connect context frames to predicted tokens, rather than relying on fixed retrieval rules. The method reuses the video generation model’s own visual priors, and is trained with efficient strategies across annotated rendered videos and unannotated real-world videos. Experiments show stronger scene consistency and memory than prior approaches, especially in challenging occlusion and dynamic-object settings.
introduction: This paper tackles the memory problem in interactive video generation models, where maintaining coherent scene state over long horizons is difficult. Existing context retrieval methods are typically rule-based and struggle when objects are occluded or when the scene changes dynamically. MemLearner proposes a learning-based alternative that adaptively queries context memory through query tokens, allowing the model to decide what context is relevant for prediction. The approach is designed to leverage the underlying video model’s pretrained visual priors without introducing entirely new modules from scratch. The authors also introduce a long-video dataset with occlusions and dynamic objects, along with camera pose annotations, and train with a multi-dataset setup combining rendered and real-world videos.
key_contributions: Proposes MemLearner, a learning-based adaptive context query mechanism for video world models.
Uses query tokens to bridge context memory and predicted tokens for better long-range consistency.
Leverages the base video generation model’s pretrained visual priors instead of training new memory modules from scratch.
Introduces a dataset of long videos with occlusions, dynamic objects, and camera pose annotations.
Presents a multi-dataset training strategy combining annotated rendered videos with unannotated real-world videos.
why_it_matters: Long-horizon consistency is a core requirement for video world models used in embodied AI and interactive simulation. Better memory handling under occlusion and motion improves the realism and reliability of generated futures, which is important for Physical AI and Vision AI systems that must reason over time.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
  A[Past video + actions] --> B[Video world model]
  B --> C[Predict future tokens]
  D[Context memory] --> E[Query tokens]
  C --> E
  E --> F[Learned memory query]
  F --> B
  G[Rendered + real videos] --> H[Training]
  H --> F
  B --> I[Consistent future frames]

title: PruneGround: Plug-and-play Spatial Pruning for 3D Visual Grounding
tldr: PruneGround improves 3D visual grounding by pruning the search space with language-guided spatial regions and then grounding objects inside those regions with a repurposed spatial LLM.
abstract: This paper addresses 3D visual grounding, where a model must locate an object in a 3D scene from a natural-language description. The authors note that many expressions only depend on local spatial context, so reasoning over the full scene can be both ambiguous and inefficient. PruneGround combines language-guided spatial pruning, multi-view reformulation of descriptions, and a grounding model adapted from a detection-pretrained spatial LLM. The method reports state-of-the-art results on ScanRefer and nearly all evaluated Nr3D/Sr3D settings.
introduction: 3D visual grounding is challenging because models must interpret language and localize the correct object among many candidates in cluttered point-cloud scenes. The paper argues that many referring expressions do not require global scene reasoning and instead focus on a smaller spatial neighborhood around the target and nearby anchors. To exploit this, PruneGround first uses a frozen VLM to prune the scene to language-relevant regions, then reformulates complex descriptions into simpler target-anchor relations using multi-view cues, and finally grounds the object with an LLM adapted for spatial reasoning. The framework is presented as plug-and-play, aiming to reduce ambiguity and computation while improving localization accuracy.
key_contributions: Introduces PruneGround, a plug-and-play framework for 3D visual grounding.
Proposes Language-Guided Spatial Pruning (LGSP) to restrict grounding to language-relevant regions using a frozen VLM.
Introduces MultiView-Conditioned Description Reformulation (MCDR) to simplify expressions and recover missing spatial cues from multiple views.
Proposes LLM-Grounder, which adapts a detection-pretrained spatial LLM to perform language-conditioned grounding within the pruned region.
Reports state-of-the-art performance on all three ScanRefer settings and 9 out of 10 Nr3D/Sr3D settings.
why_it_matters: This work is relevant to Vision AI and embodied/physical AI because it shows how language-guided scene pruning can improve both efficiency and accuracy in spatial grounding. The plug-and-play design also makes it easier to integrate into existing 3D perception pipelines.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A[Input: 3D scene + text] --> B[Language-guided prune]
B --> C[Local spatial region]
C --> D[Multi-view reformulation]
D --> E[Detection-pretrained spatial LLM]
E --> F[Object grounding]
F --> G[Accurate target box]

tldr: BrainJanus is a unified brain-vision-language model that uses discrete brain tokens and autoregressive next-token prediction to do both encoding and decoding across modalities.
abstract: The paper tackles the problem of modeling the two-way relationship between sensory inputs and neural activity, noting that prior work usually separates brain encoding and decoding and depends on unimodal alignment. BrainJanus proposes a single framework that integrates brain, vision, and language by quantizing neural activity into discrete tokens aligned with visual and linguistic representations. It then uses an all-in-one autoregressive model to support any-to-any generation, including image/text to brain and brain to image/text. The authors report strong benchmark performance, zero-shot generalization, and interpretable biological topography.
introduction: This work addresses a gap in current neuroscience-inspired AI methods: most models treat encoding and decoding as separate tasks and do not fully exploit the brain as a multimodal integration system. BrainJanus is presented as a unified model spanning brain signals, images, and text within one shared framework. The key idea is to convert continuous neural dynamics into discrete tokens via a Unified Brain Tokenizer, placing them in a shared Omni space with visual and linguistic representations. On top of this representation, an autoregressive architecture performs next-token prediction to enable flexible cross-modal generation and translation in both directions.
key_contributions: Introduces BrainJanus, described as the first unified model integrating brain, vision, and language in one framework.
Proposes a Unified Brain Tokenizer that discretizes neural dynamics into tokens aligned with visual and linguistic representations in a shared Omni space.
Uses an all-in-one autoregressive next-token prediction architecture to support any-to-any generation, including image-to-brain, text-to-brain, brain-to-image, and brain-to-text.
Reports superior performance across diverse benchmarks.
Claims zero-shot generalization and preservation of interpretable biological topography.
why_it_matters: For Physical AI and Vision AI, the work points toward a more general multimodal foundation for relating neural activity to perception and language rather than isolated task-specific decoders. If robust, such a unified representation could help build better brain-computer interfaces and more biologically grounded multimodal models.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Brain / Image / Text] --> B[Neural tokenization]
    B --> C[Discrete brain tokens]
    C --> D[Unified AR model]
    D --> E[Any-to-any generation]
    E --> F[Brain↔Image/Text]
    D --> G[Benchmarks + zero-shot]
    D --> H[Biological topography]

title: A Modular Vision-Language-Action Robotics Framework for Indoor Environments
tldr: A modular indoor VLA system combines semantic mapping and language understanding to turn natural-language instructions into actionable robot behavior under time constraints.
abstract: The paper describes an integrated system built for the CMU Vision-Language-Action Challenge. It uses two parallel components: a perception pipeline that builds a semantic voxel map from camera observations using OwlViT embeddings, and a language pipeline that classifies user instructions with a vision-language model. If exploration exceeds a 500-second limit, the system continues with a partial map. The classified instruction is then grounded in the map’s geometric and semantic context to produce a detailed prompt for the VLM and support action selection.
introduction: This work tackles the problem of enabling an autonomous robot to understand natural-language instructions and act in indoor environments. The authors propose a modular framework that separates perception, language understanding, and navigation into coordinated subsystems. On the perception side, the robot constructs a semantic voxel map from real-time camera feeds, while on the language side it categorizes the user’s command using a vision-language model. The grounded semantic and geometric state of the environment is then used to form a prompt that drives actionable outputs, with a fallback to a partial map when exploration time runs out.
key_contributions: Proposes a modular VLA robotics architecture for indoor task execution.
Builds a semantic voxel map from real-time camera feeds using OwlViT embeddings.
Uses a vision-language model to classify natural-language user commands.
Grounds the classified instruction in geometric and semantic map context to generate actionable prompts.
Includes a time-constrained exploration strategy that proceeds with a partial map after 500 seconds.
why_it_matters: This is relevant to Physical AI because it shows a practical way to connect perception, mapping, and language understanding in an embodied agent. For Vision AI and multi-module robot systems, it highlights a structured approach to grounding instructions in scene context under real-world runtime limits.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
  A[Camera obs] --> B[Perception pipeline]
  B --> C[Semantic voxel map]
  D[User instruction] --> E[Lang pipeline]
  E --> F[Instruction class]
  C --> G[Grounding]
  F --> G
  G --> H[VLM prompt + action]
  B --> I[500s limit]
  I --> J[Partial map]
  J --> G

title: Learning to Deny: Action Denial in Multimodal Large Language Models
tldr: UCF101-AD shows that strong video MLLMs often confirm plausible actions even when the action is absent, and that causal prompting can reduce these false positives.
abstract: The paper studies a neglected capability in multimodal large language models: denying an action when contextual cues make it look plausible but the action is actually not occurring. To measure this, the authors introduce UCF101-AD, a benchmark with matched positive and negative video pairs where the negative clips keep the same people, objects, locations, and motion context, but omit the target action. Across 20 state-of-the-art MLLMs, models that score above 85% on standard action recognition fall below 50% on the denial version, showing a strong tendency to affirm rather than verify. The authors also test a causal graph formulation called CausalAct, using natural-language prompts to connect context, interaction, and motion, and find that these causal cues substantially reduce false positives.
introduction: This work addresses a gap in video understanding: most benchmarks test whether a model can recognize an action when it is present, but not whether it can correctly reject that action when the surrounding scene strongly suggests it. The authors build UCF101-AD, a large-scale dataset of paired action-presence and action-denial clips designed so that negative examples preserve the same contextual cues as their positive counterparts. They then evaluate 20 modern MLLMs and find a large drop in performance on denial cases, indicating that models often over-endorse plausible actions. To investigate mitigation, they propose CausalAct, a causal graph-style prompting approach that encourages reasoning about context, interaction, and motion, which reduces false positives.
key_contributions: Introduces UCF101-AD, a benchmark of paired action-presence and action-denial video clips.
Constructs negative examples that preserve context, objects, people, and locations while removing the defining action.
Evaluates 20 state-of-the-art MLLMs and reports a consistent failure on action-denial compared with standard action recognition.
Finds that models with over 85% accuracy on positive classes drop below 50% on denial counterparts.
Explores CausalAct, a natural-language causal graph prompting formulation that substantially reduces false positives.
why_it_matters: For Vision AI, this highlights a major blind spot: models may be good at recognition but poor at verifying whether an action truly occurs. The benchmark and causal prompting setup provide a concrete way to test and improve causal reasoning in multimodal systems.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A[Video clip] --> B{Action present?}
B -->|Positive| C[Affirm action]
B -->|Negative, same context| D[Deny action]
D --> E[UCF101-AD pairs]
E --> F[MLLMs fail: affirm bias]
F --> G[CausalAct prompts]
G --> H[Lower false positives]

title: Rethinking Foundation Model Collaboration: Enhancing Specialized Models through Proxy Task Reasoning
tldr: FAT reframes foundation-model collaboration as proxy reasoning over specialist hypotheses, and ProxySelect improves several structured prediction tasks without replacing the specialized model.
abstract: The paper argues that foundation models are often weaker than task-specific predictors at precise geometric and numerical outputs needed in embodied intelligence. Instead of using foundation models as direct regressors, the authors propose FAT, a framework that decomposes structured prediction into specialist prediction, information-space reconstruction, and bounded proxy reasoning by a foundation model. In this setup, the specialist produces valid candidate hypotheses and the foundation model performs a simpler proxy task such as selection or verification over those candidates. The approach, instantiated as ProxySelect with a vision-language model, improves performance on 2D/3D detection, trajectory prediction, and semantic segmentation while using less compute than direct foundation-model regression.
introduction: This work addresses a capability mismatch in foundation-model-based embodied systems: foundation models are strong at contextual reasoning, but structured prediction tasks often require accurate geometry, physics, and numerics that specialized models handle better. Rather than asking the foundation model to replace these specialists, the authors propose collaboration through task decomposition. FAT first has the specialist generate hypotheses in its native output space, then reconstructs these into an information space suitable for language-model reasoning, and finally uses the foundation model for a bounded proxy task like choosing or verifying candidates. The concrete instantiation, ProxySelect, uses a vision-language model to refine specialist outputs across multiple structured vision tasks.
key_contributions: Proposes FAT, a collaboration framework that treats foundation-model use as proxy reasoning rather than model replacement.
Decomposes structured prediction into specialist prediction, information-space reconstruction, and foundation-model proxy reasoning.
Introduces ProxySelect, an instantiation using a vision-language model for selection/verification over reconstructed multimodal candidates.
Reports consistent gains over specialized baselines on 2D object detection, 3D object detection, trajectory prediction, and semantic segmentation.
Shows better efficiency than direct foundation-model regression while preserving the strengths of task-specific models.
why_it_matters: For Physical AI and Vision AI, the paper suggests a practical way to integrate foundation models without sacrificing the precise structured outputs that embodied systems need. The broader implication is a collaboration pattern where specialists generate valid hypotheses and foundation models add contextual judgment, which may be more scalable than direct end-to-end regression.
mermaid:
