flowchart TD
A[Structured Prediction] --> B[Specialist Model]
B --> C[Candidate Hypotheses]
C --> D[Proxy Task]
D --> E[Foundation Model]
E --> F[Select / Verify]
F --> G[Final Output]
B --> H[Info-Space Reconstruction]
H --> D

title: AC3S: Adaptive Conditioning for 3D-Aware Synthetic Data Generation
tldr: AC3S uses adaptive ControlNet conditioning plus multi-agent VLM prompting to generate photorealistic synthetic images that better preserve 3D structure and pose consistency.
abstract: The paper addresses a common issue in diffusion-based synthetic data generation: visual prompts like edges can enforce geometry, but too much conditioning can hurt realism. AC3S tackles this by adding a self-supervised modulator that adjusts ControlNet strength on the fly, so the model stays faithful to 3D structure without over-constraining the image. It also uses a multi-agent vision-language model setup to create richer, geometry-aligned prompts, improving diversity and semantic consistency. The result is a pipeline for generating high-quality synthetic datasets with accurate 2D and 3D annotations.
introduction: Synthetic data generation is increasingly important for scaling computer vision datasets, but generating images that are both photorealistic and geometrically consistent remains difficult. Standard diffusion pipelines produce convincing images, yet when guided by structural prompts they can become over-conditioned, leading to artifacts and reduced realism. AC3S proposes adaptive conditioning to dynamically control the influence of structural guidance during generation, helping preserve both image quality and 3D alignment. In addition, the authors introduce a multi-agent vision-language prompting framework to produce detailed prompts that remain consistent with the underlying geometry. Together, these components support scalable generation of annotated synthetic data.
key_contributions: Introduces AC3S, a diffusion-based framework for 3D-aware synthetic image generation.
Proposes a self-supervised visual prompt modulator that dynamically adjusts ControlNet conditioning strength to avoid over-conditioning artifacts.
Develops a multi-agent vision-language model framework for composing detailed, 3D-aware prompts with better semantic consistency and diversity.
Enables scalable generation of synthetic datasets with accurate 2D and 3D annotations.
Reports improved image quality and downstream utility in extensive experiments.
why_it_matters: For Vision AI, this offers a practical way to generate synthetic training data that is both realistic and structurally faithful, which is crucial for tasks needing geometry-aware supervision. The multi-agent prompting component is also relevant to systems that combine LLM/VLM planning with generative models.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
  A[3D-aware synthetic data] --> B[Rich VLM prompts]
  B --> C[Diffusion + ControlNet]
  C --> D[Self-supervised modulator]
  D --> E[Adaptive conditioning]
  E --> F[Better realism + geometry]
  F --> G[2D/3D annotated dataset]

title: Agentic RAG-VLM: Affordance-Aware Retrieval-Augmented Generation with Self-Reflective Planning for Robotic Grasping
tldr: Agentic RAG-VLM combines affordance-aware retrieval, scene-graph reasoning, and self-reflective retries to substantially improve robotic grasping in cluttered, diverse scenes.
abstract: The paper targets generalizable robotic grasping in cluttered human environments, where prior VLM-based methods often match objects by visual similarity instead of physical affordances like graspable handles or fragility. The proposed Agentic RAG-VLM framework fuses retrieval-augmented generation with vision-language models and adds agentic planning to support spatial reasoning and failure recovery. It reports strong gains on a 12-task benchmark, suggesting that affordance-aware retrieval and closed-loop refinement are key for robust manipulation.
introduction: Robotic grasping in unstructured, cluttered settings is difficult because robots must choose not just visually similar objects, but physically compatible grasp strategies. This work argues that VLM-only approaches are limited because they ignore affordances, spatial relationships, and recovery from failed attempts. Agentic RAG-VLM addresses these gaps with a unified framework that retrieves grasp strategies using affordance descriptors, reasons over scene graphs to adjust grasp parameters, and iteratively retries based on a failure taxonomy. The system is evaluated across single-grasp, interactive, and long-horizon tasks to test robustness under diverse conditions.
key_contributions: Introduces Hierarchical Affordance-Aware RAG (HAA-RAG) that retrieves grasp strategies using four-dimensional affordance descriptors: type, material, fragility, and graspable region.
Adds a Scene Graph Constraint Reasoner that converts perceived spatial relations such as proximity, occlusion, and support into concrete grasp-parameter adjustments.
Proposes an Agentic Self-Reflective Pipeline with a 14-type failure taxonomy and three-level adaptive retry for closed-loop grasp refinement.
Evaluates on a 12-task benchmark with 360 trials per configuration, covering single-grasp, interactive, and long-horizon scenarios.
Reports 78.3% overall success and a 53.3 percentage-point absolute improvement over VLM-only baselines.
why_it_matters: This work is relevant to Physical AI because it shows how semantic reasoning must be coupled with physical affordances and closed-loop recovery to make grasping robust in real-world clutter. For Vision AI and multi-agent-style planning pipelines, it highlights the value of retrieval, structured scene reasoning, and self-reflection rather than one-shot prediction.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A[Cluttered scene] --> B[VLM perception]
B --> C[Affordance-aware retrieval]
C --> D[Agentic planning]
D --> E[Grasp proposal]
E --> F[Self-reflective check]
F -->|retry on fail| D
F -->|execute| G[Robust grasping]

title: Decodable Is Not Grounded: A Vision-Ablation Arbiter for VLM Spatial Reasoning
tldr: A blank-image control reveals that high probe/steering performance in VLM spatial reasoning can reflect priors or inverted use of latent features, not true visual grounding.
abstract: The paper argues that standard interpretability tools—linear probes and steering-based recovery—can make VLMs look more visually grounded than they really are. Using spatial reasoning as a case study, the authors show that a simple gray-image ablation exposes cases where a model’s apparent knowledge is actually independent of the image, or even used with the wrong sign. They propose a taxonomy with three regimes: grounded, prior, and inverted, and demonstrate that this distinction is missed by probing and steering alone.
introduction: The work studies whether latent knowledge decoded from a vision-language model is truly grounded in vision. On spatial reasoning tasks, the authors compare ordinary probe/steering results against a causal control that replaces the image with a blank gray input. This arbiter separates genuine visual dependence from vision-independent priors and from inverted representations that are decodable and steerable but deployed incorrectly. Across many VLMs and model families, the authors find systematic patterns by spatial axis and show that inversion can emerge at scale, motivating a simple control for future claims about latent knowledge and steering.
key_contributions: Introduces a one-line causal control—replacing the image with a gray blank—to test whether decoded spatial knowledge is actually grounded in vision.
Defines three grounding regimes for VLM spatial reasoning: grounded, prior, and inverted.
Shows that linear probing and steering can overstate grounding by conflating prior-based decoding with genuine visual dependence.
Reports results across 14 VLMs spanning 6 language-model families and 2B–27B parameters: horizontal is grounded, vertical is a prior, and depth is inverted in the studied models.
Demonstrates that decode-versus-deploy inversion can be corrected with either a training-free rotation or a trained low-rank edit depending on model/geometry complexity.
why_it_matters: For Physical AI and Vision AI, this work is a caution that high decodability does not guarantee that a model is using visual input causally. The proposed blank-image arbiter is a cheap sanity check for interpretability, steering, and grounding claims in multimodal models.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
  A[VLM spatial task] --> B[Probe / steer analysis]
  B --> C[Appears grounded]
  A --> D[Gray-image ablation]
  D --> E[Compare image vs gray]
  E --> F{Response changes?}
  F -->|Yes, correct sign| G[Grounded]
  F -->|No| H[Prior]
  F -->|Opposite sign| I[Inverted]

title: One Video, One World: Turning Monocular Video into Physical 4D Scenes
tldr: OVOW is a training-free pipeline that turns a single monocular video into instance-level, watertight, simulation-ready 4D mesh scenes with physical consistency.
abstract: The paper introduces OVOW, a first training-free system for reconstructing instance-level 4D mesh scenes from one monocular video. Unlike prior 4D methods that output implicit fields, Gaussian primitives, or point clouds, OVOW produces watertight meshes with separated instances and standardized interfaces suitable for physics simulation and embodied AI. It uses a four-stage pipeline: instance discovery and motion classification via a vision-language model, category-aware mesh reconstruction, iterative render-match optimization for metric scale and 6-DoF trajectories, and physics-grounded assembly to enforce support and ground contact. The authors also propose the first structured Video-to-4D benchmark and report strong performance on synthetic static and dynamic benchmarks, with much faster inference than baselines and physically stable simulations.
introduction: This work addresses the gap between high-quality 4D reconstruction and outputs that can actually be used in physical simulation or embodied AI. Existing methods may render well, but they often lack watertight topology, instance decomposition, and physically meaningful geometry. OVOW is designed to reconstruct a structured 4D scene from monocular video without training, using a sequence of modules that identify objects, reconstruct per-instance geometry, estimate motion and scale, and assemble the scene under physical constraints. The result is a mesh-based 4D representation intended to be directly usable for simulation, editing, and downstream world-model learning.
key_contributions: Introduces OVOW, a training-free system for reconstructing instance-level, simulation-ready 4D mesh scenes from a single monocular video.
Uses a four-stage pipeline: VLM-based instance discovery and motion classification, category-aware reconstruction, render-match optimization for pose/scale recovery, and physics-grounded assembly.
Models both rigid and non-rigid motion through direct vertex deformation, avoiding category-specific priors and skeleton rigging.
Establishes the first benchmark for structured Video-to-4D evaluation with metrics for geometric correctness, instance separation, and physical plausibility.

Reports that OVOW achieves the best overall layout and geometry accuracy on two synthetic benchmarks, runs one to two orders of magnitude faster than baselines on monocular video, and yields physically stable simulations.
why_it_matters: For Physical AI and embodied systems, reconstruction is only useful if the output can be simulated and edited; OVOW targets that need directly. Its structured mesh outputs and physical assembly step move 4D reconstruction closer to usable scene representations for robotics, simulation, and world-model data generation.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
  A["Monocular video"] --> B["Instance discovery"]
  B --> C["Motion class"]
  C --> D["Category-aware mesh recon"]
  D --> E["Render-match opt"]
  E --> F["Physics assembly"]
  F --> G["Watertight 4D meshes"]

title: Language-Assisted Super-Resolution from Real-World Low-Resolution Patches
tldr: LA-SR tackles real-world unpaired super-resolution by using vision-language models to enforce both semantic content preservation and perceptual quality improvement.
abstract: This work addresses the gap between synthetic super-resolution training data and real low-resolution images, which often degrade differently from hand-crafted simulations. The authors observe that a single real image can contain both high-resolution and naturally degraded low-resolution patches due to depth variation, enabling extraction of real LR patches without paired HR targets. To handle this unpaired setting, they introduce LA-SR, which uses vision-language models to project images into a semantic space and apply language-guided losses for content fidelity and image quality. The result is a framework designed to super-resolve real LR inputs more realistically than methods trained only on synthetic degradations.
introduction: Single-image super-resolution usually relies on paired HR-LR data, but obtaining such pairs from real scenes is difficult and synthetic degradations do not fully reflect real image formation. The paper leverages an observation from natural images: distant regions can behave like low-resolution patches while nearer regions appear as high-resolution patches, allowing real degraded patches to be extracted from a single image. Because these LR patches have no matching HR counterparts, the authors reformulate unpaired SR through language space using vision-language models. Their LA-SR framework uses semantic representations to connect content and quality, with separate linguistic losses encouraging both faithful reconstruction and perceptual realism.
key_contributions: Observes that real images can contain naturally degraded low-resolution patches, enabling extraction of real LR samples without synthetic degradation.
Introduces LA-SR (Language Assistant for SR), a framework for unpaired super-resolution based on vision-language alignment.
Defines two language-guided objectives: a linguistic content loss to preserve semantics and a linguistic quality loss to improve perceptual realism.
Targets the domain gap caused by training SR models only on synthetic degradations, aiming for better real-world generalization.
why_it_matters: For Vision AI, this offers a practical route to train super-resolution models on real degradations without requiring paired HR data. The language-based formulation may also be useful for other restoration problems where supervision is scarce and perceptual quality matters.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A[Real image] --> B[HR & LR patches]
B --> C[Unpaired real LR patches]
C --> D[LA-SR]
D --> E[VLM semantic space]
D --> F[Language-guided losses]
E --> G[Content fidelity]
F --> G
G --> H[Real-world SR output]

title: Bridging Video Understanding and Generation in a Unified Framework
tldr: Vega unifies video understanding and generation by using autoregressive keyframe token prediction to guide diffusion-based frame rendering.
abstract: The paper argues that video is a better testbed than images for unified multimodal modeling because it combines spatial semantics with temporal dynamics. It introduces Vega, a framework that shares a vocabulary for text and visual representations and combines autoregressive prediction with diffusion-based rendering. The autoregressive component predicts semantically meaningful tokens for keyframes, and the diffusion module uses this structured signal to generate dense, high-resolution video frames. The authors report strong results on both video generation benchmarks (VBench) and video understanding benchmarks (VideoMME).
introduction: This work addresses the gap between video understanding and video generation in unified models. The core difficulty is that understanding benefits from compact semantic representations, while generation needs rich, detailed signals that preserve appearance and temporal consistency. Vega tackles this by pairing an autoregressive model that predicts keyframe visual tokens with a diffusion renderer that expands those tokens into full video frames. The shared vocabulary is used to bridge text and visual modalities, aiming to support both tasks within one framework.
key_contributions: Introduces Vega, a unified framework for both video understanding and video generation.
Uses a shared vocabulary to jointly model text and visual representations.
Combines autoregressive prediction for semantically meaningful keyframe tokens with diffusion-based rendering for dense video synthesis.
Positions keyframe token prediction as a structured intermediate representation that guides high-resolution video generation.
Reports strong performance on both generation benchmarks (VBench) and understanding benchmarks (VideoMME).
why_it_matters: For Vision AI, this is a concrete step toward a single model that can both interpret and synthesize video, rather than optimizing those capabilities separately. Such unified video representations could also be useful in physical-world systems that need both scene understanding and faithful motion generation.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A[Video input / text] --> B[Shared vocabulary]
B --> C[Autoregressive keyframe tokens]
C --> D[Diffusion renderer]
D --> E[Dense video frames]
C --> F[Video understanding]
E --> G[Video generation]
F --> H[Strong benchmark results]
G --> H

title: Surprise as a Signal for Plasticity and Metacognition
tldr: A surprise signal from a frozen encoder’s latent space can both control when to write memories and improve metacognitive behavior in vision-language models, with strong proof-of-concept gains in continual learning and novelty detection.
abstract: The paper studies one core mechanism in two settings: a prediction-error, or surprise, signal computed by a small predictor on top of a frozen encoder. In the first system, this signal gates when a non-parametric episodic memory stores a new concept, and an offline replay phase later consolidates those traces into a slower linear readout. In the second system, the same kind of surprise signal in a shared text-image space modulates a vision-language model’s behavior so it can answer confidently for known concepts, hedge for partially familiar ones, or refuse and ask for an explanation when encountering something novel.
introduction: The work asks whether a simple surprise estimate can serve as a general-purpose signal for both plasticity and metacognition. To test this, the author builds two proof-of-concept systems around a small predictor operating on frozen representations. For continual learning, surprise controls episodic memory writes and a replay-based consolidation phase improves retention on a 1000-class ImageNet stream with DINOv2 or I-JEPA backbones. For multimodal interaction, the same principle is used to detect novelty in a vision-language setting and to guide whether the model should answer, hedge, or defer while learning new concepts from a single user utterance.
key_contributions: Introduces a shared surprise signal, computed from frozen latent features, as a gate for plasticity and a substrate for metacognitive behavior.
Shows that in continual learning on 1000 ImageNet classes, offline consolidation improves retention of old classes by 17.7 points for DINOv2 and 51.3 points for I-JEPA in single-seed runs.
Finds that replaying only a recent window is worse than doing no replay at all in this setup.
Demonstrates few-shot performance of 91.6% on 5-way 1-shot mini-ImageNet, while noting that the 500-way regime is much harder.

In a vision-language system, the external surprise detector distinguishes known from novel concepts with AUROC 0.966, outperforming the model’s own verbalized confidence, and after consolidation the system recalls 99.2% of fifty taught facts while the base model recalls none.
why_it_matters: For Physical AI, Multi-Agent Systems, and Vision AI, this suggests a lightweight mechanism for deciding when to adapt, store, or defer based on novelty rather than relying only on model-internal confidence. It also points to a practical route for memory-augmented multimodal systems that can learn from sparse interaction while avoiding overconfident answers on unfamiliar inputs.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
  A[Input] --> B[Frozen encoder]
  B --> C[Small predictor]
  C --> D[Surprise / prediction error]
  D --> E1[Plasticity: gate memory store]
  E1 --> F1[Offline replay]
  F1 --> G1[Slow linear readout]
  D --> E2[Metacognition: confidence / hedge / refuse]

title: ReGRPO: Reflection-Augmented Policy Optimization for Tool-Using Agents
tldr: ReGRPO teaches tool-using vision-language agents to recover from failures by jointly learning to reflect on errors and correct actions, improving performance on multimodal multi-step tasks.
abstract: Tool-augmented vision-language models can solve complex multimodal tasks by calling external tools, but they often break down when a step goes wrong. The paper argues that current supervised fine-tuning and trajectory-level reinforcement learning provide weak learning signals for recovery and error repair. ReGRPO addresses this by collecting grounded failure cases, turning them into reflection-oriented training triplets, and optimizing both reflection tokens and corrective actions together. Experiments on GTA and GAIA show that this approach outperforms strong open-source baselines using the same backbone and tool set.
introduction: This work targets a practical weakness of tool-using agents: they can execute multi-step plans, but they are fragile when intermediate tool calls fail or lead to near-miss outcomes. The authors propose ReGRPO, or Reflection-augmented Group Relative Policy Optimization, to make agents better at diagnosing and correcting their own mistakes. Their pipeline first constructs reflective supervision from near-miss executions, then uses these reflection signals during policy optimization. By learning reflection and correction jointly, the method aims to improve recovery behavior without encouraging unnecessary reflection at every step.
key_contributions: Introduces ReGRPO, a reflection-augmented policy optimization method for tool-using vision-language agents.
Builds a structured reflective data engine that executes near-miss actions to collect grounded failure observations.
Creates Reflection-of-Thought triplets in the form of ErrorType, Evidence, and FixPlan, paired with corrected actions for warm-start supervised fine-tuning.
Optimizes reflection tokens and corrective actions jointly with group-relative advantages within local trajectories.
Adds a reflection-cost term to discourage unnecessary reflection and reports strong results on GTA and GAIA against open-source baselines.
why_it_matters: For Physical AI and Vision AI agents that must use tools reliably, the key challenge is not just solving tasks but recovering when execution deviates from plan. This paper offers a concrete training recipe for making agents more fault-tolerant and self-correcting.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A[Tool-using VLM agent] --> B[Task step fails]
B --> C[Collect grounded failures]
C --> D[Build reflection triplets]
D --> E[Jointly train]
E --> F[Reflection tokens + corrective actions]
F --> G[Better recovery]
G --> H[Higher GTA / GAIA performance]

title: Xiaomi-GUI-0 Technical Report
tldr: Xiaomi-GUI-0 is a real-device-trained mobile GUI agent that closes the benchmark-to-deployment gap by learning and being evaluated in a closed loop on physical phones.
abstract: The paper argues that current GUI agents look good on offline benchmarks but often fail in real mobile apps because real execution involves changing layouts, permissions, payment prompts, account states, and other abnormal conditions. To address this, the authors build Xiaomi-GUI-0, a multimodal GUI agent trained and evaluated in a real-device closed loop, with physical devices as the main execution environment and sandboxes used only as support. They also assemble multi-source data for common tasks, long-tail intents, reflection, and memory, and use failed trajectories to generate corrected actions and recovery examples. The resulting system is trained in three stages and reports improved real-world stability, including 72.0% success on the in-house RealMobile benchmark and 78.9% on AndroidWorld.
introduction: This work targets a practical weakness in current GUI agents: benchmark performance often overestimates how well they work on real phones. The authors note that real applications introduce unstable and rarely seen states, such as account verification, permission dialogs, payment authentication, and risk-control checks, which are poorly represented in offline trajectories and simulated environments. Xiaomi-GUI-0 is designed around a real-device-dominant infrastructure so that data collection, training, rollout, and evaluation all occur in an execution distribution close to deployment. The training recipe combines supervised fine-tuning, step-level reinforcement learning, and agentic reinforcement learning, supported by an error-driven data flywheel that converts failures into additional training signal.
key_contributions: Introduces Xiaomi-GUI-0, a native multimodal GUI agent for real mobile environments.
Uses a real-device-dominant hybrid infrastructure where physical devices are the primary execution environment and sandboxes are auxiliary.
Constructs multi-source training data covering head tasks, long-tail intents, reflection, and memory capabilities.
Implements an error-driven data flywheel that turns failure trajectories into corrected actions, reflective explanations, and recovery demonstrations.
Reports 72.0% success on the in-house RealMobile benchmark and 78.9% on AndroidWorld, with improved execution stability and abnormal-state recognition.
why_it_matters: For Vision AI and Physical AI, this paper is a step toward agents that are trained and validated under the same conditions they face at deployment. For multi-agent or interactive systems, it highlights the importance of closed-loop, failure-driven learning when operating in messy real-world environments rather than sanitized benchmarks.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
  A["Real mobile apps"] --> B["Closed-loop on device"]
  C["Multi-source data"] --> E["3-stage training"]
  D["Failures & recoveries"] --> E
  B --> E
  E --> F["Xiaomi-GUI-0 agent"]
  F --> G["Real-world success"]

title: Visual Semantic Entropy: Do Vision Language Models Recognize Visual Ambiguity?
tldr: Visual Semantic Entropy (VSE) estimates VLM uncertainty by perturbing only the image, avoiding prompt-driven variability and improving ambiguity detection.
abstract: Vision-language models can sound confident even when an image is visually ambiguous, which leads to biased predictions. The authors argue that standard semantic-entropy methods can underestimate uncertainty because overconfident visual embeddings reduce answer diversity, and that perturbing text or both text and image often measures prompt sensitivity more than visual ambiguity. To address this, they propose Visual Semantic Entropy (VSE), which keeps the text fixed, perturbs only the image, clusters answers into semantic prototypes, and measures dispersion among them. Across five VLMs and five VQA benchmarks, VSE is reported to better capture visual ambiguity and achieve state-of-the-art uncertainty estimation.
introduction: This work studies how to estimate uncertainty in vision-language models when the input image itself is ambiguous. Existing entropy-based approaches depend on diversity in sampled outputs, but the paper argues that this diversity can be artificially suppressed by confident visual embeddings or distorted by text paraphrases and joint text-image perturbations. The proposed solution, Visual Semantic Entropy, isolates visual uncertainty by holding the text query fixed and perturbing only the image. The method then clusters generated responses into semantic prototypes and computes a mass-weighted dispersion score to quantify uncertainty.
key_contributions: Identifies a failure mode of Semantic Entropy in VLMs: overconfident visual embeddings can suppress output diversity and cause uncertainty underestimation.
Shows that text paraphrasing and joint text-image perturbation methods often conflate visual ambiguity with prompt sensitivity.
Introduces Visual Semantic Entropy (VSE), which perturbs only the image while keeping the text query fixed.
Uses semantic clustering of generated answers and mass-weighted dispersion to estimate uncertainty from visually induced response variability.
Reports extensive evaluation on five vision-language models and five VQA benchmarks, where VSE achieves state-of-the-art uncertainty estimation.
why_it_matters: Reliable uncertainty estimation is important for Vision AI systems that must know when they are unsure, especially under ambiguous or noisy visual conditions. By focusing perturbations on the image rather than the prompt, VSE offers a more faithful measure of visual ambiguity for VLMs.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A[Ambiguous image] --> B[VLM answers]
B --> C[Keep text fixed]
C --> D[Perturb image only]
D --> E[Cluster answers]
E --> F[Semantic prototypes]
F --> G[Measure dispersion]
G --> H[VSE uncertainty]
H --> I[Captures visual ambiguity]

title: MV-GEL: Language-Driven Multi-View Geometric Entity Localization on Meshes
tldr: MV-GEL localizes language-specified CAD-like geometric entities on 3D meshes by ranking the most informative views before running VLM-based segmentation and lifting the masks back to the mesh.
abstract: The paper tackles language-driven localization of fine-grained geometric entities in 3D, such as faces, edges, and curved surfaces, which is important for CAD and robotics but difficult because visibility changes drastically across viewpoints. MV-GEL addresses this by first selecting views that best expose the queried entity, then applying a vision-language segmentation model, and finally projecting the predicted masks onto the mesh with ray casting. The authors report strong gains over baselines, especially for thin and view-sensitive structures.
introduction: This work studies referring localization for structured 3D geometry on polygon meshes, where a user query describes a specific CAD entity and the goal is to identify it precisely. The main challenge is that a single viewpoint can hide or distort the target through occlusion or foreshortening, making naive language-conditioned 3D localization unreliable. MV-GEL introduces a multi-view pipeline that first ranks candidate views using language-conditioned observability of geometric entities, then performs segmentation on selected views, and maps the result back to the mesh. The method is designed to be CAD-agnostic and uses only mesh input.
key_contributions: Introduces MV-GEL, a framework for language-driven localization of fine-grained geometric entities on polygon meshes.
Proposes GELviews, a prompt-conditioned view ranking module that selects viewpoints where the queried entity is most interpretable.
Uses a VLM-based reasoning segmentation backbone on selected views, then lifts predicted masks to the mesh via geometry-aware ray casting.
Reports up to 1.7× improvement in face-level IoU and over 4.5× gains in edge-level F1 versus vanilla baselines.
Shows stronger performance than CLIP-based and random view sampling methods, especially for thin and view-sensitive structures.
why_it_matters: This is relevant to Physical AI and vision systems that must interact with real geometric objects, since accurate localization of CAD entities is a prerequisite for inspection, manipulation, and simulation. The view-selection idea is also broadly useful for multi-view perception pipelines where observability varies strongly with pose.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A[Text query] --> B[Select best views]
B --> C[Vision-language segmenter]
C --> D[Predict masks]
D --> E[Ray cast to mesh]
E --> F[Entity localization]
F --> G[Faces / edges / surfaces]

title: Think While You Map: Asynchronous Vision-Language Agents for Incremental 3D Scene Graphs
tldr: An asynchronous vision-language mapping pipeline builds incremental 3D scene graphs that are queryable during exploration, while improving grounding performance over prior open-vocabulary methods.
abstract: The paper argues that open-vocabulary 3D scene graph systems do not need to wait for full reconstruction before adding semantics. Instead, it proposes running lightweight online mapping and heavier vision-language refinement in parallel. A probabilistic voxel backbone maintains object identities over time, while background VLM agents add attributes, spatial relations, and resolve duplicates via semantic loop closure. The system also uses a multi-target frame scheduler to reduce VLM cost by choosing frames that inform multiple objects at once.
introduction: Existing open-vocabulary 3D scene graph pipelines usually reconstruct first and semantically enrich later, which means the graph cannot be queried during exploration. This work replaces that sequential design with an asynchronous architecture where mapping and semantic refinement happen concurrently. The core mapping component is a probabilistic voxel-based model that incrementally tracks stable object identities, while VLM agents run in the background to enrich the graph. To make this efficient, the method schedules a small set of informative frames that can serve multiple targets, and it uses semantic loop closure to merge duplicate tracks. The end result is a scene graph that becomes usable earlier and gains detail over time.
key_contributions: Proposes an asynchronous architecture for incremental 3D scene graph construction with concurrent mapping and semantic refinement.
Introduces a probabilistic voxel-based backbone that incrementally maintains stable object identities.
Uses background vision-language agents to add fine-grained visual attributes and infer spatial relations.
Resolves duplicate object tracks through semantic loop closure.
Employs a multi-target frame scheduler to amortize VLM cost by reusing informative frames across multiple objects.
why_it_matters: For Physical AI and embodied perception, this enables agents to query a scene graph while still exploring, rather than waiting for a full offline reconstruction. It also shows that asynchronous semantic enrichment can improve practical usability without giving up accuracy on semantic segmentation and visual grounding.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A[RGB-D Stream] --> B[Online Voxel Map]
B --> C[Object IDs Over Time]
A --> D[Frame Scheduler]
D --> E[VLM Agents]
E --> F[Attributes & Relations]
E --> G[Duplicate Merge]
F --> H[Incremental 3D Scene Graph]
G --> H
C --> H

title: Localized Conformal Prediction for Image Classification with Vision-Language Models
tldr: The paper benchmarks localized conformal prediction for image classification with vision-language models and finds that raw cosine similarity is not enough, but a simple nonlinear similarity transform reduces prediction set size while preserving marginal coverage.
abstract: This work studies conformal prediction for natural image classification using vision-language models. The authors benchmark a recent localized conformal prediction method and test whether cosine similarity between test and calibration visual features can improve performance. They find that using cosine similarity directly does not outperform non-local baselines, so they introduce a simple nonlinear transformation of similarity scores. This transformation keeps marginal coverage guarantees and yields statistically significant reductions in average prediction set size.
introduction: Conformal prediction is widely used for uncertainty quantification because it provides strong marginal coverage guarantees, but exact conditional coverage is generally unattainable. A common workaround is to localize conformal sets using similarity between a test example and calibration data, with better calibration expected for nearby points. While this idea has worked well in regression, its use in image classification remains less explored. In this paper, the authors evaluate localized conformal prediction on natural image classification tasks with vision-language models and propose a nonlinear similarity transform to improve set efficiency without sacrificing marginal coverage.
key_contributions: Benchmarked a recent localized conformal prediction method on natural image classification tasks with vision-language models.
Showed that using raw cosine similarity between visual features of test and calibration samples does not improve over non-local baselines.
Proposed a simple nonlinear transformation of cosine similarities tailored to localized conformal prediction.
Demonstrated statistically significant reductions in mean prediction set size while preserving marginal coverage guarantees.
Released an open-source implementation of the method and experiments.
why_it_matters: This work is directly relevant to Vision AI because it studies how to make conformal uncertainty estimates more efficient for image classifiers built on VLM embeddings. More compact prediction sets with preserved coverage can improve the practicality of calibrated decision-making in downstream systems.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A["VLM features"] --> B["Calibration set"]
A --> C["Test image"]
B --> D["Localized CP"]
C --> D
D --> E["Cosine similarity"]
E --> F["Nonlinear transform"]
F --> G["Prediction set"]
G --> H["Coverage + smaller size"]

title: Token-Sparse Medical Multimodal Reasoning via Dual-Stream Reinforcement Learning
tldr: ViToS uses dual-stream reinforcement learning to jointly prune irrelevant visual tokens and improve medical multimodal reasoning, yielding faster inference with strong benchmark performance.
abstract: The paper studies a key limitation of vision-language models in medicine: clinical images often contain only sparse visual evidence, so reasoning can be hurt by unnecessary tokens. To address this, the authors propose ViToS, a dual-stream RL framework that jointly learns active visual token pruning and medical question answering. They also introduce cross-feedback sequential optimization to stabilize training of the shared policy and reduce gradient conflicts.
introduction: Medical vision-language models need to reason over images where the useful evidence is often localized and sparse, making full-sequence token processing inefficient and potentially noisy. The proposed ViToS framework treats token pruning and question answering as two coordinated branches within a single policy model: one branch learns grounding to identify relevant visual regions, while the other performs reasoning using the pruned token set. To make this coupled optimization work, the authors use cross-feedback sequential optimization, which is designed to avoid gradient conflict and improve convergence. The method is evaluated on seven medical benchmarks and is reported to reduce the visual token sequence to 77% of its original length while improving performance relative to the referenced baselines.
key_contributions: Introduces ViToS, a dual-stream reinforcement learning framework for joint visual token pruning and medical multimodal reasoning.
Uses two task branches in one shared policy model: one for grounding and one for token-sparse question answering after pruning.
Proposes cross-feedback sequential optimization to handle coupled policy learning and mitigate gradient conflict.
Reports evaluation on seven medical benchmarks, with reduced visual tokens and improved relative performance on Lingshu-7B and HuatuoGPT-Vision-7B.
Shows that pruning outside the grounding region can improve both efficiency and reasoning quality in medical VLMs.
why_it_matters: This work is relevant to Vision AI because it targets efficient inference on sparse medical images, where not all visual tokens are informative. The approach may also generalize to other multimodal settings where selective perception and reasoning need to be learned jointly.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A[Clinical image + question] --> B[Dual-stream RL]
B --> C[Visual token pruning]
B --> D[Medical QA]
C --> E[Sparse visual evidence]
D --> E
E --> F[Shared policy]
F --> G[Cross-feedback sequential opt]
G --> B

title: Seeing Is Not Sharing: Some Vision-Language Models Overestimate Common Ground in Asymmetric Dialogue
tldr: Some vision-language models confuse what dialogue partners could infer from a shared map with what they have actually established through interaction, leading to overestimated common ground.
abstract: The paper studies whether vision-language models can tell the difference between shared perception and shared understanding in collaborative dialogue. Using 13,077 annotated reference expressions from HCRC MapTask, the authors test interpretation matching under controlled changes to dialogue context and access to map information. They find that authentic map images improve overall accuracy but also make models more likely to predict alignment even when it is not present. Text descriptions of the same map content reproduce the same bias, while non-informative images suppress alignment predictions, suggesting the effect comes from task-relevant map content rather than the visual modality itself.
introduction: This work addresses a core grounding problem in dialogue: seeing the same scene or map does not mean two interlocutors have reached the same interpretation. The authors ask whether vision-language models can distinguish potential common ground from grounded common ground established through conversation. They frame this as an interpretation-matching task over annotated reference expressions in MapTask dialogues and vary both dialogue context and map-information access. The results indicate that several VLMs, especially Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct, tend to use static referential cues from the map as evidence of mutual understanding instead of tracking grounding over the course of the dialogue.
key_contributions: Introduces an interpretation-matching evaluation on 13,077 annotated reference expressions from HCRC MapTask dialogues.
Systematically tests VLMs under controlled manipulations of dialogue context and map-information access.
Shows that authentic map images improve overall performance but also increase over-prediction of alignment.
Demonstrates that textual descriptions of the same map content reproduce the same bias, while non-informative images largely suppress alignment predictions.
Provides calibration analysis and reference-chain tracking evidence that models rely on static map cues rather than grounding dynamics across dialogue.
why_it_matters: For Physical AI and embodied dialogue systems, this is a warning that multimodal models may overestimate shared understanding when given environmental context. Robust collaborative agents need to track what has actually been mutually established, not just what is visually available to both parties.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A[MapTask refs] --> B[Context changes]
B --> C[VLM match test]
C --> D[Map image]
C --> E[Text map desc]
C --> F[Blank image]
D --> G[Higher acc + overalign]
E --> G
F --> H[Lower align preds]
G --> I[Common ground overestimated]

title: Technical Report of RoboSpatial Challenge at CVPR 2026: Selective Reasoning Activation and Reference-Frame Disambiguation for Embodied Spatial Reasoning
tldr: RoboSpatialBrain wins the RoboSpatial Challenge by combining inference-time reasoning prompts with reference-frame disambiguation for embodied spatial reasoning.
abstract: Vision-language models are good at general perception, but they often fail on spatial reasoning needed for embodied tasks. This report presents RoboSpatialBrain, built on RoboBrain2.5-8B-NV, and uses two training-free inference-time components: a forced <think> prefix with a task-specific post-prompt to encourage deliberate reasoning, and a reference-frame redirection pipeline to resolve camera-centric versus object-centric ambiguity. The authors also study fine-tuning on compatibility data and how it interacts with prompting. The system placed first in the RoboSpatial Challenge and reached 80.9% success on RoboSpatial-Home.
introduction: The paper addresses a common weakness of vision-language models: strong general understanding does not reliably translate to spatial reasoning in embodied environments. To target this, the authors build RoboSpatialBrain on top of RoboBrain2.5-8B-NV for the RoboSpatial Challenge at CVPR 2026. Their approach is centered on two inference-time mechanisms that require no additional training: a forced <think> prefix plus task-specific prompting to activate deliberate reasoning, and a reference-frame redirection pipeline to disambiguate spatial context. They also examine whether fine-tuning on compatibility data improves performance and how that interacts with the prompting strategy.
key_contributions: Introduces RoboSpatialBrain, the authors’ submission to the RoboSpatial Challenge, built on RoboBrain2.5-8B-NV.
Proposes a training-free forced <think> prefix activation strategy with a task-specific post-prompt to elicit deliberate reasoning on context and compatibility tasks.
Adds an explicit reference-frame redirection pipeline to resolve camera-centric and object-centric ambiguity in context tasks.
Explores fine-tuning RoboBrain2.5 on compatibility data and analyzes its interaction with prompting.
Achieves first place in the RoboSpatial Challenge with an 80.9% success rate on RoboSpatial-Home.
why_it_matters: This work highlights practical, low-overhead ways to improve embodied spatial reasoning without relying solely on heavier training pipelines. The reference-frame disambiguation idea is especially relevant for Vision AI systems that must reason about objects from different spatial perspectives.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A[Vision-language model] --> B[RoboSpatialBrain\nRoboBrain2.5-8B-NV]
B --> C[Forced <think> + post-prompt]
B --> D[Reference-frame redirection]
C --> E[Deliberate spatial reasoning]
D --> F[Camera vs object frame disambiguation]
E --> G[Robot task success]
F --> G

title: No Place to Hide: Benchmarking Video Hallucination with Background-Controlled Pairs
tldr: VidPair-Halluc is a controlled benchmark that isolates video hallucination by pairing clips with nearly identical backgrounds but different foreground semantics, exposing weaknesses in large video models.
abstract: The paper introduces VidPair-Halluc, a benchmark designed to evaluate video hallucination in large video models under controlled visual conditions. Instead of relying mainly on text perturbations or adversarial questions, it uses video pairs with highly similar backgrounds and different foreground meanings, so errors can be attributed more confidently to hallucination rather than background mismatch. The benchmark is built with PairFlow, a pipeline that uses text-to-image and video generation to create coherent story clips and then assemble them into adversarial pairs. It includes 1K adversarial video pairs and 11K spatio-temporal QA pairs spanning ten semantic aspects, and the authors report that mainstream LVMs still struggle with fine-grained understanding in these adversarial settings.
introduction: Video hallucination is difficult to measure cleanly because many existing benchmarks confound model errors with changes in scene context, especially background variation. This work addresses that problem by constructing video pairs that preserve background consistency while changing foreground semantics, making hallucination evaluation more rigorous. The authors build the benchmark through PairFlow, which composes stories, generates coherent video clips, and assembles them into adversarial pairs using recent generative models. The resulting dataset spans both spatial and temporal reasoning across ten semantic categories, enabling targeted evaluation of large video models.
key_contributions: Introduces VidPair-Halluc, a benchmark for measuring video hallucination under background-controlled conditions.
Proposes PairFlow, a pipeline that uses text-to-image and video generation to systematically create adversarial video pairs.
Provides 1K high-quality adversarial video pairs with highly similar backgrounds but different foreground semantics.
Includes 11K spatio-temporal question-answer pairs covering ten semantic aspects.
Shows that mainstream large video models still struggle with robust fine-grained video understanding in adversarial settings.
why_it_matters: For Vision AI, this benchmark improves attribution of failure modes by separating hallucination from background-induced confusion. It offers a more controlled way to stress-test large video models on fine-grained spatial and temporal reasoning, which is relevant for reliable perception in real-world applications.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A[VidPair-Halluc] --> B[PairFlow]
B --> C[Generate story clips]
C --> D[Match backgrounds]
D --> E[Swap foreground meaning]
E --> F[Adversarial video pairs]
F --> G[Spatio-temporal QA]
G --> H[Test LVMs]
H --> I[Expose hallucination]

title: Think in English, Answer in Korean: Efficient Adaptation of Multilingual Tool-Using Agents
tldr: LuckyStar 111B is a post-trained multilingual hybrid reasoning model that adapts efficiently for Korean-English enterprise tool use under tight serving constraints.
abstract: The paper presents LuckyStar 111B, a 111B-parameter model built by adapting Cohere’s fully post-trained Command A model rather than training from scratch. It uses preamble conditioning to switch between concise responses and longer tool-oriented reasoning, and studies four efficiency-oriented adaptation choices: multilingual supervised fine-tuning, reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards for multi-step tool use, language-consistency rewards for Korean responses, and 4-bit quantization for single-GPU serving. The adapted model improves mathematical reasoning, function calling, and agentic NL2SQL performance while keeping general Korean and English instruction-following quality. The authors frame this as a practical recipe for deploying multilingual agentic systems under memory and serving limits.
introduction: This work addresses how to adapt a large multilingual post-trained model for enterprise tool-using agents without the cost of full pretraining. The focus is on Korean-English settings, where the system must both reason over multi-step tool-use tasks and produce user-facing Korean responses that remain fluent and consistent. To achieve this, the authors build on Command A and use preamble conditioning to control whether the model behaves in a short-answer mode or a more deliberative, tool-oriented reasoning mode. They then evaluate a set of training and deployment strategies aimed at improving agentic performance while remaining practical to serve on constrained hardware.
key_contributions: Introduces LuckyStar 111B, a 111B-parameter hybrid reasoning model adapted from a fully post-trained base model.
Uses preamble conditioning to toggle between concise non-reasoning behavior and longer tool-oriented reasoning.
Studies multilingual supervised fine-tuning for adapting the model to Korean-English enterprise use.
Applies reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards for multi-step tool-use tasks, along with language-consistency rewards for Korean outputs.
Shows that 4-bit quantization enables single-GPU serving, and reports gains in mathematical reasoning, function calling, and agentic NL2SQL without losing general instruction-following quality.
why_it_matters: For Physical AI and multi-agent/agentic systems, the paper offers a concrete recipe for making large multilingual tool-using models deployable under real serving constraints. Its emphasis on verifiable rewards, language consistency, and efficient quantization is especially relevant for practical enterprise agents that must reason, call tools, and respond in Korean and English.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A[Base model: Command A] --> B[Adaptation
LuckyStar 111B]
B --> C[Preamble control
short vs tool reasoning]
B --> D[Multilingual SFT]
B --> E[RL w/ verifiable rewards]
E --> F[Tool use + NL2SQL]
B --> G[Korean consistency reward]
B --> H[4-bit quantization]
D --> I[Better EN/KR tasks]
F --> I
G --> I
H --> J[Single-GPU serving]

title: InstanceControl: Controllable Complex Image Generation without Instance Labeling
tldr: InstanceControl uses a VLM to match text-described instances to visual-condition regions, enabling multi-instance controllable image generation without manual instance labels.
abstract: The paper addresses a common failure mode in controllable image generation: when scenes contain multiple instances, models can confuse attributes across objects. Existing fixes often rely on manually labeling instances, which is costly and not scalable. InstanceControl removes this requirement by using a vision-language model to infer correspondences between instance descriptions in the prompt and regions in the visual condition, then refines the resulting masks during generation. The authors report that this improves fidelity and instance-level control over prior methods.
introduction: Controllable image generation methods like ControlNet can use visual conditions such as depth maps to steer synthesis, but they often struggle in complex scenes with multiple objects. In these settings, instance attributes can become entangled, leading to incorrect assignments between text descriptions and image regions. InstanceControl targets this problem by automatically establishing instance-level correspondences between the prompt and the conditioning image, without requiring manual instance annotations. It uses a VLM to parse instance descriptions and predict masks from the visual condition, then applies adaptive mask refinement to improve robustness during generation.
key_contributions: Identifies instance-description/region association as a key bottleneck in multi-instance controllable generation.
Introduces InstanceControl, a method for controllable complex image generation without manual instance labeling.
Uses a vision-language model to automatically parse instance descriptions from text prompts and predict corresponding instance masks from visual conditions.
Proposes an adaptive mask refinement strategy to mitigate noise in the predicted masks during generation.
Reports extensive experiments showing improved fidelity and more precise instance-level control than prior state-of-the-art methods.
why_it_matters: This is relevant for Vision AI because it improves fine-grained, text-guided image synthesis in cluttered multi-object scenes, a setting where existing control methods often fail. The label-free design also reduces annotation burden, making instance-level control more practical to deploy.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
  A[Prompt with instances] --> B[VLM infers correspondences]
  C[Visual condition] --> B
  B --> D[Initial instance masks]
  D --> E[Mask refinement during generation]
  E --> F[Controlled image synthesis]
  F --> G[Better fidelity & instance control]

title: Agentic-Ideation: Sample Efficient Agentic Trajectories Synthesis for Scientific Ideation Agents
tldr: Agentic-Ideation uses oracle-guided trajectory synthesis to train a scientific ideation agent that beats a workflow baseline by 11.91% while cutting high-quality data synthesis cost by over 10×.
abstract: The paper tackles automated scientific ideation with agentic LLMs, noting that fixed workflows limit flexibility and previous agentic data synthesis is too expensive for this domain. The authors propose Agentic-Ideation, which first defines a tool space with external and cognitive tools, then uses an oracle-guided synthesis strategy to reconstruct reasoning and tool-use trajectories from a reference idea. The synthesized trajectories are used to train an agentic LLM, with masking applied to tool execution results so the model learns decision logic rather than overfitting to feedback. Experiments show improved overall idea quality over a workflow-based baseline and substantially better sample efficiency for high-quality data generation.
introduction: This work addresses automated ideation for scientific discovery, where models must navigate large literature spaces and complex reasoning/action spaces. Existing AI Scientist-style systems often depend on hand-designed workflows, which can constrain flexibility. The paper instead treats scientific ideation as an agentic problem and builds a training pipeline for learning tool-using trajectories. A key idea is to use a reference idea as oracle guidance to generate directed multi-agent trajectories more efficiently than trial-and-error exploration.
key_contributions: Defines a comprehensive tool space combining three external tools and three cognitive tools for scientific ideation agents.
Introduces an Oracle-Guided Data Synthesis strategy that reconstructs logical reasoning and tool invocation paths from a reference idea.
Trains an agentic LLM on synthesized trajectories with masking of tool execution results to emphasize decision-making logic.
Reports 11.91% improvement in overall quality over the best workflow-based baseline.
Improves sample efficiency for high-quality data synthesis by more than 10×.
why_it_matters: For Physical AI, Multi-Agent Systems, and Vision AI research pipelines, the paper offers a more flexible way to train autonomous ideation agents that can reason with tools rather than follow rigid scripts. Its main practical value is reducing the cost of creating training data for agentic scientific assistants.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A[Science ideation] --> B[Tool space: external + cognitive]
B --> C[Oracle-guided trajectory synthesis]
C --> D[Reconstruct reasoning + tool use]
D --> E[Mask tool results]
E --> F[Train agentic LLM]
F --> G[Better idea quality + sample efficiency]

title: ShopX: A Foundation Model for Intent-to-Item Fulfillment in Agentic Shopping
tldr: ShopX is a foundation model that lets an LLM directly plan and execute intent-to-item fulfillment in shopping using semantic item IDs, reducing reliance on brittle tool-mediated retrieval pipelines.
abstract: The paper argues that current AI shopping systems force complex user intents through search or ranking tools that are not well matched to language-driven agent workflows. To address this, the authors introduce ShopX, a foundation model that combines intent understanding, planning, and item-space operations in one model via semantic IDs. They also build a serving framework with a model-facing action protocol for catalog grounding, context access, and state management. Experiments on anonymized Taobao single- and multi-turn fulfillment tasks show that this model-native approach works better overall, especially on ambiguous or complex requests.
introduction: The work targets a bottleneck in agentic shopping: LLM agents can understand natural-language intents, but existing systems usually hand off execution to search and recommendation pipelines with limited interfaces. This creates a gap between what the user asks and how items are selected, ranked, or bundled in item space. ShopX is proposed as a foundation model that natively operates over semantic IDs, allowing it to plan and compose fulfillment actions such as retrieval, ranking, and bundling. The system is deployed with a serving harness that exposes structured actions and support surfaces for shopping-state management and catalog grounding. The training recipe is designed to make a general LLM capable of flexible multi-turn shopping fulfillment while preserving instruction-following and general knowledge.
key_contributions: Introduces ShopX, a foundation model for intent-to-item fulfillment in agentic shopping.
Defines a model-native fulfillment framework with a serving harness, action protocol, catalog grounding, context access, and state management.
Uses semantically recoverable, LLM-operable semantic IDs to bridge language understanding and item-space execution.
Supports flexible SID-native item-space operations including SID beam-search retrieval, listwise ranking, and product bundling.
Reports improved overall behavior versus tool-mediated agentic systems on single- and multi-turn tasks derived from anonymized Taobao logs, especially for complex or ambiguous requests.
why_it_matters: For Physical AI and Vision/agentic commerce systems, this shows a practical way to reduce brittle tool handoffs and let an LLM reason directly over items it can act on. That can make shopping agents more robust to ambiguous intent and multi-turn fulfillment workflows.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A[User intent] --> B[ShopX model]
B --> C[Understand & plan]
C --> D[Semantic IDs]
D --> E[Item-space ops]
E --> F[Action protocol]
F --> G[Catalog grounding]
F --> H[State/context]
G --> I[Fulfillment]
H --> I

title: Design and Implementation of Agentic Orchestrations and Orchestration of Agents
tldr: The paper proposes a framework for designing agentic business process orchestrations, balancing LLM-agent autonomy with process-level robustness, traceability, and correctness assurance.
abstract: This paper studies how to combine autonomous AI agents, especially LLM-based agents, with process technology in agentic business process management. The authors introduce a classification framework for orchestration options based on properties such as task specificity, traceability, tractability, autonomy, reactivity, and correctness assurance. They also propose qualitative decision criteria and quantitative metrics for evaluating these designs, and illustrate them with different implementations of a predictive light sensing scenario.
introduction: Agentic Business Process Management is emerging as a way to coordinate AI agents without losing the guarantees expected from process-aware systems. The central challenge is to preserve the flexibility and autonomy of agents while still enabling robust, explainable, and controllable execution. To address this, the paper develops a framework for comparing different orchestration styles and their design trade-offs. It further provides criteria and metrics to assess how well a given implementation meets these properties, demonstrated through a predictive light sensing example.
key_contributions: Introduces a classification framework for agentic orchestration options.
Characterizes orchestration designs along dimensions including task specificity, traceability, tractability, autonomy, reactivity, and correctness assurance.
Provides qualitative decision criteria for selecting suitable realizations in different scenarios.
Defines metrics for quantitatively assessing realization properties.
Demonstrates the framework through multiple agentic implementations of a predictive light sensing scenario.
why_it_matters: For Physical AI and multi-agent systems, this work is useful because it offers a systematic way to design agentic workflows that are more controllable and auditable than ad hoc agent chaining. It helps practitioners reason about when to prioritize autonomy versus process guarantees.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
  A[Agentic BPM] --> B[Orchestration Options]
  B --> C[Classify by Properties]
  C --> D[Decision Criteria]
  D --> E[Quantitative Metrics]
  E --> F[Predictive Light Sensing]
  F --> G[Implementation Comparison]

title: 3D HAMSTER: Bridging Planning and Control in Hierarchical Vision Language Action Models through 3D Trajectory Guidance
tldr: 3D HAMSTER upgrades hierarchical VLA planning from 2D to metrically grounded 3D waypoints, improving downstream manipulation robustness and performance.
abstract: The paper argues that 2D end-effector trajectories are a poor interface for modern low-level robot policies that operate in 3D point-cloud space, because missing depth information can distort the intended path. To address this, the authors propose 3D HAMSTER, which equips a vision-language model with a depth encoder and a dense depth reconstruction objective so it can predict 3D waypoint sequences directly. These 3D trajectories are then used as guidance for a point-cloud-based control policy. The method is reported to outperform proprietary VLMs and 2D-guided baselines in trajectory prediction, simulation, and real-world manipulation, especially under appearance changes and unseen language, spatial, and visual conditions.
introduction: Hierarchical vision-language-action systems often separate planning and control: a high-level model decides what to do, and a low-level policy handles how to move. In recent approaches, the planner provides 2D end-effector trajectories as explicit guidance, but this mismatches the 3D metric space used by point-cloud-based controllers. The result is that 2D waypoints must be projected onto scene surfaces with ambiguous depth, which can warp the intended motion. 3D HAMSTER addresses this by making the planner predict metrically reliable 3D trajectories directly, using a depth encoder and dense depth reconstruction to better align planning with control.
key_contributions: Introduces 3D HAMSTER, a hierarchical VLA framework that predicts 3D waypoint trajectories instead of 2D image-plane paths.
Adds a dedicated depth encoder and dense depth reconstruction objective to a vision-language model to produce metrically grounded 3D plans.
Integrates the predicted 3D trajectories directly into a point-cloud-based low-level manipulation policy.
Reports consistent improvements over proprietary VLMs and 2D-guided baselines across trajectory prediction, simulation, and real-world manipulation.
Shows the largest gains under appearance shifts and unseen language, spatial, and visual conditions.
why_it_matters: For Physical AI and robot manipulation, planning in the same 3D representation used by the controller can reduce geometric mismatch and improve robustness. This work is relevant because it suggests a practical path for making VLA hierarchies more reliable under distribution shifts.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A[RGB-D input] --> B[VLM + depth encoder]
B --> C[Dense depth reconstruction]
C --> D[3D waypoint sequence]
D --> E[Point-cloud control policy]
E --> F[Robot action]
D --> G[Robust to appearance/novel conditions]

title: Z-1: Efficient Reinforcement Learning for Vision-Language-Action Models
tldr: Z-1 shows that GRPO-style reinforcement learning can substantially improve a flow-based vision-language-action policy on RoboCasa using only public demonstrations for SFT.
abstract: The paper addresses a common limitation of vision-language-action (VLA) policies for robot manipulation: they are often trained only by behavior cloning or supervised fine-tuning, so they cannot learn much from their own mistakes. Z-1 is a reinforcement learning post-training framework built on top of the flow-based model c00.5, starting from supervised fine-tuning on publicly released RoboCasa demonstrations and then applying task-wise Group Relative Policy Optimization across 24 RoboCasa tasks. The method is designed for efficiency and stability during online optimization, using shared-prefix rollouts, tree-structured trajectory branching, completion-aware reward calibration, and selective joint training of the VLM and Action Expert. It reports an average success rate of 80.6%, which is 13.2 percentage points higher than its SFT initialization and better than published state-of-the-art models.
introduction: This work focuses on improving vision-language-action models for robotic manipulation beyond imitation learning from fixed demonstrations. The authors argue that supervised fine-tuning alone leaves performance gains on the table because the policy does not get to learn from its own failures. To address this, they introduce Z-1, a reinforcement learning post-training framework for the flow-based VLA model c00.5. Z-1 is trained with only public RoboCasa demonstrations for SFT, then fine-tuned with task-wise GRPO over 24 standard RoboCasa tasks using a set of mechanisms intended to make online RL more efficient and stable.
key_contributions: Introduces Z-1, an RL post-training framework for flow-based vision-language-action models.
Applies task-wise Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) on 24 RoboCasa manipulation tasks.
Uses only publicly released RoboCasa demonstrations for supervised fine-tuning, avoiding additional private data.

Proposes efficiency/stability mechanisms including shared-prefix rollouts, tree-structured trajectory branching, completion-aware reward calibration, and selective joint training of the VLM and Action Expert.
Achieves an average 80.6% success rate on RoboCasa, improving SFT initialization by 13.2 points and outperforming published state-of-the-art models.
why_it_matters: For Physical AI, this suggests that reinforcement learning post-training can meaningfully boost robot policy performance even when only public demonstrations are available. It is also relevant to Vision AI because it combines language, vision, and control in a way that improves task success without requiring extra private datasets.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A[Pub RoboCasa demos] --> B[SFT init]
B --> C[RL post-training]
C --> D[GRPO over 24 tasks]
D --> E[Efficient rollouts]
E --> F[Shared prefix\nTree branching]
E --> G[Reward calibration]
E --> H[Selective joint train]
H --> I[Z-1 policy\n80.6% success]

title: UniTacVLA: Unified Tactile Understanding and Prediction in Vision Language Action Models
tldr: UniTacVLA turns tactile sensing from a passive input into a unified latent prior for understanding and predicting contact, improving dexterous manipulation under clean and perturbed real-world conditions.
abstract: The paper addresses a limitation of vision-language-action models in contact-rich dexterous manipulation: they often struggle to reason about tactile contact and future physical interactions. UniTacVLA adds tactile sensing in a way that jointly models current tactile states and future contact changes, rather than using touch as a simple auxiliary signal. It learns a unified tactile latent space, uses tactile chain-of-thought reasoning with coarse-to-fine future tactile prediction, and then feeds this into a tactile-action mixed controller. In real-world tests on adjustment, insertion, wiping, and assembly tasks, the method improves success, accuracy, and robustness compared with existing approaches.
introduction: Vision-language-action models work well for many robotic manipulation tasks, but contact-rich dexterous manipulation remains challenging because physical interaction is hard to infer from vision alone. Existing vision-tactile-language-action methods incorporate tactile sensing, yet often treat touch as a passive signal instead of a structured source of semantic and dynamic information. UniTacVLA proposes a unified tactile learning framework that models tactile signals as interaction cues for both understanding the current contact state and predicting future contact changes. The method builds a tactile latent space, reasons over tactile sequences, predicts future tactile feedback at multiple levels of granularity, and uses both real-time and predicted tactile signals to refine action execution.
key_contributions: Introduces a unified tactile learning framework for contact-rich manipulation within VLA models.
Constructs a unified tactile latent space that represents both current tactile state and future contact changes.
Uses tactile chain-of-thought reasoning and coarse-to-fine future tactile prediction to build a state-aware and dynamics-aware tactile prior.
Proposes a tactile-action mixed controller that combines real-time and predicted tactile feedback to correct low-frequency action chunks with high-frequency adjustments.
Demonstrates improved success rate, manipulation accuracy, and contact robustness on four real-world task categories under clean and perturbed settings.
why_it_matters: This work is relevant for Physical AI because it tackles the core problem of robust dexterous contact, where accurate interaction modeling is essential. By making tactile sensing predictive rather than merely observational, it could enable more reliable manipulation in real-world environments with disturbances and changing contact conditions.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A[Vision-Language-Action Model] --> B[Tactile Input]
B --> C[Unified Tactile Latent]
C --> D[Coarse-to-Fine Tactile Reasoning]
D --> E[Future Contact Prediction]
E --> F[Tactile-Action Controller]
F --> G[Dexterous Task Execution]

title: A Self-Evolving Agentic System for Automated Generation and Execution of Biological Protocols
tldr: ProtoPilot is a self-evolving multi-agent system that converts biological protocols into executable lab automation code and iteratively revises workflows using wet-lab feedback, with strong benchmark and execution results.
abstract: This work addresses autonomous wet-lab experimentation, where it is not enough to generate plausible protocol text—the protocol, SOP, code, device constraints, and experimental feedback all need to stay aligned. The authors introduce ProtoPilot, a multi-agent system, along with a benchmark and evaluation framework built from synthetic- and molecular-biology tasks derived from gold-standard protocols. ProtoPilot generates protocols, expands SOPs, produces SDK-compliant code, and updates its workflow using wet-lab feedback. In evaluation and wet-lab tests, it achieved high expert preference and execution pass rates and produced validated experimental outputs.
introduction: The paper frames biological automation as a conversion problem: transforming experimental intent into protocols, then into code that can actually run on lab devices, while preserving correctness at each stage. To study this, the authors built an expert-grounded benchmark spanning 294 tasks derived from 98 gold-standard protocols, with rubrics, device validity gates, and real experimental tests. They propose ProtoPilot, a self-evolving multi-agent system that uses layer-wise verifiability, orchestration across agents, and a runtime-updated skill library. The system is designed to generate protocols, expand SOPs, synthesize SDK-compliant code, and revise workflows based on wet-lab feedback.
key_contributions: Introduced ProtoPilot, a self-evolving multi-agent system for automated generation and execution of biological protocols.
Built an expert-grounded evaluation framework with 294 synthetic-biology and molecular-biology tasks derived from 98 gold-standard protocols.
Added layer-wise verifiability, multi-agent orchestration, and a runtime-updated skill library to support protocol-to-code conversion and revision.
Reported strong benchmark results, including a 90.2% Top@3 expert-preference rate, 89.5% protocol-to-code gate pass rate, and 88.24% Opentrons pass rate.
Demonstrated wet-lab validation with interpretable readouts, Sanger-confirmed products, and feedback-corrected PCA-assembled DNA targets.
why_it_matters: For Physical AI and lab automation, the key challenge is reliable translation from intent to executable action under real device constraints. This work is relevant because it combines multi-agent planning, verification, and feedback-driven adaptation in a concrete embodied-science setting.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
  A[Gold protocols] --> B[Benchmark tasks]
  B --> C[ProtoPilot agents]
  C --> D[Protocol + SOP]
  C --> E[SDK code]
  D --> F[Wet-lab exec]
  E --> F
  F --> G[Feedback]
  G --> C

title: Revisiting Parameter Redundancy in Vision-Language-Action Models: Insights from VLM-to-VLA Adaptation
tldr: This work shows that VLM-to-VLA adaptation has structured, module-specific parameter divergence, and uses that signal to prune 12%–30% of OpenVLA and π0.5 while preserving about 90% of performance without recovery fine-tuning.
abstract: Vision-language-action models are powerful for embodied intelligence, but their size makes them expensive to run and highly sensitive to pruning. The authors revisit what “redundant” means in this setting by studying how parameters change during adaptation from vision-language models to VLAs. They find structured divergence across modules and use controlled pruning, without any recovery training, to link those divergence patterns to actual functional importance. Based on this, they propose a multi-module joint pruning method that keeps most of the original performance while removing a meaningful fraction of parameters.
introduction: The paper addresses a key practical problem in deploying VLA models: their large parameter counts create computational burden, yet naive pruning often causes severe performance loss. Rather than assuming that lost performance can simply be recovered by fine-tuning or low-rank correction, the authors ask whether existing pruning methods are removing truly redundant weights or also discarding important ones. They analyze parameter evolution during VLM-to-VLA adaptation and observe structured, module-dependent divergence patterns. Using controlled, recovery-free pruning as a diagnostic, they connect these divergence signals to functional contributions and then design a pruning scheme that exploits modular heterogeneity across the model.
key_contributions: Quantifies parameter divergence during VLM-to-VLA adaptation and shows that it is spatially structured across different modules.
Introduces controlled pruning without any post-pruning fine-tuning as a diagnostic to test whether candidate parameters are genuinely redundant.
Establishes a causal link between adaptation-induced divergence and functional importance for VLA performance.
Proposes a multi-module joint pruning scheme that leverages observed modular heterogeneity.
On LIBERO, reduces OpenVLA and π0.5 parameter counts by 12%–30% while retaining roughly 90% of original performance without recovery; prior pruning criteria collapse under the same constraint.
why_it_matters: For Physical AI and robotic policy deployment, this suggests efficient VLA compression is possible without relying on expensive recovery training. It also reframes pruning as a way to probe model adaptation dynamics, not just as a compression heuristic.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A[VLM → VLA Adaptation] --> B[Measure Param Divergence]
B --> C[Module-wise Pattern]
C --> D[Controlled Pruning]
D --> E[No Recovery Training]
E --> F[Assess Functional Importance]
F --> G[Multi-module Joint Pruning]
G --> H[Keep Perf, Drop Params]

title: ELASTIC: Efficiently Learning to Adaptively Scale Test-Time Compute for Generative Control Policies
tldr: ELASTIC learns when to spend robot test-time compute on more denoising steps versus more parallel samples, improving success/latency tradeoffs for generative control policies.
abstract: The paper studies how to allocate test-time compute for generative control policies such as diffusion policies and vision-language-action models. Instead of using a fixed number of denoising steps or samples, ELASTIC learns a state-dependent schedule that decides, at each denoising iteration, how much sequential refinement and parallel exploration to use. The method is trained with reinforcement learning as a meta-policy that operates on top of a frozen pretrained robot policy, without needing the original training data. In simulation and on a real robot, it improves the success-compute tradeoff and reduces latency.
introduction: Generative control policies can improve robot control by using test-time compute to refine actions or sample multiple candidates, but the best way to spend that compute depends on the current state and task. ELASTIC frames this as a meta-Markov Decision Process, where a learned meta-policy observes the control process and adaptively chooses both sequential denoising steps and the number of parallel samples during inference.
