This lets the system trade off exploration and refinement dynamically, rather than relying on fixed scaling rules. The approach is evaluated on simulated manipulation benchmarks with diffusion policies and on real-world manipulation with the π0.5 vision-language-action model.
key_contributions: Introduces ELASTIC, a method for learning state-dependent test-time compute schedules for generative control policies.
Formulates compute allocation as a meta-Markov Decision Process over sequential and parallel scaling decisions.
Uses reinforcement learning to train a meta-policy on top of a frozen pretrained robot policy, without access to the policy’s training data.
Shows in simulation that ELASTIC Pareto-dominates fixed and single-axis compute-scaling baselines at matched compute budgets.
Demonstrates on real-world robot manipulation that ELASTIC matches best-of-10 success while reducing wall-clock latency by 34%.
why_it_matters: For Physical AI, this provides a practical way to make robot policies faster and more reliable by adapting inference compute to the current state. It is especially relevant for deployment, where latency, success rate, and compute budget must be balanced online.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A[State input] --> B[Frozen robot policy]
B --> C[Meta-policy scheduler]
C --> D[Choose compute per step]
D --> E[Sequential refinement]
D --> F[Parallel exploration]
E --> G[Action output]
F --> G
C --> H[RL training on top]
H --> C

title: Autonomous UAV Navigation for Individual Wildlife Re-Identification
tldr: An autonomous UAV system uses real-time detection and pose-aware flight control to capture re-ID-ready wildlife images, rather than just passive aerial footage.
abstract: This work proposes a drone navigation pipeline for wildlife individual re-identification (re-ID), a task that supports population monitoring and conservation but is usually done with labor-intensive manual data collection. The system combines YOLOv11 for animal detection with a DINOv2-based pose classifier so the drone can orient toward the animal’s lateral flank and approach until the subject is large enough in frame for downstream re-ID. The authors demonstrate feasibility on zebra footage from Kenya and argue the approach transfers to other patterned species such as giraffes, tigers, and elephants.
introduction: The paper addresses a gap between aerial wildlife sensing and the image-quality needs of individual re-identification models. Instead of optimizing drone behavior for generic observation or group-level video capture, the proposed system actively steers the UAV to obtain views that are useful for identifying individual animals from surface patterns. The navigation policy uses object detection to find animals, pose classification to determine whether the lateral flank is visible, and a bounding-box threshold to decide when to stop approaching. The result is a task-aware embodied AI framework where downstream ecological analysis directly shapes real-time perception and control.
key_contributions: Introduces an autonomous UAV navigation framework aimed specifically at collecting images suitable for individual wildlife re-identification.
Combines YOLOv11 object detection with a DINOv2-based pose classifier to guide real-time flight decisions.
Uses flight control objectives tailored to re-ID image requirements, including lateral-flank orientation and a minimum bounding-box size threshold.
Demonstrates feasibility on zebra footage collected in Kenya.
Reports that the approach generalizes conceptually to other species with diagnostic surface patterns, including giraffes, tigers, and elephants.
why_it_matters: This is a concrete example of Physical AI for scientific data collection: the robot does not just sense the world, it actively acquires the specific views needed by a downstream model. For conservation and ecology, such task-aware autonomy could reduce manual effort and improve the scalability of wildlife monitoring.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A[UAV Camera] --> B[YOLOv11 Detect Animal]
B --> C[DINOv2 Pose Classify]
C --> D[Face Lateral Flank]
D --> E[Approach Close Enough]
E --> F[Large Re-ID View]
F --> G[Individual Re-ID]
G --> H[Population Monitoring]

title: TreeAgent: A Generalizable Multi-Agent Framework for Automated Bias Labeling in Forestry via Compiled Expert Rules and Vision-Language Models
tldr: TreeAgent uses a multi-agent orchestration of expert decision trees and vision-language models to automate forestry bias labeling with lower annotation cost and better performance than supervised baselines.
abstract: The paper addresses a common bottleneck in expert annotation: labels are slow, variable across annotators, and expensive to scale. It proposes a multi-agent framework that combines expert decision trees with vision-language models, using the tree as a structural guide and VLMs for localized decisions at each node. Multi-agent voting is used to reduce randomness in VLM outputs. The authors report that their framework generalizes across decision structures without modification and improves over supervised baselines on tree bias classification.
introduction: This work targets automated labeling in forestry remote sensing, specifically tree height bias classification, where expert annotation is a limiting factor. TreeAgent frames the expert decision tree as a declarative prior and delegates node-level semantic judgments to vision-language models. A multi-agent system then coordinates these judgments and votes to stabilize the final label. The authors formalize this as a Decoupled Declarative Decision (D3) framework, emphasizing zero-modification generalization across different expert-defined rules. The result is an interpretable labeling pipeline that aims to reproduce expert procedures with less manual effort.
key_contributions: Introduces a multi-agent framework that combines expert decision trees with vision-language models for automated bias labeling.
Formalizes the Decoupled Declarative Decision (D3) framework for zero-modification generalization across diverse decision structures.
Uses multi-agent voting to mitigate VLM stochasticity during node-level decision making.
Demonstrates improved performance over supervised ML baselines on a tree bias classification testbed.
Shows reduced expert labeling effort while preserving interpretability of the labeling procedure.
why_it_matters: For Physical AI and Vision AI, this suggests a practical way to scale expert-style labeling in domains where ground truth is expensive and rule-based. The approach is especially relevant for workflows that need both interpretability and reduced annotation cost.
mermaid:
flowchart TD
A[Expert rules] --> B[Decision tree]
B --> C[TreeAgent agents]
C --> D[VLM local checks]
D --> E[Multi-agent voting]
E --> F[Bias label]
B --> F
